153532.body:
This proposal seems more complex than necessary, I'd suggest something like:
```py
@dataclasses.dataclass
class MultiplePassesCombinedIntoOne:
    passes: list[Callable[[torch.fx.Graph], None]]

    def __call__(self, gm: torch.fx.GraphModule) -> None:
        for p in self.passes:
           p(gm)
    
    def append(self, p): ...
    def prepend(self, p): ...
```

Then you can do:
```py
@torch.compile(options={"joint_custom_pre_pass": MultiplePassesCombinedIntoOne([pass1, pass2]))
```

You then could implement your entire proposal out of tree.
```py
passes = {
	"post_grad_custom_pre_pass": MultiplePassesCombinedIntoOne([]),
	"post_grad_custom_post_pass": MultiplePassesCombinedIntoOne([]),
	"joint_custom_pre_pass": MultiplePassesCombinedIntoOne([]),
	"joint_custom_post_pass": MultiplePassesCombinedIntoOne([]),
	"pre_grad_custom_pass": MultiplePassesCombinedIntoOne([]),
}
...
passes["post_grad_custom_post_pass"].append(...)
passes["joint_custom_pre_pass"].prepend(...)
...
@torch.compile(options=passes)
```

The nice thing about using a config (rather than globals), is two extensions to `torch.compile` can both co-exist without impacting each-other, since the changes can be isolated to a specific call site.  In general, I don't think you want to pool all passes together.  You might be compiling some stuff for CPU with set of passes A, then other stuff for GPU with a set of passes B.  I think it would be bad for the passes from A to leak into B.

We could add some of the pass manager utilities to PyTorch, to make it easier to do this.  But I think they should still go through the existing config mechanism.

@jansel, your proposal will work fine for use cases, like vLLM, where the `torch.compile` is used. I like the idea to limit the custom FX passes to the context of single `torch.compile`.

However, there may be external software components that do not implement models or utilize `torch.compile`, but still require the ability to register custom passes. For instance, Inductor provides an interface for device backend registration via `register_backend_for_device` (see [link](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/v2.7.0/torch/_inductor/codegen/common.py#L319)), allowing external components to register their own backends for lowering loop-level IR to specific devices. Such backends may also need to register custom passes to optimize the FX graph accordingly. In these cases, it would be necessary to register custom passes during the initialization of the external software, ensuring they are applied to all subsequent graph compilations.

Additionally, it is important to consider that there may be multiple external software components, each independently registering custom passes, without awareness of one another.

How would you envision to address such use cases?

I would like also to make comment about the following:
> You might be compiling some stuff for CPU with set of passes A, then other stuff for GPU with a set of passes B. I think it would be bad for the passes from A to leak into B.

Please note that FX graph may contain FX nodes, executed on multiple devices (e.g. CPU and GPU), and then custom passes for both devices have to be applied.

For custom backends, I think it would be reasonable for them to expose custom passes.  This should be part of the existing backend registration system and the passes should be gated on the device.

I think the semantics of globally adding passes not tied to any backend is confusing for users.

If you have something like:
```py
torch.compile(...)  # behavior A happens
import some_module_that_adds_passes
torch.compile(...)  # behavior B happens
```

This seems bad.  If the passes are buggy people will open bug reports against PyTorch rather than `some_module_that_adds_passes`, and people may not even know that the import had the side effect of modifying `torch.compile`.

I'd rather have `some_module_that_adds_passes` expose a wrapper around `torch.compile` that sets the right `options=` flags locally.  So users only get the modified behavior if they opt into it.

Related: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/151876

I would also lean towards a simpler, list based API with append/prepend.

I also prefer to simply the idea to make it simple enough. n particular the order idea, I think it is not clear about the scenarios.

- How do custom backends determine the priority of its custom passes? What's the guideline or rule?
- What's the behavior of different custom passes of different backends with the same priority? Although, the custom backend may always intend make its custom passes to be the top priority?
- The different order may impact performance as different passes may conflict with each other. Does users/custom backends need to tune the order?
@jansel Do you think that we can just extend the current `register_backend_for_device` function, to take the additional argument of custom FX pass to register?
Either that, or we could also add some abstract methods to the class backends subclass.

I would prefer to extend the `register_backend_for_device` function, since the custom FX pass appears to be a complementary feature to the `BaseScheduling` (operating on scheduler nodes) and wrapper codegen (generating the code) classes. I will prepare PR with the changes and we may continue reviewing that in more detail.

If a valid overload exists, the error message becomes unnecessary. Therefore, I'm considering deferring the Tensor representation (i.e., `__repr__`) until no valid overload can be found.

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/7482eb217c621749dc11413ca1ae114690a09c55/torch/csrc/jit/python/pybind_utils.cpp#L797-L806

cc @albanD
From a closer look at this, this is actually expected. Do you have any use case where you need to call the packet and not a specific overload?

I'm unsure whether `torch.ops.aten` is intended to be an official or user-facing way to develop PyTorch programs. If it is exposed as a public programming interface, I'm concerned that users may not be aware of the differences between `torch.ops.aten` and standard torch APIs, which could lead to unintended performance overhead or misuse.

Ex1:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/ffd49d538e8fcd274555df018ea2ba2ad8523df8/torch/masked/maskedtensor/_ops_refs.py#L147
```bash
>>> torch.ops.aten.where.overloads()
['self', 'ScalarOther', 'ScalarSelf', 'Scalar', 'default', 'self_out']
```

Ex2:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/ffd49d538e8fcd274555df018ea2ba2ad8523df8/torch/_inductor/fx_passes/misc_patterns.py#L56
```bash
>>> torch.ops.aten.index.overloads()
['Tensor', 'Tensor_out', 'Tensor_hacked_twin', 'str', 'list_int', 'list_float', 'list_bool', 'list_Tensor', 'list_str']
```

torch.ops.aten is "public" in the sense that it is exposed to the world and you will commonly encounter it when working with torch.compile/torch.export. We expect that most end users will not use torch.ops.aten but will instead use the corresponding torch.* apis, because those are well documented.

If you want to make this faster we can discuss how to make it faster. In torch.compile/torch.export we work around it mostly by avoiding OpOverloadPacket.

> We expect that most end users will not use torch.ops.aten but will instead use the corresponding torch.* apis, because those are well documented.

It makes sense as torch.* apis are well documented. Since we always encourage users to use torch.* rather than torch.ops.aten.*, the exposure of this issue is low. We can close this issue and reopen it if users experience the issue in the future. @zou3519 , @albanD , are you okay with that?

153697.body:
Perhaps part of the answer lies in the file torch/_inductor/runtime/triton_heuristics.py. This specific code snippet:

```Python
        target = GPUTarget(
            compile_meta["device_type"],
            arch,  # <-- This 'arch' variable
            cc_warp_size(compile_meta["cc"]),
        )

        compile_kwargs = {
            "target": target,
            "options": options,
        }
        
        try:
            binary = triton.compile(*compile_args, **compile_kwargs)
        # ... (rest of the try-except block) ...
```

It explicitly specifies the arch variable here. This might be the reason why artifacts compiled on an SM_86 GPU (A10) cannot run on an SM_90 GPU (H20).

Although I believe this behavior might not be ideal/reasonable, because in the context of large-scale AOTInductor applications, the process of cross-compilation followed by distribution is almost inevitable. Targeting a specific arch so strictly during compilation on one machine seems to prevent this desired flexibility.
@desertfire Can you share your thoughts on this?

AOTInductor is designed to do native compilation, not cross compilation. But I do see this is a reasonable ask for crossing different Nvidia GPU generations.

I can work on adding a new option, to emit .ptx or .fatbin files for the kernels.  @sujuyu , do you have a preference on which format you prefer?

> AOTInductor is designed to do native compilation, not cross compilation. But I do see this is a reasonable ask for crossing different Nvidia GPU generations.
> 
> I can work on adding a new option, to emit .ptx or .fatbin files for the kernels. [@sujuyu](https://github.com/sujuyu) , do you have a preference on which format you prefer?

Thank you for your reply. I personally think that .ptx files are more reasonable. On the one hand, they are more in line with the "native" intuition and take up less space. On the other hand, they are more readable. For experienced engineers, they may help with debugging. For example, when there is an diff in the calculation, try to find the source of the diff.

153701.body:
cc @anijain2305 @StrongerXi as we've had this discussion before, esp. around weakrefs.

`del` semantics will be difficult to fully model since we would have to model Python memory management. In particular, it can be difficult to determine when an object should be deleted/gc'd, so we only provide the promise that 1) intermediate compiled values will not leak and 2) if the compiled object is deleted, we will not leak any references.

Workaround here would be to fix the graph break or to explicitly clear the memory of the affected variable.
I'll take a look, I think we can fix this particular case, i.e., faithfully replay `del` as a side effect on pre-existing object.

Ugh it actually boils down to #83137, basically graph break introduces a function call (to some `resume_at_...` function), and we pass all the live locals into the resume function as individual arguments, which increments each one of their refcount, so even if we generate a `del` in the resume function (which was my original attempt to fix this), the caller still owns a reference to these arguments, preventing them from being freed.

We basically want `func(std::move(arg), ...)`, but we don't have that in python, so like #83137 and #122353, one way is to pass them inside a list to avoid the aforementioned refcount bump.

The input to compiled graph is also not boxed, meaning if we have any computation on `x_int` before `del x_int`, compiled code lose the opportunity to reduce peak memory usage:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/984b1a80e3fc4c9829ca22382fe9391f6f2e6354/torch/_dynamo/codegen.py#L631

Chatted with @IvanKobzarev offline and discussed why `list.clear()` still doesn't fix the divergence in compile. Here's what I found:

1. Note that in the `x_int.clear()` example, `x_int[0]` got freed correctly, so that's actually working (although `x_int` is probably a trivial example because in practice I'd imagine it get used in some other tensor op before `x_int.clear()`, and that actually will cause issue, see (2) below).
2. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/153701#issuecomment-2941907485 is the root cause for `x[0]` not freeing with `x.clear()` under compile. Basically we need to "box and unbox" arguments when calling the compiled subgraph:

```python
graph_args = [arg0, arg1, ...]
del arg0, arg1, ...
compiled_graph(graph_args)

def compiled_graph(graph_args):
    arg0, arg1, ... = graph_args
    graph_args.clear()
    ... # optimized code
```

This can be tricky, because `arg0, ...` usually comes from inputs of the compiled function, and in cases where we'd want to free them early, they must've come in boxed form, e.g., from a list input to the compiled function, and there will be an unpack and `list.clear()` early in the code. However, when Dynamo codegen, the side effects (e.g., the `list.clear()`) won't happen until after the call to `compiled_graph`, so we have to modify the codegen logic a bit, I'll look into it. 
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/984b1a80e3fc4c9829ca22382fe9391f6f2e6354/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py#L1407-L1422

To clarify the `list.clear()` issue above, assuming https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/153701#issuecomment-2942129755 gets fixed, the following demonstrates the problem and proposed fix:

```python
import torch


@torch.compile(backend="eager")
def fn(x):
    # Some compute
    t1 = x + 1

    # Some graph break
    torch._dynamo.graph_break()

    # More compute
    t2 = t1 * 2 # `t1` still used after graph break, before deletion
    del t1
    return t2 + 2


def fn_compiled(x):
    t1 = compiled_graph1(x) # x + 1
    args = [t1]
    del t1
    return resume1(args)

def resume1(args):
    # prologue
    t1, = args
    args.clear()

    # `fn` after graph break
    t2 = t1 * 2
    del t1
    return t2 + 2

# Existing codegen
def resume1_compiled(args):
    t1, = args # PROBLEM: lifetime of `t1` spans this entire function...
    res = compiled_graph2(t1) # `t1 * 2` and `t2 + 2`

    args[:] = [] # Dynamo generated bytecode to emulate `args.clear()`
    return res

# Proposed codegen
def resume1_compiled(args):
    # This is the tricky part, unclear what a general solution would look like
    t1, = args
    args[:] = []

    graph_args = [t1]
    del t1
    res = compiled_graph2(graph_args) # does graph_args.clear(), etc.

    return res
```

153852.body: Which version of PyTorch are you using? I can't reproduce it locally.

Oh, sorry. The tensor must be created inside the compiled region. I'll update the reproducer

> Which version of PyTorch are you using? I can't reproduce it locally.

I can reproduce this on PyTorch main branch.

This issue is in the tracer of dynamo. there is an inconsistent assignment for the sign of 0 of the real part of the complex tensor. 

This recreates OP's issue. 
```python
from torch.fx import Graph, GraphModule
# Define the constant complex tensor exactly as in your forward
const_x = torch.tensor([(-0 - 1e-28j)], dtype=torch.cfloat)

graph = Graph()
# Retrieve the constant by name
x_node = graph.get_attr("const_x")
# Call the cosine method on it
cos_node = graph.call_method("cos", args=(x_node,))
# Return the result as a tuple to mirror your forward's (cos,)
graph.output((cos_node,))

# Build the GraphModule with the constant embedded
gm = GraphModule({"const_x": const_x}, graph, "CosineConstModule")
print("gm() =", gm())
```

This does not have OP's issue
```python
from torch.fx import Graph, GraphModule
# Define the constant complex tensor exactly as in your forward
const_x = torch.tensor([(-1e-28j)], dtype=torch.cfloat)

graph = Graph()
# Retrieve the constant by name
x_node = graph.get_attr("const_x")
# Call the cosine method on it
cos_node = graph.call_method("cos", args=(x_node,))
# Return the result as a tuple to mirror your forward's (cos,)
graph.output((cos_node,))

# Build the GraphModule with the constant embedded
gm = GraphModule({"const_x": const_x}, graph, "CosineConstModule")
print("gm() =", gm())
```

The key difference is that torch.tensor([(-1e-28j)], dtype=torch.cfloat) is traced as  torch.tensor([(-0-1e-28j)], dtype=torch.cfloat) in dynamo, which returns tensor([0.-1.0000e-28j]).  However as pointed out in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/158743, there is no negative integral 0 defined by the python parser, so it gets "converted" to a positive 0.

torch.compile can cause numerical differences from Eager. We recommend to use torch._dynamo.utils.same (https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/6c2c527cd671f5c825876cb16b650c2da8cfc32b/torch/_dynamo/utils.py#L2727) for result comparison and use fp64 result as the common baseline.

For this particular issue, setting `TORCHINDUCTOR_EMULATE_PRECISION_CASTS=1` will solve the problem, see
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/141436#issuecomment-2756030982

154025.body:
Some current limitations of TMA descriptors in comparison to block ptrs: https://github.com/triton-lang/triton/blob/c32d6963c9dfed9810f8e0e15e728637fe14ecdb/python/triton/language/semantic.py#L1960
- The tensors innermost stride must be 1
- The size of the last dimension of the block should result in atleast 16 bytes of data being loaded. So the block sizes produced in triton heuristics need to match that constraint.
@blaine-rister @jansel This is a PR that adds an experimental `use_tma_api` option to generate tma descriptors: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/157906
Also, Triton 3.4.0 got released a few days ago (which deleted block ptrs), so does it mean that PyTorch with `config.triton.use_block_ptr=True` is not compatible with Triton 3.4.0, right?

> Also, Triton 3.4.0 got released a few days ago (which deleted block ptrs), so does it mean that PyTorch with `config.triton.use_block_ptr=True` is not compatible with Triton 3.4.0, right?

Are you sure block pointers have been removed?   I still see them in: https://github.com/triton-lang/triton/blob/f824a5e053ba3dd29deecf87eb7d25a603c43fec/python/triton/language/core.py#L2217

> > Also, Triton 3.4.0 got released a few days ago (which deleted block ptrs), so does it mean that PyTorch with `config.triton.use_block_ptr=True` is not compatible with Triton 3.4.0, right?
> 
> Are you sure block pointers have been removed? I still see them in: https://github.com/triton-lang/triton/blob/f824a5e053ba3dd29deecf87eb7d25a603c43fec/python/triton/language/core.py#L2217

The `boundary_check` and `padding_option` params have been removed from `tl.load` and `tl.store`, see https://github.com/triton-lang/triton/pull/6848

Do they now match the semantics of tensor descriptor? (check all boundaries and zero pad?)

In that case we should be able to remove the boundary_check/padding_option from the generated code.

154111.body: Can be reproduced locally with CUDA. Not CPU specific. Error stack points to AOT Dispatcher.
@azahed98 this will be a good issue to look into as part of the dynamic shape + DTensor ramp-up

I tracked this down to tracing of ATen addmm, and in fact the core issue happens with vanilla tensors as well, but the tensor subclass handler specifically exposes the issue.

```
x = torch.ones(2, 1)
y = torch.ones(1, 1)

def my_matmul(a, b):
    return a @ b

jitted_fn = torch.compile(my_matmul, dynamic=True)
result2 = jitted_fn(x, y)
```

Set a breakpoint [here at `torch/_decomp/decompositions.py` in `matmul`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/392fa75411a1f293e891395f005615b257c03eda/torch/_decomp/decompositions.py#L4430) (and continuing a couple times first) shows that the argument size is modified to replace the symint

```
> /data/users/arshzahed/pytorch/torch/_decomp/decompositions.py(4432)matmul()
-> torch.distributed.breakpoint()
(Pdb) args
tensor1 = FunctionalTensor(_to_functional_tensor(FakeTensor(..., size=(s77, 1))))
tensor2 = FunctionalTensor(_to_functional_tensor(FakeTensor(..., size=(1, 1))))
is_out = False
(Pdb) n
> /data/users/arshzahed/pytorch/torch/_decomp/decompositions.py(4433)matmul()
-> return torch.mm(tensor1, tensor2)
(Pdb) n
--Return--
> /data/users/arshzahed/pytorch/torch/_decomp/decompositions.py(4433)matmul()->FunctionalTen...size=(2, 1))))
-> return torch.mm(tensor1, tensor2)
(Pdb) args
tensor1 = FunctionalTensor(_to_functional_tensor(FakeTensor(..., size=(2, 1))))
tensor2 = FunctionalTensor(_to_functional_tensor(FakeTensor(..., size=(1, 1))))
is_out = False
```

The result is the compiled fn does not include the dynamic shape info in the return. This does not happen for the cases mentioned in the original post that don't raise the error with DTensor.

After further investigation, I found that this is stemming from the [inductor decomposition for aten.mm](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/392fa75411a1f293e891395f005615b257c03eda/torch/_inductor/decomposition.py#L367).

Specifically, there is a guard around the number of elements, but for total elements <= 32 the result involves iteration through self `[self[i, :] * input2 for i in range(self.size(0))]`. This loop is what concertizes the shape, leading to the downstream error.

Discussed with @BoyuanFeng and found a potential fix with `self * input2`. Testing and will follow up!

154150.body: Inductor may fuse computation from multiple ops which can cause minor numerical differences, so I don't think this is a valid test.

@desertfire I can't see how fusing ops would cause this to be nonzero.

Consider the following:

```python

import os

os.environ["CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG"] = ":4096:8"
import torch as th
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

th.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False
th.use_deterministic_algorithms(True)
th.random.manual_seed(0)
th._dynamo.config.cache_size_limit = 1000
B = 10000

device = "cuda"
x = th.randn((B,), device=device)


def f(x):
    return F.mse_loss(F.gelu(x), F.gelu(x), reduction="none").max()


print(th.compile(f, fullgraph=True)(x))

```
The kernel generated (one out of two):

```
@triton.jit
def triton_red_fused_gelu_max_mse_loss_0(in_ptr0, out_ptr0, xnumel, r0_numel, XBLOCK : tl.constexpr, R0_BLOCK : tl.constexpr):
    xnumel = 2
    r0_numel = 5000
    rnumel = r0_numel
    RBLOCK: tl.constexpr = R0_BLOCK
    xoffset = tl.program_id(0) * XBLOCK
    xindex = xoffset + tl.arange(0, XBLOCK)[:, None]
    xmask = xindex < xnumel
    r0_base = tl.arange(0, R0_BLOCK)[None, :]
    rbase = r0_base
    x0 = xindex
    _tmp12 = tl.full([XBLOCK, R0_BLOCK], float("-inf"), tl.float32)
    for r0_offset in range(0, r0_numel, R0_BLOCK):
        r0_index = r0_offset + r0_base
        r0_mask = r0_index < r0_numel
        roffset = r0_offset
        rindex = r0_index
        r0_1 = r0_index
        tmp0 = tl.load(in_ptr0 + (r0_1 + 5000*x0), xmask & r0_mask, eviction_policy='evict_first', other=0.0)
        tmp1 = 0.5
        tmp2 = tmp0 * tmp1
        tmp3 = 0.7071067811865476
        tmp4 = tmp0 * tmp3
        tmp5 = libdevice.erf(tmp4)
        tmp6 = 1.0
        tmp7 = tmp5 + tmp6
        tmp8 = tmp2 * tmp7
        tmp9 = tmp8 - tmp8
        tmp10 = tmp9 * tmp9
        tmp11 = tl.broadcast_to(tmp10, [XBLOCK, R0_BLOCK])
        tmp13 = triton_helpers.maximum(_tmp12, tmp11)
        _tmp12 = tl.where(r0_mask & xmask, tmp13, _tmp12)
    tmp12 = triton_helpers.max2(_tmp12, 1)[:, None]
    tl.store(out_ptr0 + (x0), tmp12, xmask)
```

surely `tmp9 = tmp8 - t

Simplifying the example leads to the following:

```python
import triton
import triton.language as tl
import torch
@triton.jit
def kernel(in_ptr0, out_ptr0, XBLOCK : tl.constexpr):
    xoffset = tl.program_id(0) * XBLOCK
    tmp0 = tl.load(in_ptr0 + (0))
    tmp1 = tl.broadcast_to(tmp0, [XBLOCK])
    tmp14 = tmp1 * tmp1
    tmp15 = tmp14 - tmp14
    tl.store(out_ptr0 + (tl.full([XBLOCK], 0, tl.int32)), tmp15, None)

x = torch.randn((1,)).cuda()
y = torch.empty((1,)).cuda()
kernel[lambda meta: (1, )](x, y, 1)
print(y.item())
```

The relevant part of the ptx is:
```
mov.b32 	%f1, %r1;
.loc	1 9 19                        
mul.f32 	%f2, %f1, %f1;
.loc	1 10 20                        
neg.f32 	%f3, %f2;
fma.rn.f32 	%f4, %f1, %f1, %f3;
```

it's doing an FMA(x, x, -x * x). I suppose it's not a bug, but this is still not ideal.

154195.body:
Would you like to provide a repro script that contains `rms_norm`?

Also the default Dynamo backend like "aot_eager" and "inductor" don't go through `aot_export_joint_simple`, instead they go through `aot_module_simplified`:

When `torch.compile(..., backend="aot_eager")` is used, here's the flow:

  1. `aot_eager` backend (torch/_dynamo/backends/debugging.py:175-188):
```python
  def aot_eager(gm, fake_tensor_inputs, fw_compiler=None, bw_compiler=None, **kwargs):
      return aot_autograd(
          fw_compiler=fw_compiler or boxed_nop,
          bw_compiler=bw_compiler or boxed_nop,
          partition_fn=min_cut_rematerialization_partition,
          keep_inference_input_mutations=True,
      )(gm, fake_tensor_inputs, **kwargs)
```
  2. aot_autograd (torch/_dynamo/backends/common.py:116-117) returns an AotAutograd instance
  3. AotAutograd.call (torch/_dynamo/backends/common.py:106) calls:
  `cg = aot_module_simplified(gm, example_inputs, **self.kwargs)`


When `torch.compile(..., backend="inductor")` is used, here's the flow:

  1. `inductor` backend (torch/_dynamo/backends/inductor.py:20-25) calls compile_fx
  2. compile_fx (torch/_inductor/compile_fx.py:2324) calls aot_autograd:
```python
  return aot_autograd(
      fw_compiler=fw_compiler,
      bw_compiler=bw_compiler,
      inference_compiler=inference_compiler,
      decompositions=decompositions,
      partition_fn=partition_fn,
      keep_inference_input_mutations=True,
      cudagraphs=cudagraphs,
      boxed_forward_device_index=forward_device,
      ignore_shape_env=ignore_shape_env,
  )(model_, example_inputs_)
```
  3. aot_autograd returns an AotAutograd instance (from torch/_dynamo/backends/common.py)
  4. AotAutograd.call calls aot_module_simplified (line 106 in common.py)

If you are trying to implement a backend similar to Inductor, you might want to look into how the "inductor" backend is currently integrated.

Design:

Test case:
```py


import triton  # @manual
import triton.language as tl  # @manual
 
@triton.jit
def sin_kernel(x_ptr, y_ptr, n_elements, BLOCK_SIZE: "tl.constexpr"):
    pid = tl.program_id(axis=0)
    offsets = pid * BLOCK_SIZE + tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE)
    mask = offsets < n_elements
    x = tl.load(x_ptr + offsets, mask=mask)
    tl.store(y_ptr + offsets, tl.sin(x), mask=mask)
 
wrapped = torch.library.wrap_triton(sin_kernel)
 
def fn(x):
out = torch.empty_like(x)
n_elements = x.numel()
grid = lambda meta: (triton.cdiv(n_elements, meta["BLOCK_SIZE"]),)
wrapped[grid](x, out, n_elements, BLOCK_SIZE=128)
return out
 
compiled = torch.compile(fn, fullgraph=True, backend="eager")
x = torch.randn(1024, device=GPU_TYPE)
result = compiled(x)
self.assertEqual(result, torch.sin(x))
```

154467.body:
Copied from issue. tldr I think its an existing bug.

Let me clarify:

Question 1: How does we currently share the same kernel?
We rely on a few things:
1. Choice caller of different swizzle has the same hash key 
2. So during precompilation, only one will be compiled since they share the same hash key 
3. During autotuning, all of them will be run if **that hash key is not found** 

In other words, even before my change, there is a bug. Say you run an mm with swizzle = 1. Then you run an mm with swizzle = 2. The second run will read the cache from swizzle = 1, see the same cache key, and return the wrong timings.

Just to illustrate:
```
AUTOTUNE mm(147456x6144, 6144x2048)
strides: [6144, 1], [2048, 1]
dtypes: torch.bfloat16, torch.bfloat16
  cutlass_00fc8ad8 8.3515 ms 100.0% cutlass3x_sm90_tensorop_s64x128x16gemm_bf16_bf16_f32_void_bf16_128x128x64_1x1x1_0_ttn_align8_cpasync_warpspecialized_cooperative_epi_nosmem swizzle=1
  cutlass_8ce566a2 8.6482 ms 96.6% cutlass3x_sm90_tensorop_s64x128x16gemm_bf16_bf16_f32_void_bf16_128x128x64_1x1x1_0_ttn_align8_stream_k_cpasync_warpspecialized_cooperative_epi_nosmem swizzle=1
  cutlass_500ad94c 16.0794 ms 51.9% cutlass3x_sm90_tensorop_s64x128x16gemm_bf16_bf16_f32_void_bf16_128x128x64_1x1x1_0_ttn_align8_cpasync_warpspecialized_epi_nosmem swizzle=1
SingleProcess AUTOTUNE benchmarking takes 0.0003 seconds and 0.0027 seconds precompiling for 3 choices
```

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2222c695-8bd6-46c9-a6d5-7ab10352491a)

Another option is to determine if there is a method to avoid recompilation if I must have an int value in the model.forward, the int value changes in every epoch of generation.

Hi @anijain2305   You can run the following script to reproduce the recompilation issue without any third libs except transformers

TORCH_LOGS="+graph_breaks,+recompiles" python script.py
```python
import os
import torch
from transformers import AutoConfig
from torch.library import Library

lib = Library("my_ops", "DEF")
lib.define("multi_input_op(int a, Tensor b) -> Tensor")


config = AutoConfig.from_pretrained("Felladrin/Llama-68M-Chat-v1")


@torch.library.impl("my_ops::multi_input_op", "Meta")
def multi_input_op_meta(a, b):
    return b.new_empty(b.shape)  # return a dummy tensor with correct shape

# Create the impl for the op
@torch.library.impl("my_ops::multi_input_op", "CPU")
def my_op_impl(a, b):
    return b

def forward(input_data, input_tensor) -> int:
    """
    A simple forward function that returns the input value.
    """
    int_data = input_data.input_int
    input_data = torch.ops.my_ops.multi_input_op(int_data, input_tensor)
    return input_data + input_tensor

torch._inductor.config.cpp_wrapper = False
os.environ["TORCHINDUCTOR_FREEZING"] = "1"
forward = torch.compile(forward)

input_tensor = torch.Tensor([1])
input_data = config
for i in range(10):
    setattr(input_data, "input_int", i)
    output = forward(input_data=input_data, input_tensor=input_tensor)
    print(f"Input: {input_data.input_int}, Output: {output}")
```

Before your commit, there is no recompilation
After your commit:
```
...
a, 'input_int') == 6  # input_data = torch.ops.my_ops.multi_input_op(int_data, input_tensor)  # test_compile_2.py:27 in forwa[0/1831]
.py:1178 in __call__)                                                                                                                V0529 09:10:29.345000 2537328 pytorch/torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3110] [0/8] [__recompiles]     - 0/5: object.__getattribute__(input_dat
a, 'input_int') == 5  # input_data = torch.ops.my_ops.multi_input_op(int_data, input_tensor)  # test_compile_2.py:27 in forward (_ops
.py:1178 in __call__)

So there should be one recompilation because the first time the graph is specialized for input_int to be 0. But then it is marked dynamic, and Dynamo creates a symint for it. All goes well, but then dynamic shape infra decides to bind the symint to the concrete value at `torch/_ops..py:1178 in __call__` I am out of depth here why there is a specialization here. Tagging @bobrenjc93 and @zou3519 (because this is specific to custom ops) Marking high prio because this is an important repo from HF.

The problem is the schema is `lib.define("multi_input_op(int a, Tensor b) -> Tensor")` and needs to be rewritten to `lib.define("multi_input_op(SymInt a, Tensor b) -> Tensor")`

"int" means we will specialize on the integer, "SymInt" means we will not.

I would recommend using torch.library.custom_op to create and manipulate custom operators instead, it covers this footgun and others.

This is user error, but I wonder if there's anything we can do to provide a better error message. @bobrenjc93 Are there recompile reasons in TORCH_LOGS=recompiles or tlparse, and can we thread through "schema uses int" through it?
We could add some custom logic to look at the stacktrace during specialization. I filed an issue here: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/154851
The issue is fixed by changing `int` to `SymInt`. Thanks!

cc @oulgen @eellison @jamesjwu for code cache related failure

@anijain2305 Just FYI the error is coming from PyCodeCache: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/cd3d2b75b302858d1935148a421b27400c12569d/torch/_inductor/codecache.py#L3263

This is the in-memory cache, not the slew of caches we have built. Still if it works when caches are turned off, might be related.
cc @laithsakka, as I think this touches the in memory pycodecache

I debugged this for a while and figured out a few issues that are all interacting together, though I'm still not 100% following all the logic. The core of the problem is that `PyCodeCache.stack_frames_for_code` does not handle empty linemaps properly. 

The reason it gets triggered by caching in this example, as far as I can tell, is the complex interaction of many moving parts. 

- We compile backward functions lazily usually. 
- The function in question has a graph break which calls sum().backward() in our code. 
- The backward function that inductor generates, in this case, has an empty linemap (i.e. `PyCodeCache.linemap["path_to_backward.py"] = []`)
- For some reason, on an AOTAutogradCacheHit on cpu tensors, when the graph break is exactly in this form, dynamo **retraces** through the inductor generated code, (which almost certainly shouldn't happen)!

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/155064 will fix the more immediate issue which unblocks the test case, but @anijain2305 is looking into the behavior of why dynamo is tracing through inductor generated code in this specific case.
I think #155251 is the full fix for stopping dynamo from tracing into any backward code loaded from cache

154554.body:
Note that `flex_attention` + NJT does not even run when using `torch.compile`. This could potentially be related?
See this inssue: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/154556

Thanks for the report @mauriceweiler! I'm actively looking into it. Note that these sorts of errors relate to symbolic shape handling within torch.compile and can be tricky to resolve, but hopefully I can get a fix up soon.

@mauriceweiler I'm looking into the torch.compile issue separately, but I did notice in your snippet above that there is no call to `create_nested_block_mask()`, which is intended to be used to get a BlockMask taking into account the input NJTs' ragged structures. With this, I can get all implementations to agree:
```python
def full_mask_mod(b, h, q_idx, kv_idx):
    return torch.ones_like(q_idx, dtype=torch.bool)

# This handles masking out inter-sequence attention
nested_block_mask = create_nested_block_mask(
    full_mask_mod, B, None, Q_njt, K_njt
)
attn_out_njt = flex_attention(Q_njt, K_njt, V_njt, block_mask=nested_block_mask)
```

The call from your snippet was:
```python
attn_out_njt = flex_attention(Q_njt, K_njt, V_njt)
```

which implicitly passes `block_mask=None` and allows unrestricted attention (including across sequences), just like for the non-NJT case. That said, I think an argument could be made that a more intuitive default behavior for NJT inputs specifically would be to disallow inter-sequence attention. cc @drisspg for opinions here

That resolves the issue, thanks! I was under the impression that flex_attention with NJTs would automatically take care of document masking, and `create_nested_block_mask` would only be necessary if additional masking (e.g. a causal mask) is desired.
Looking at the implementation made clear that `create_nested_block_mask` / `_nested_mod_func_adaptor` are essentially combining the passed `mask_mod` and something like document masking from the first flex attention blog post. To obtain pure document masking one needs to pass a `mask_mod` which always evaluates to True. I ended up using this trivial `mask_mod`, which is functionally equivalent to your's but broadcasts over the query index:
```python
def njt_mask_mod(b_idx, h_idx, q_idx, kv_idx):
    return torch.tensor(True, device=device, dtype=torch.bool)
```

It would be helpful if the docs would explain that flex_attention + NJT _always_ requires a `mask_mod` to produce valid (document masked) results. You could also think about `assert block_mask is not None` if `query.is_nested`is True to prevent others from stepping into the same trap.
@mauriceweiler Agreed completely, this is unclear and I think the assumption you made was reasonable. I'm chatting with @drisspg to determine the best way to ensure others don't hit this footgun.

154556.body:
Note that the code runs without `torch.compile`, but yields inconsistent results. This could potentially be related?
See this inssue: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/154554
Thanks for the minimal repro @mauriceweiler ! I'm looking into this now.

The symptom comes from the functionalization implementation for the flex_attention HOP:

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/b394c6e89c2f7986274e405ec8f91c12fa52b5e2/torch/_higher_order_ops/flex_attention.py#L378-L399

On the surface, it seems like `ctx.unwrap_tensors()` (with ctx being a `PythonFunctionalizeAPI` in this case) is not smart enough to handle tensor subclasses? Should it be handled there or more ad-hoc within flex_attention's impl? cc @zou3519 / @bdhirsh / @drisspg

After addressing this, I run into a problem in `trace_flex_attention()` due to a recent change in #153432 to the `is_contiguous()` prim. cc @laithsakka / @bobrenjc93

Flex has problems with subclasses + compile in general; issues have also surfaced for DTensor. Additional context: #151719

I'm working on resolving this in some way or another for NJT.

After discussion with @drisspg and others, we plan to discourage the use of NJT + flex attention since it is broken within torch.compile. It's a fair amount of work to fix and no one is actively prioritizing NJT development at this time.

We encourage everyone interested to instead follow the document masking example [here](https://github.com/pytorch-labs/attention-gym/blob/main/attn_gym/masks/document_mask.py).

That's a pity - I was actually planning to switch to flex + NJT since I encountered too many issues with (compiled) flex + document masking. In the end I got a version which was running for hundreds of iterations but then randomly crashed with `RuntimeError: CUDA error: an illegal memory access was encountered`. I gave up on it since this happened non-deterministically and I couldn't trim it down to a reasonable reproducer. It was somehow related to the backward pass, which made debugging at my end impossible.

I guess the options I have left are flex + padding or `F.scaled_dot_product_attention` + NJT? Or is the latter unstable as well?

Would there be an advantage of non-NJT SDPA with variable sequence lengths compared to NJT SDPA? Is it more stable since it doesn't rely on NJT?

In my use case I would ideally have variable sequence lengths in contiguous layout + additional score/mask_mods. Padding is inconvenient since I want to interleave attention layers with other operations that require contiguous/concatenated inputs - this would require me to cast back and forth between padded/contiguous layouts. SDPA with contiguous sequences might be a solution, but I am worried that masking / score biases would be infeasible in this case?

I went down a similar path considering my options. I was also blocked by SDPA not supporting masking with NJTs: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/138993

So seems like the current best option is still good ol padding?

154652.body:
I had an idea a while ago which I think would solve this (basically only enable fusions within a window) https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/106996/files

Let me see if this would work today with a benchmark run

I mentioned this to @mlazos previously but posting here for posterity:

Today, we group nodes together by buffer access, then try to individually fuse them. In the individual fusion, we pairwise fuse all nodes in a group. This pairwise fusion goes through a slower path that might try to do things like reorder dependencies, which is good. 

However, in rare cases, we can have buffer accesses > 1000 nodes. Obviously we should not pairwise fuse here. In this case - for larger N in buffer access - we should group nodes by (buffer_access, memory_exprs). That way we only fuse things that are guaranteed to have same dependency.  

Even 64 * 64 = 4096 is quite large.
Fyi to anyone reading this, https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/154688 is the same as the previously linked PR from 2023, just updated for 2025 code.

154847.body:
Let me know if I understand this properly: this is only really an issue in the presence of graph-breaks between where the `AsyncCollectiveTensor` is created and when it's used, is that right?

One more question: do we expect AsyncCollectiveTensor to be useful in compile mode, given that the compiler will reorder / bucket comms? My understanding was that AsyncCollectiveTensor was mostly a eager-mode thing to simplify having async semantics in eager mode, without too much hassle from the user side.

One thing which makes me a bit uneasy with having isolated `wait_tensor` from their corresponding issuing collective (like `all_gather`) is that it kinds of breaks the current invariant:
- that the only user of a collective is it's `wait_tensor` -- now we also have `output` nodes that can be consumers of collectives
- the only parent of a `wait_tensor` is a collective -- now it can also be input nodes.

This makes it harder to ensure passes are correct, because we need to track behavior across graphs (instead of being isolated into a single graph).

Given those points, I'd actually vote for option 0, which is to unconditionally add `wait_tensor` whenever an `AsyncCollectiveTensor` is created in compile mode. This could indeed loose some perf because we can't overlap across graph boundaries anymore, but I think having to support multi-graph collectives might require further reflexion.

Thoughts?

Thanks, Brian for this.

I'm with Francisco: `AsyncCollectiveTensor` to me is effectively type sugar to annotate a tensor as needing synchronization via some comms.

Graph breaks with synchronization just introduces a scheduling problem. This is obviously going to be even more common as tensor parallel (and even heterogeneous, RL-based) workloads proliferate. Writing passes that are aware of both dependent ops and the synchronization (or communication) components required for data availability and thus graph dependencies is quite important, especially as we're building cost models that are comms aware.

My view: adding `wait_tensor` everywhere (option 0) in the same graph is the only option that seems reasonable; it makes no sense to not recompile but to not always add `wait_tensor`.
- The overhead of enforcing the dependency across streams (this is just `cudaStreamWaitEvent`, right) should be low?
- One difficulty might come from how async collectives get scheduled with ops in other streams because we can't decide _when_ collectives get executed, and it's possible that collectives use SMs that otherwise would be performing arithmetic ops that expect those SMs to be available. This is a problem that can be resolved with other passes, though, to insert that `wait_tensor` in the right place.

> this is only really an issue in the presence of graph-breaks between where the AsyncCollectiveTensor is created and when it's used, is that right?

Yup I would say this is right. I'm not sure exactly how Jacob ran into it, but the other way I've seen this manifest is that torchtitan compiles each transformer block layer (instead of the whole model) - so the input to layer 0 is a plain tensor, while the inputs to layers 1...N are the outputs of collectives from previous layers.

So one way to split this might be... agree that this is only a problem if you have graph breaks / regional compilation, and require capturing larger regions of code to avoid this recompile. (just to call it out - or maybe we think this case is important enough to fix)

> One more question: do we expect AsyncCollectiveTensor to be useful in compile mode, given that the compiler will reorder / bucket comms? My understanding was that AsyncCollectiveTensor was mostly a eager-mode thing to simplify having async semantics in eager mode, without too much hassle from the user side.

@wconstab can probably comment better on the "why we have ACT" story.

But I think that one way to think of it is that:

(1) we made the decision to model collectives' asynchronous nature via an explicit `wait_tensor` IR node in the graph

(2) ACT "mostly" does not exist inside of the compiler. When we trace functional collectives inside a compiled region, we don't use ACT at all, and just directly emit calls to `wait_tensor` as necessary.

(3) collectives at the graph boundary are a bit different, because we need to glue together compiled regions with surrounding eager code. The way this is modeled today is that if you have a collective output from a previous compiled region that goes into another compiled region, it will show up as an ACT graph input. In practice though, the ACT doesn't actually show up in the graph that compile captures. It just desugars into a call to `wait_tensor`. So in both cases here, from the c

> it kinds of breaks the current invariant:

> that the only user of a collective is it's wait_tensor -- now we also have output nodes that can be consumers of collectives

> the only parent of a wait_tensor is a collective -- now it can also be input nodes.

hmmm let me know if I'm talking past you. But if we really do care about graph breaks, this feels kind of inevitable? Insofar as "graph A returns a collective, graph B/C both might consume it".

>  I'd actually vote for option 0, which is to unconditionally add wait_tensor whenever an AsyncCollectiveTensor is created in compile mode. This could indeed loose some perf because we can't overlap across graph boundaries anymore, but I think having to support multi-graph collectives might require further reflexion.

Ah ok - you're advocating for "force every graph to synchronize its collective outputs"? I agree that makes things simpler. Although two there are two reasons I'm not sure if this will fix our problem in all cases (let me know if you agree):

(1) even in the fullgraph case, we don't really promise to capture the optimizer today (we can, but this is extra work + an extra burden on the user, so I imagine we can't force everyone to do it). If we assume that a chunk of users will be ok with fullgraph fw / bw but not necessarily capture the optimizer into their bw graph, I don't have a good sense for how much synchronizing the collective before we start running the optimizer might cause problems

(2) there's also nothing stopping users from running collectives in eager mode *outside* of the compiler. If a user runs a collective in eager, and passes that output to a compiled region, then we still have the same problem of "input to a compiled region needs to be synchronized".

The other option in my head that is pretty similar to yours (assuming we are ok with losing a bit of perf when you don't capture a full graph) is: just have the compiler automatically synchronize inputs tensors that came from collectives,

> If a user runs a collective in eager, and passes that output to a compiled region

I believe @Microve (Shuai)'s use case on sparse arch currently does have this usage pattern (and also the reverse: runs collective in compile, passes the output to eager). Would be great to see if can support Shuai's use case as well.

> > If a user runs a collective in eager, and passes that output to a compiled region
> 
> I believe [@Microve](https://github.com/Microve) (Shuai)'s use case on sparse arch currently does have this usage pattern (and also the reverse: runs collective in compile, passes the output to eager). Would be great to see if can support Shuai's use case as well.

Yes, my use case is that currently we disable sparse arch so the collectives are issued in eager via `dist.all_to_all` but we want its wait part in the compiled region, because right after the wait part is over_arch which we compiled, if the wait part is compiled then it can be merged with the subsequent over_arch.

This is forward pass, in the backward pass, the collectives will be issued in compiled region with `dist._functional_collectives.all_to_all_single` and the wait part will be in the eager region

155044.body: It looks like export is struggling to trace through torch.autograd.grad. cc: @zou3519 Can we also proxy this in the pre-dispatch IR?

It's not always safe to proxy autograd.grad into the graph. Here are some failure cases (there may be more):
1) if the autograd graph is not completely contained in the exported program
2) if there are any hooks (backward hooks, accumulate grad hooks, etc)

I'm bumping priority for this because @Darktex showed me a use case last week where some folks were also trying to torch.export through grad/jacobian and it was not working. You actually got pretty close to cracking the code in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/146719

Here is the problem: the lifted tensor is, as you identified, a value computed from forwards that was saved for backwards and should be reused later. This constant SHOULD NOT EXIST AT ALL. Instead, we should have been using 'sqrt' node's value in your graph. 

This is in all likelihood an own-goal by the export logic somewhere, because when we just do a plain `make_fx` there is no problem:

```
import torch

class Model(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()

    def forward(self, x):
        y = torch.sqrt(x)
        dy_dx = torch.autograd.grad(y, x, torch.ones_like(y))[0]
        return y, dy_dx

model = Model()

x = torch.rand([100,100])
x = x.requires_grad_(True)

inputs = (x,)

y_ref, dy_dx_ref = model.forward(*inputs)

from torch.fx.experimental.proxy_tensor import make_fx

gm = make_fx(model)(x)
gm.print_readable()
```

produces

```
class <lambda>(torch.nn.Module):
    def forward(self, arg0_1: "f32[100, 100]"):
        # No stacktrace found for following nodes
        sqrt: "f32[100, 100]" = torch.ops.aten.sqrt.default(arg0_1);  arg0_1 = None
        detach: "f32[100, 100]" = torch.ops.aten.detach.default(sqrt)
        ones_like: "f32[100, 100]" = torch.ops.aten.ones_like.default(sqrt, pin_memory = False)
        detach_1: "f32[100, 100]" = torch.ops.aten.detach.default(detach);  detach = None
        mul: "f32[100, 100]" = torch.ops.aten.mul.Scalar(detach_1, 2);  detach_1 = None  # <--- NOT LIFTED!
        div: "f32[100, 100]" = torch.ops.aten.div.Tensor(ones_like, mul);  ones_like = mul = None
        return (sqrt, div)
```

I /know/ this should work, because we rely on tracing through grad all the time in AOTAutograd.
 
I suppose there's also an extra

As a neat trick, if you make_fx and then torch.export the GM, it works!

```
import torch

class Model(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()

    def forward(self, x):
        y = torch.sqrt(x)
        dy_dx = torch.autograd.grad(y, x, torch.ones_like(y))[0]
        return y, dy_dx

model = Model()

x = torch.rand([100,100])
x = x.requires_grad_(True)

inputs = (x,)

y_ref, dy_dx_ref = model.forward(*inputs)

from torch.fx.experimental.proxy_tensor import make_fx

ep = torch.export.export(make_fx(model)(*inputs), args=inputs, strict=False)
#ep = ep.run_decompositions(torch.export.default_decompositions()) #Can't decompose because we hit 'RuntimeError: CUDA error: an illegal memory access was encountered'
print(ep)

y_test, dy_dx_test = ep.module()(*inputs)

assert torch.allclose(y_ref, y_test), 'Mismatch between Python module and call to its underlying export program for y!' #Passes

print('Reference: ' + str(dy_dx_ref))
print('Export: ' + str(dy_dx_test))
assert torch.allclose(dy_dx_ref,dy_dx_test), 'Mismatch between Python module and call to its underlying export program for dy_dx!' #Fails because dy_dx_test is a FakeTensor
```

> As a neat trick, if you make_fx and then torch.export the GM, it works!

There are other use cases for this (see one of the export user interviews) and this is what those people have discovered too.

Just making a comment on our enthusiasm for this to be fixed as people who have models that include autograd for inference "forward", and how we based a big part of our infrastructure on `make_fx` (https://github.com/mir-group/nequip/blob/main/nequip/utils/fx.py) partly because of this comment https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/91469#issuecomment-1873221656. e.g.

- `make_fx` -> `torch.compile` to get around double backward problems for training (https://github.com/mir-group/nequip/blob/main/nequip/nn/compile.py) 
- `make_fx` -> `export` -> AOT Inductor for this specific issue (https://github.com/mir-group/nequip/blob/main/nequip/utils/aot.py)

Just to say that we've gotten to "production" with `make_fx`-taped solutions, but feels like a workaround that makes us concerned about its long-term sustainability.

I think `make_fx` is likely to keep existing "as is" for the forseeable future. So if there's some new thing that make_fx doesn't support, we're probably not going to fix it (especially if it's handled by a different layer), but we're not going to gratuitously break it. So you should be able to tell if you get to the point (e.g., dynamic shapes, pytree, dynamo etc) that you need more assist.
assuming this doesn't require triage review since it's assigned.

155105.body: Have you looked at https://docs.pytorch.org/tutorials/intermediate/compiled_autograd_tutorial.html#common-recompilation-reasons-for-compiled-autograd yet? To get recompile information for compiled autograd, you'll need to turn on verbose logging with env var `TORCH_LOGS="compiled_autograd_verbose"`. This will output the reasons to stdout, and also log them in [tlparse](https://github.com/pytorch/tlparse/tree/main?tab=readme-ov-file#tlparse-parse-structured-pt2-logs).
It's also possible that this is from dynamo recompiling, the tlparse will also have the recompile_reasons for dynamo if any

@xmfan Thank you for your reply! After enabling TORCH_LOGS="compiled_autograd_verbose", we observed the following log:
```
[__compiled_autograd_verbose] Cache miss due to new autograd node: ApplyTemplateBackward (NodeCall 7) with key size 81, previous key sizes=[81,81,81,81,81]
```
This suggests  Autograd Function in our implementation leads to recompilations in compiled autograd. It is strange that there is not any dynamic branch nor shapes in these functions. Also we currently rely on autograd function for some operations. Would you recommend any strategies to mitigate such recompiles?

Is ApplyTemplate a python custom autograd function? If so, the key construction referred to by the logs is done here: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/79aef141695f2daea4a9aeb0f385726c5794a242/torch/csrc/autograd/python_function.cpp#L305-L359, where each args.collect/collect_size call contributes some bytes to the key, for a total of 81. Although the key is 81 bytes each time, it is different in value meaning one/some of those args.collect/collect_size calls made us specialize.

Could you share more about ApplyTemplate's implementation? 

Is the ApplyTemplate class being redefined each iteration? Each autograd.Function is assigned an id at class definition time, so even with the same name, a new class will be treated differently

Otherwise, we should inspect its `ctx` usage and the differences it has each iteration.

`ApplyTemplate` is defined inside `AutogradFunctionApply` at:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/ea37f720995616d8570045c63486198a021f8285/torch/_functorch/autograd_function.py#L740-L774

Will this be redefined every time we apply autograd function?

ApplyTemplate should be redefined each time the dynamo recompiles the forward. If the forward cache hits, I wouldn't expect compiled autograd to need to recompile.

Do you see recompiles in the forward (TORCH_LOGS="recompiles")?
The forward cache can hit and there are no recompiles, but for the backward, it keeps missing the cache and do recompiles.

When I run the following toy example, I could observe the same behavior. The backward graph keeps recompiling `[__compiled_autograd_verbose] Cache miss due to new autograd node: ApplyTemplateBackward (NodeCall 1) with key size 79, previous key sizes=[79,79,79,79,79,79,79,79,79]`. Does this have something to do with torch.autograd.Function?

```python import torch
import torch._dynamo
from torch.autograd.variable import Variable

class MatMul(torch.autograd.Function):
    @staticmethod
    def forward(ctx, a, b):
        c = a @ b
        ctx.save_for_backward(a, b)
        return c
    
    @staticmethod
    def backward(ctx, grad_output):
        a, b = ctx.saved_tensors
        grad_a = grad_output @ b.t()
        grad_b = a.t() @ grad_output
        return grad_a, grad_b

class MM(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        
    def forward(self, a, b):
        return MatMul.apply(a, b)

def custom_compiler(gm, example_inputs):
    print("=== Captured Graph ===")
    gm.print_readable()
    print("=" * 50)
    return gm

mul = MM()
compiled_mul = torch.compile(mul, backend=custom_compiler, fullgraph=True, dynamic=True)
a = torch.randn(2, 3, requires_grad=True)
b = torch.randn(3, 3, requires_grad=True)

for _ in range(10):

    output_tensor = compiled_mul(a, b)
    output_grad = torch.ones_like(output_tensor)

    with torch._dynamo.compiled_autograd._enable(
        torch.compile(
            backend=custom_compiler,
            fullgraph=True,
            dynamic=True,
        ),
        dynamic=True,
    ):
        Variable._execution_engine.run_backward(
            tensors=(output_tensor,),
            grad_tensors=(output_grad,),
            keep_graph=False,
            create_graph=False,
            inputs=tuple(),
            allow_unreachable=True,
            accumulate_grad=True,
        )

Ah I see, this is because of an interaction with the custom backend and the higher order op.

> ApplyTemplate should be redefined each time the dynamo recompiles the forward.

This is only true if you use AOT Autograd on your forward, which will inline into the higher order op, and desugar that ApplyTemplateBackward into:
```python
# backward graph
class GraphModule(torch.nn.Module):
    def forward(self, primals_2: "Sym(s98)", primals_1: "Sym(s97)", t: "f32[s98, s98][1, s98]cpu", t_1: "f32[s98, s97][1, s98]cpu", tangents_1: "f32[s97, s98][s98, 1]cpu"):
         # File: /home/xmfan/core/a/pytorch/carecompile.py:23 in forward, code: return MatMul.apply(a, b)
        mm_1: "f32[s97, s98][s98, 1]cpu" = torch.ops.aten.mm.default(tangents_1, t);  t = None
        mm_2: "f32[s98, s98][s98, 1]cpu" = torch.ops.aten.mm.default(t_1, tangents_1);  t_1 = tangents_1 = None
        return (None, None, mm_1, mm_2)
        
```

But without using AOT Autograd, the `AutogradFunctionApply` higher order op is not desugared, and will define a new ApplyTemplate each time. From the perspective of Compiled Autograd, the code has changed.

I'm not sure how to fix yet, two possible solutions are:
1. Define ApplyTemplate only once cc @oulgen
2. Relax compiled autograd's torch.autograd.Function key
Defining ApplyTemplate once might be hard since you need to capture stuff from AutogradFunctionApply's call method for backward and forward. @zou3519 in case you have any ideas?

155121.body: This happens with torch.cat two non_blocking Transfers, not on its own.

I think we'd want to track whether Tensors are pinned in their Layout. And then in the 
```
a = torch.ops.prims.device_put.default(arg0_1, device(type='cpu'), True);
b = torch.ops.prims.device_put.default(arg0_1, device(type='cpu'), True);
torch.cat([a, b])
```

inputs pinned_memory should transfer to the ConcatKernel: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/a99a01a677f859bc9800730927c8b7ae4adf7e41/torch/_inductor/ir.py#L4987-L4998. 

thoughts @jansel @shunting314 ?
Yea, good point, I guess even outside of cat, we'll need to fix the cpu allocation. For this repro in particular - we'll also need to transfer the pinned_allocation in ir.Concat.

Pointers

For the following program:

```
import torch
torch._inductor.config.force_disable_caches = True


@torch.compile()
def foo(x):
    return x.to("cpu", non_blocking=True)

x = torch.tensor(3, device="cuda")
foo(x)
```

We should be allocating `buf0 = empty_strided_cpu((), (), torch.int64)` in pinned memory. We should add `is_pinned` to [Layout](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/35df895d0564cc53dfcad829732fc6b3a9b7eb86/torch/_inductor/ir.py#L3577) and then make sure we respect that in tensor allocation.

> Pointers
> 
> For the following program:
> 
> ```
> import torch
> torch._inductor.config.force_disable_caches = True
> 
> 
> @torch.compile()
> def foo(x):
>     return x.to("cpu", non_blocking=True)
> 
> x = torch.tensor(3, device="cuda")
> foo(x)
> ```
> 
> We should be allocating `buf0 = empty_strided_cpu((), (), torch.int64)` in pinned memory. We should add `is_pinned` to [Layout](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/35df895d0564cc53dfcad829732fc6b3a9b7eb86/torch/_inductor/ir.py#L3577) and then make sure we respect that in tensor allocation.

@eellison regarding https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/155121#issuecomment-2940443970 , should the logic for the concat memory is_pinned be based on whether *any* input is_pinned, or whether *all* inputs are is_pinned?

So, in general, `torch.cat` does not preserve whether the inputs are pinned.

```
>>> import torch
>>> x = torch.rand([4]).pin_memory()
>>> x.is_pinned()
True
>>> print(torch.cat((x, x)).is_pinned())
False
```

The reason inductor will need to handle this is because Inductor may memory plan away the Concat making it a no-op. See, [strategy 1 here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1wk00PtNqV2pFf5j8OXdAejiTh89sSD03PYkHHDDp8og/edit?tab=t.0). In this case Concat is now doing a single allocation. See output code in, https://manifold.edge.x2p.facebook.net/v0/read/tree/logs/eellison/custom/index.html?bucketName=tlparse_reports&apiKey=tlparse_reports-key&withPayload=1&timeoutMsec=10000, there is only a single `empty_strided_cpu`. That allocation needs to be on pinned memory so its memory - planned away inputs behave as they should in the prior device_puts.

In the first pr, let's just handle the case where they're all on pinned memory. Potentially afterwards if not all inputs are on pinned memory and the not pinned inputs would allocate an excessive amount of pinned memory (not sure how to determine this), we could realize the pinned inputs and then clone them into the cat, at the cost of extra cpu kernel.

I don't think torch.compile has any modeling of pinned memory, unless someone added it without me noticing.

Does FakeTensor/decomps/AOTAutograd model pinned memory properly?  I imagine that will be the main challenge.   First make `node.meta["val"].is_pinned()` correct, then when inductor generates an allocation consult that value.

In case of `device_put` pinning output memory is an implementation detail in ATen, so FakeTensor/AOTAutograd doesn't handle  it, but they properly preserve non-blocking argument which determines whether memory will be pinned

155209.body: Do you know offhand if your test passes on nvidia?
It passes on Nvidia and I noticed it only happens when up-sampling, eg. (128, 128) -> (24, 24) is fine but (128, 128) -> (200, 200) fails

Note sure why the example runs on NVIDIA.

The maximum integer that can be stored with unint8 is 255. If you are upsampling, integers would rollover.  There is no way to ensure you have the same integer rollover in eager mode and compile mode. 

If I change your example to `torch.uint16`, the max difference is now 0.0. I think that is the safer and more robust route.

155309.body:
**Observations:**
1- Works on CPU → confirms model logic is sound and compatible with torch.export.

2- Fails only on GPU → suggests backend kernel incompatibility or faulty tracing of native GPU LSTM internals.

3- Error hints at inappropriate tracing into custom kernels, likely violating the fake tensor abstraction during export.

**Potential Workaround Ideas:**
1- Try disabling fused LSTM kernels (e.g., by setting torch.backends.cudnn.enabled = False) — not ideal, but worth testing.

2- Replace nn.LSTM with a manually unrolled LSTM or use torch.nn.LSTMCell in loop (slower but may sidestep export tracing issue).

3- Explore exporting from CPU and then using TorchScript or ONNX to bridge to GPU usage downstream.

_If the issue lies in how torch.export interprets custom CUDA kernels or fused ops as traceable instead of opaque, this might warrant an enhancement in fake tensor dispatching or tracing internals to properly mark them._

> **Observations:** 1- Works on CPU → confirms model logic is sound and compatible with torch.export.
> 
> 2- Fails only on GPU → suggests backend kernel incompatibility or faulty tracing of native GPU LSTM internals.
> 
> 3- Error hints at inappropriate tracing into custom kernels, likely violating the fake tensor abstraction during export.
> 
> **Potential Workaround Ideas:** 1- Try disabling fused LSTM kernels (e.g., by setting torch.backends.cudnn.enabled = False) — not ideal, but worth testing.
> 
> 2- Replace nn.LSTM with a manually unrolled LSTM or use torch.nn.LSTMCell in loop (slower but may sidestep export tracing issue).
> 
> 3- Explore exporting from CPU and then using TorchScript or ONNX to bridge to GPU usage downstream.
> 
> _If the issue lies in how torch.export interprets custom CUDA kernels or fused ops as traceable instead of opaque, this might warrant an enhancement in fake tensor dispatching or tracing internals to properly mark them._

Thank you so much for the detailed and insightful analysis.

I followed your suggestion and tested with:
```python
torch.backends.cudnn.enabled = False
```
As you anticipated, export on GPU succeeded when disabling cuDNN. This strongly indicates that the issue is indeed related to certain cuDNN-optimized fused kernels, likely being incorrectly traced during export when fake tensors are used.

For completeness, my environment reports the following cuDNN version:
```python
torch.backends.cudnn.version() == 90100
```
This further supports the hypothesis that some low-level dispatch or fusion within cuDNN is not being properly marked as opaque or guarded during export tracing?


Additionally, I’d like to report that the same issue also occurs when using nn.RNN instead of nn.LSTM. Specifically, like:
```python
self.rnn = nn.RNN(input_size, hidden_size, num_layers, batch_first=True)
```
This confirms that the problem is not limited to LSTM, but more broadly affects cuDNN-backed recurrent layers, including `nn.RNN`. Dis
I reproduced the issue in Google Colab, but I don’t have a local GPU. If I make a fix, what’s the best way for me to test it on a GPU?

Testing fused op internals like cuDNN LSTM/RNN behavior across PyTorch export mechanisms really needs a stable local GPU with debugger-level access. Colab's environment is helpful for basic repro, but isn't ideal for validating backend behavior across drivers, CUDA versions, or cuDNN optimizations — especially with torch.export and fake_tensor flows.

If helpful, I can run patches or test branches locally on an RTX 5080 with full stack access (including driver-level tracing). Let me know how I can assist.

@malfet Thanks for stepping in — this seems to hinge on how torch.export interacts with fused CuDNN ops like LSTM/RNN under PT2. I suspect it’s not just a tracing mismatch but possibly a backend assumption not holding in exported graphs. Debugging this properly probably needs local GPU access with kernel-level visibility.

If you’ve got any insight on how torch.compile or torch.export stabilizes around CuDNN graph capture for stateful ops (like LSTMs), that’d be clutch. Happy to repro or benchmark anything you want.
This is interesting. Does it repro on latest nightly?

It is due to this line which is not PT2 compatible: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/be2ad70cfa1360da5c23a04ff6ca3480fa02f278/torch/nn/modules/rnn.py#L256

I think we need a better way to detect aliasing here. 
cc: @zou3519l @bdhirsh

Thanks for chiming in @tugsbayasgalan — that’s an insightful callout.

You're absolutely right: relying on p.data_ptr() in the context of PT2 + fake tensor export is incompatible because fake tensors deliberately lack storage. The direct dereferencing of data pointers breaks the abstraction layer that torch.export relies on when simulating backend behavior without materialized memory.

If the intent behind that data_ptr() call is to perform aliasing checks or validate weight layout contiguity, we might need a functional alias-safe mechanism that introspects tensor metadata (like shape/stride/type) without assuming physical storage access. Maybe something along the lines of:

_# Pseudocode for fake-tensor-safe alias detection
torch._utils.assert_no_overlap_or_duplicate(self._flat_weights, mode="meta_safe")
Another potential direction could be to wrap the logic in a dispatch guard or fallback for fake tensors — similar to how other parts of PT2 gracefully bail out or skip layout-sensitive checks when running under export or compile._

Also worth noting: this issue isn't isolated to nn.LSTM, but also affects nn.RNN and likely nn.GRU, as all of them rely on _flat_weights. So the fix needs to generalize across those layers.

If you're exploring refactors for that section, I’m happy to test branches locally with real GPU, export, and fake tensor setups (RTX 5080, latest nightly). Let me know what’s helpful — I can drop in profiling, test against fused/non-fused CuDNN, or benchmark ONNX + export graphs as needed.

Good evening,
Kent (aka @kentstone84)

That’s super helpful — thanks for surfacing that from @bdhirsh.

StorageWeakRef is a clean way to safely detect shared storage without violating fake tensor constraints. I like that it bypasses the whole .data_ptr() issue entirely while remaining backend-agnostic.

Do we know how robust this is under various contexts like XLA, Meta tensors, or export? I can run local tests across:

-torch.export() vs non-export paths

-nn.LSTM, nn.GRU, and fused/unfused backends

-Fake tensor + meta tensor scenarios

If it checks out across all of those, we could consider a drop-in replacement or utility wrapper for internal _flat_weights usage across RNN modules.

Hi @pytorchbot, @tugsbayasgalan, and PyTorch maintainers,

I’m interested in contributing to resolving this issue with torch.export.export() failing on GPU for RNN modules. I see that PR #155734 has been linked to address this, likely implementing the StorageWeakRef-based fix suggested by @bdhirsh to replace the .data_ptr() call in torch/nn/modules/rnn.py.

Could I assist with this issue, perhaps by testing the existing PR or exploring an alternative approach? Specifically, I’d like to confirm:

1. Should I focus on testing PR #155734 across different setups (e.g., GPU with fused/unfused cuDNN, latest nightly, or meta tensor scenarios)?

2. Is there an opportunity to contribute an alternative fix, such as a utility wrapper for alias detection that generalizes across nn.LSTM, nn.RNN, and nn.GRU?

3. Are there specific test cases I should add to test/test_export.py to validate the fix for fake tensor compatibility?

4. What’s the best way to coordinate with @penknife6153 or others working on PR #155734 to avoid duplicating efforts?

Do you have a full repro we can run locally? Or a [tlparse](https://github.com/pytorch/tlparse) output?  There are many reasons the cache may not hit or bypass your use case, but it's hard without a repro. 

Also, the compiler cache will save *compile* time, but not necessarily **inference** time, depending on how much inference you do. It only saves the time torch.compile takes on first compile, so a better measurement would be to measure how long torch.compile compiles for (which a tlparse would be able to tell us)

```python
import torch
from torch.fx.experimental.proxy_tensor import make_fx

class M(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.linear = torch.nn.Linear(3, 3)

    def forward(self, x1, x2):
        y = self.linear(x1)
        return [x2 + 0.5, y]
        # the following works

x1 = torch.randn(2, 3, device="cuda")
x2 = torch.randn(2, 3, device="cuda")
model = M().to(device="cuda")

fx_model = make_fx(model, tracing_mode="symbolic", _allow_non_fake_inputs=True, _error_on_data_dependent_ops=True)(*[x1, x2])

ep = torch.export.export(fx_model, (x1, x2))

155433.body:
Some quick thoughts: I would have imagined that instead of saving the params in the `BundledAOTAutogradCacheEntry`, we would want to have some similar logic to lift them at runtime into extra graph arguments. Also, is there a repro that uses `inline_inbuilt_nn_modules=True` where we see the params/buffers that AOT has to lift?

(1) the params themselves can be large tensors, so won't reading/writing them directly to the cache cause problems? (longer read/write latency, also blowing up the size of the cache)

(2) The background here is that... AOTDispatcher used to lift **all** params/buffers into graph inputs a while ago, but after @anijain2305 landed `inline_inbuilt_nn_modules=True`, most params/buffers started getting lifted by dynamo. I don't actually have a good sense of which params/buffers are not handled by this case and still need to be lifted by AOT. Maybe @anijain2305 knows? If we want to avoid saving these params to the cache directly, we probably need to either have dynamo properly lift **all** of these params/buffers, or figure out what their dynamo source is so we can lift them properly on a cache hit.
Ah, it's possible that this is just legacy code, and we don't need to care about it as long as `inline_inbuilt_nn_modules=True`. I will double check!
Verified that we don't need this as liong as inline_inbuilt_nn_modules=True, though there is https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/159228 for mark_static_address behavior

    batch_dim = torch.export.Dim("batch", min=1, max=1024)
    exported = torch.export.export(model, example_inputs, dynamic_shapes={"x": {0: batch_dim}})
```

155679.body: This is a known behavior of Nvidia's CUDAGraph API that launches an arbitrary number of CUDA streams. torch.compile cannot control this behavior. It does not show a large perf impact, but leads to some UX issues: when there are many CUDA streams, it is harder to read the trace. cc @galv @ngimel @kwen2501 @eellison

> This is a known behavior of Nvidia's CUDAGraph API that launches an arbitrary number of CUDA streams

Does this occur only in multi-stream scenarios like when NCCL is launched on a separate stream because when I turn off all the NCCL calls, I don't see compute kernels being launched on different streams. However with NCCL, I see an arbitrary number of streams and both compute and communication being in various different streams.
IIRC, it happens for multi-stream scenarios and it's not limited to NCCL.

NCCL internally has a "hostStream" and "deviceStream" associated with each ncclComm_t. It does all "nccl work" on these two streams in order to prevent two streams of work on the same communicator from executing at the same time. This precludes deadlock scenarios, as I understand it. Unfortunately, the cudaStreamWaitEvents and cudaEventRecords that do this (e.g., here: https://github.com/NVIDIA/nccl/blob/72d2432094d6ae36abd6e511c3a16a2d052dbf94/src/enqueue.cc#L1475) cause a fork-join pattern that result in a new stream for every fork-join unfortunately due to some quirks in the cuda graph implementation. It shouldn't affect the correctness of your performance. We are working on fixing this behavior on the cuda driver side, but I cannot give a timeline right now.

You might want to read https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-c-programming-guide/index.html#cross-stream-dependencies-and-events if you haven't already to understand how streams turn into forks and joins inside a cuda graph during stream capture.

> related [#152114](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/152114)

This suggests that there might be a way of writing the code such that so many streams are not created. I do see performance due to the creation of streams. Is the code re-write something that can be done on torch's end or will it have to be within NCCL?

It will have to be within cudagraphs, it's a cudagraphs bug. We cannot do anything (short of manually modifying cudagraph) on pytorch side, because we just directly call into nccl and nccl inserts branching events. It's also unclear what the exact rewrite should be as no one knows the heuristics cudagraphs use to spawn new streams. The performance impact of multiple streams as currently happens should be minimal though, it's mostly inconvenience in profiling. cc @eqy if there's maybe more information from nvidia side.

> NCCL internally has a "hostStream" and "deviceStream" associated with each ncclComm_t. It does all "nccl work" on these two streams in order to prevent two streams of work on the same communicator from executing at the same time. 

Is it possible to specify (through the NCCL API or some flag) to disable this behaviour, knowing that we will never launch two streams of work on the same communicator at the same time?

I do not fully understand this but I think [NCCL_GRAPH_MIXING_SUPPORT](https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/nccl/user-guide/docs/env.html#nccl-graph-mixing-support). also does something similar and disabling it helped reduce the number of streams and also improved performance. (We do see a performance impact as a side effect of creating multiple streams with an increased GPU idle time in our application)
You are welcome to file a nccl bug, in the meantime your best bet is direct manipulation of your graph to remove unneeded branching https://gist.github.com/ngimel/0f69199142671a3583538f3adbfb1594

Hi Everyone, Posting a quick update on this issue from Nvidia. We have an improvement scheduled to release in CUDA 13.2 that improves CUDA Graph stream usage for graphs that have a fork-join pattern. I'll follow up once CUDA 13.2 is released to see if that improvement fixes this issue for you.
