155825.body:
I noticed that when running a DispatchMode in compiled regions (note: infra mode enabled), __torch_dispatch__ reports that torch.compiler.is_compiling() is always False

However in the case of a Tensor subclass, is_compiling returns True for all graph compiled regions (and false for eager regions, as expected)

Is this expected behavior?

Here's an example of printing ops & compiled regions

Simple model with disabled submodule, and a dynamo backend that just prints ops
```
import torch 

@torch._dynamo.disable()
class TestSubModule(torch.nn.Module):
    def forward(self, x):
        return 2 * x


class TestModule(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.submod = TestSubModule()

    def forward(self, x):
        tmp = x + 3
        tmp = self.submod(tmp)
        return tmp + 4

class CustomBackend:
    def __call__(self, gm, example_inputs):
        print("____ GRAPH MODE ____")
        gm.print_readable()
        return gm
```

Tensor Subclassing:
```

class CustomTensor(torch.Tensor):
    elem: torch.Tensor
    __slots__ = ["elem"]

    @staticmethod
    def __new__(cls, elem):
        r = torch.Tensor._make_wrapper_subclass(
            cls,
            elem.size(),
            strides=elem.stride(),
            storage_offset=elem.storage_offset(),
            dtype=elem.dtype,
            layout=elem.layout,
            device=elem.device,
            requires_grad=elem.requires_grad,
        )
        
        r.elem = elem
        return r

    def __repr__(self):
        if self.grad_fn:
            return f"CustomTensor({self.elem}, grad_fn={self.grad_fn})"
        return f"CustomTensor({self.elem})"

    def __torch_dispatch__(self, func, types, args=(), kwargs=None):
        def unwrap(e):
            return e.elem if isinstance(e, CustomTensor) else e

        # no_dispatch is only needed if you use enable_python_mode.
        # It prevents infinite recursion.
        rs = func(*tree_map(unwrap, args), **tree_map(unwrap, kwargs))

        print("____ DISPATCH MODE ____")
        print("OP:", func.__name__)
        print("Is compiling?: ", torch.compiler.is_compiling())

        def wrap(e):
            return CustomTensor(e) if isinstance(e, torch.Tensor) else e

        rs = tree_map(wrap, rs)
        return rs

mod = torch.compile(TestModule(),
We don't support arbitrary dispatch modes today, the is_infra_mode is for allowing internal modes that we use for compilation to work properly

        {0: 'd0', 2: 'd2', 3: 'd3'},
        # ... x n_layers
    ]
]
```

156037.body:
@CaoE I've been tracking this at https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/154598, but yes. When we split the build, we swapped over to building the wrapper code at `-O1` under the theory that it was not performance sensitive numerical code. Clearly for some models, that doesn't work as we'd hoped.

I have a couple of experiments I'm planning to run, to get a better sense of how to walk this back without giving up _all_ of the compile-time savings we eked out with this change. Are you able to share any details of how you setup your benchmarking run? I want to reproduce it as closely as possible while I test.

@benjaminglass1 I tested our internal llama int8 on CPU. And there is another discovery for your reference, even if I remove the setting of `min_optimize` for main code, it still can't reach the original performance. Separating entry part and kernel part seems to make the compiler miss some optimization opportunities, such as inline, etc. I think maybe we can try to reproduce with some simple functions: for example, generate many kernels whose calculation takes less time, and the entry code will call a large number of such simple kernels.
@benjaminglass1 Since we are close to the release branch cut (June 20), can we make this feature optional through #156050 to prevent any regressions? Thanks for any suggestions.

Hi @tgymnich, now `torch.export.export()` returns **pre-dispatch non-functional** IR by default and that's why you didn't see the BUFFER_MUTATION in the graph signature. To learn more, please refer to https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/export.html#export-for-training-and-inference

You can add one line after calling `torch.export.export()`
```
ep = exported_fx_graph.run_decompositions()
```
Then printing the graph signature again will give you the `BUFFER_MUTATION` in the output.

I'll update the document to reflect the latest status.

156206.body:
@aw632 Looking at the difference between `model_method1` and `model_method2`
```
# model_method1
SimpleMainModule(
  (encoder): Linear(in_features=512, out_features=512, bias=True)
  (submodule): CompiledSubmoduleWrapper()
  (output_layer): Linear(in_features=512, out_features=10, bias=True)
)
```

```
# model_method2
UnflattenedModule(
  (encoder): InterpreterModule()
  (submodule): CompiledSubmoduleWrapper()
  (output_layer): InterpreterModule()
)
```
My guess is that the slowness may come from the non-compiled part. As `encoder` and `output_layer` are now  `InterpreterModule()` as defined here https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/torch/export/unflatten.py#L130
Running `forward()` with `InterpreterModule()` in eager maybe slower than the native `nn.Linear`

@aw632 Kind of confirmed my guess above. In `model_method2`, if you don't do submodule replacement and directly run `model_method2`, it leads to even slower inference time. This means that AOTI compilation actually accelerates the submodule, but unflattened module interpreter slows down the execution.

My question for you is that you mentioned the motivation of using method 2 is "when deploying on edge without nn.Module definition". But the `InterpreterModule()` itself inherits from `nn.Module`, I wonder does Method 2 work on the edge? Are you trying to run inference on edge using Python but without PyTorch dependency?
cc @angelayi to comment on using `_disable_interpreter` to accelerate inference for unflattened module.

Yeah we've also noticed that running the InterpreterModule is slow because it's using torch.fx.Interpreter. Here's some tricks to make it faster:
* If you call `unflatten` with the `_disable_interpreter` context, this should change the runtime so that it's using torch.fx.GraphModule's native way of running the code, which should make it close to eager. 
* Additionally we can do some caching through calling `unflattened.finalize()`. 
* And then finally you can also disable checking the input constraints if you're confident in your inputs being the correct shape.

The code would look something like:

```python
with _disable_interpreter():
    unflattened = unflatten(export_module)

# < do your AOTI compilation + module swapping >

# cache the fx graphs
unflattened.finalize()

# disable the input checks
unflattened.check_input_constraints = False
```

I'm curious why you're doing partial submodule AOTI compilation though! Would love to learn more!

> [@aw632](https://github.com/aw632) Kind of confirmed my guess above. In `model_method2`, if you don't do submodule replacement and directly run `model_method2`, it leads to even slower inference time. This means that AOTI compilation actually accelerates the submodule, but unflattened module interpreter slows down the execution.
> 
> My question for you is that you mentioned the motivation of using method 2 is "when deploying on edge without nn.Module definition". But the `InterpreterModule()` itself inherits from `nn.Module`, I wonder does Method 2 work on the edge? Are you trying to run inference on edge using Python but without PyTorch dependency?

Sorry, by "without `nn.Module`, I mean that the inference infra was built with ONNX in mind, so we kept the nn.Module model definitions in the training side stack only. Now that we are moving to torch.compile/AOTI, we need the nn.Module *or* some alternative way of serving the model (in this case, we are exporting the model, then unflattening and replacing the one module we compiled).

> I'm curious why you're doing partial submodule AOTI compilation though! Would love to learn more!

Thanks for those tips! I will try that out. For us, compiling the submodule is (1) faster than compiling the whole model, and (2) does not cause accuracy issues. Roughly speaking, the architecture is a ViT backbone + diffusion head on top, and compiling the diffusion model sidesteps the accuracy issues for some reason. (The accuracy issues arise when TF32 is enabled, which is a must for performance for us.) As I understand from @ezyang's blog, partial submodule AOTI compilation is something that Meta is also doing, but maybe for different reasons?

```python
        with _disable_interpreter():
            # Unflatten
            model_method2 = torch.export.unflatten(loaded_exported)
        
        # Replace the submodule in the unflattened model
        model_method2.submodule = CompiledSubmoduleWrapper(compiled_submodule)
        model_method2.encoder.finalize()
        model_method2.output_layer.finalize()
        model_method2.check_input_constraints = False
```
With these changes, I do see a speedup although the gap is not closed just yet:
```
Method 1 median time:        0.526ms (speedup: 0.84x)
Method 2 median time:        0.783ms (speedup: 0.56x)
```
So I think for my use case, I'll proceed to refactor the code so that nn.Modules are available at inference time. Feel free to close if this is unflatten's intended behavior, I agree this is very much a "Hyrum's Law" use of AOTI :)

156308.body: Correct, the semantics are the inner call to `torch.compile` is ignored.  @williamwen42 has been adding some APIs that allow configs to be changed mid-compile though.

Rather than inducing a graph break with `@torch.compiler.disable(recursive=False)`, is there a way to retrieve which mode is actually being utilized and statically branch on that (without generating any guards)? In some cases I'd like to preserve full-graph compatibility and switch to the version of the code that best fit with the current mode, rather than graph breaking. 

The issue with graph breaks is that it interacts with my ability to export the model. As far as I'm aware there is no way to tell dynamo to do a graph break only for compile but not for export. And that means reverting to non-strict based export, which means potentially introducing bugs somewhere else. This is because as far as I can see (maybe it's a bug?) currently `torch.compiler.is_compiling()` and `torch.compiler.is_exporting()` are set together, and there is no export-only code path that sets only `torch.compiler.is_exporting()` https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/75f3e5a88df60caef27fd9c9df3fd51161378fcc/torch/_export/utils.py#L1301 .

As a general comment, I feel like that what I'm missing is a macro-like infrastructure to tell dynamo which piece of code it should be looking at based on its config and whether it's compile or export. I'm not the biggest fan of TorchScript, but with TorchScript having a mostly global config I remember I was able to do import-time transformations in the style of https://github.com/lihaoyi/macropy to make code more scripting friendly (a bit convoluted but sometimes helpful) and I could also distinguish easily between tracing and scripting. I don't know how to do that with Dynamo in a way that also accounts for a scoped config being propagated down from a different frame.

The backward pass for this variant of `_grouped_gemm` is tested [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/94f8679019ea4b1272f1ad58ad7cad87147cf5a7/test/test_matmul_cuda.py#L361-L415), and it works fine. `torch.export.load()` only supports loading the pt2 file generated by `torch.export.save()`. See https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/export.html#serialization

156377.body:
> I think you want `mark_static_address(static_y1, guard=False)`

This works, which makes me wonder if we should change the default to `False`. Are there any major performance implications? If not, we should make guard to `False` by default.

When I use guard=False, cudagraphs compilation ends up copying the tensor every single time. Therefore, guard=True does have performance implications. For a ~1gb llama model with 270 parameters I see about 40% of the invocation time taken up by copying all of the layers onto static memory; before running the model. Then doing this 1000 more times, once for each token.

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/157221

> > I think you want `mark_static_address(static_y1, guard=False)`
> 
> This works, which makes me wonder if we should change the default to `False`. Are there any major performance implications? If not, we should make guard to `False` by default.

There shouldn't be any performance implications, the main reason I made `True` the default is for correctness. I want to make sure a user knows what they're doing before setting it to `False`.  For @rsxdalv this shouldn't even be necessary because he's creating an NNModule. From our inlining work, we should automatically treat NNModule parameters as static with guard=False to do what you're describing @anijain2305.

156412.body:
`only_one_input` is used when the input matrix is repeated twice, since the generated args for the CPP template will have a definition with only one input: for example `cpp_bmm(T* X, T*Y_out){...}` instead of `cpp_bmm(T* X, T*W, T*Y_out){...}`. The benchmark code would pass in `[tensor1, tensor1]`, so having `only_one_input==True]` just takes the slice of the list `[tensor1]`.

We have a test `test/inductor/test_cpu_select_algorithm:TestSelectAlgorithm.test_bmm_self_square` that actually tests this exact scenario, but there seems to be some difference in compile settings between the test env and the code provided, so the benchmark code is only providing `[tensor1]` as the input instead of repeating the tensor. I'm looking into it now.

The generated code changed after [PR #151887](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/151887) for `functorch_maml_omniglot`, which appears to be a regression. However, it seems the CI did not flag this issue when the PR landed — not sure why. cc @chuanqi129

Submit a new PR: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/156526 which should fix the regression brought by [PR #151887](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/151887) and expect the perf of `functorch_maml_omniglot` should be improved.

> We have seen benchmark stability issues with the torchbench runners in CI. Is that related?

Not sure? I've only done the analysis on functorch_maml_omniglot. The thing about this one is it seems to randomly wobble up and down with people's changes, for no apparently discernible reason.

The answer why the regression is not detected is that the regression test is one-sided; you don't get forced to update the number with improvements in perf:

```
def check_perf_csv(filename, threshold, threshold_scale):
    """
    Basic performance checking.
    """

    df = pd.read_csv(filename)

    failed = []
    for _, row in df.iterrows():
        model_name = row["name"]
        speedup = row["speedup"]
        if speedup < threshold * threshold_scale:
            failed.append(model_name)

        print(f"{model_name:34} {speedup}")
```

cc @desertfire the original author of the performance script benchmarks/dynamo/check_perf_csv.py

@leslie-fang-intel While I can't easily review your PR, the fact that you were able to figure out the regression suggests that maybe this benchmark is doing something useful. I guess I'll try to debug my perf problem some more...

cc @williamwen42

There is another problem, which is that this signal is being put into the unstable bucket, e.g., as seen here When the signal is marked as unstable, that also means that we aren't getting signal on CI jobs

Hi @ezyang. Regarding this issue, the CI baseline data is not automatically updated when there are performance improvements. We have two proposals:
1. We can update the implementation in `check_perf_csv.py` so that if a model shows performance gains, the CI will also fail and suggest the PR owner update the baseline. This way, we can ensure the baseline is updated promptly whenever a PR brings performance improvements.
2. The second proposal is to establish a post-processing mechanism for updating baseline data. This mechanism would periodically monitor whether the baseline needs to be updated and perform the updates accordingly.

We’d greatly appreciate any suggestions or feedback you might have on the above proposals.

I have been thinking about what the preferred mechanism should be. For now, I think testing both sides of the bounds is the easiest to do and good. I also think that for the CPU tests specifically, we shouldn't clamp against the speedup number, as this subjects us to noise from both eager and compile run; instead, we should just record the absolute latency number.

The main risk from doing this is if the number slowly drifts over time we will be flaky again. A fancy longer term mechanism would be to have some process performing a weighted average over time, and then proposing updates as a benchmark slowly shifts faster/slower. I don't have any good idea for how to solve the death by thousand cuts situation, but a periodic mechanism to recenter the number should prevent us from becoming flaky again if we slowly creep to the threshold again.
For the speedup, do we measure wall clock time or number of CPU instructions issued? IMO switching to latter would help get rid of flakiness, wouldn't it?
When you look at the graph, it's already pretty tight, it's not obvious to me that CPU instructions will make it tighter

> CUDAGraph issue `Error: accessing tensor output of CUDAGraphs that has been overwritten by a subsequent run.` when compiling each layer vs compiling full model.

I have a smaller repro.

Example 1 (error when compiling each layer):

```python
import torch

def f(x):
    return x + 1

def g(x):
    return x + 2

f = torch.compile(f, mode="reduce-overhead")
g = torch.compile(g, mode="reduce-overhead")

for _ in range(3):
    # torch.compiler.cudagraph_mark_step_begin() # uncomment to fix the error
    x = torch.randn(10, device="cuda")
    y = f(x)
    z = g(y) # "Error: accessing tensor output of CUDAGraphs that has been overwritten by a subsequent run"

new_id = torch._inductor.cudagraph_trees.get_manager(0).new_graph_id()

print(new_id)
# print: GraphID(id=2)
```

Example 2 (succeed when compiling full model):

```python
import torch

def f(x):
    return x + 1

def g(x):
    return x + 2

def h(x):
    y = f(x)
    z = g(y)
    return z

h = torch.compile(h, mode="reduce-overhead")

for _ in range(3):
    # no need for torch.compiler.cudagraph_mark_step_begin() here
    x = torch.randn(10, device="cuda")
    z = h(x)

new_id = torch._inductor.cudagraph_trees.get_manager(0).new_graph_id()

print(new_id)
# print: GraphID(id=1)
```

Why compiling full model is fine but compiling each layer is bad? Since cg tree needs to decide whether it's 1) `root->f->g`, or 2)
```
root -> f
root -> g
```
By default, cg tree assumes 2). So when we start to execute `g`, it cleans all output memory of `f` to reuse the memory. Then, `g` accesses f's output and errors.

`torch.compiler.cudagraph_mark_step_begin()` gives extra information to compiler that it's 1). So when executing `g`, output memory of `f` is still kept and the error does not happen.

When compiling the full model, we only have 1 function `h` so it's fine.

Question: Would `torch.compiler.cudagraph_mark_step_begin()` fix the issue?

As we discussed, we may have a config to clone CUDAGraph outputs for avoiding the CUDAGraph issue. In the layerwise compilation setting, the cloned outputs is no longer a static tensor address and requires clone again as the inputs to the next layer. I.e.,

x -> f's cg -> static_address_y_as_output -> (clone) -> eager_y -> (clone) -> static_address_y_as_input -> g's cg.

not set `torch._dynamo.config.capture_dynamic_output_shape_ops = True` and remove these lines solves the problem: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/f8c0a4bd28087b02958b92d7b4f41ebc607292b7/torch/nn/modules/_functions.py#L87
```
        if not (torch.cuda.is_available() and torch.cuda.is_current_stream_capturing()):
            # The lines below force a synchronization between CUDA and CPU, because
            # the shape of the result count_all depends on the values in mask tensor.
            # Such synchronizations break CUDA Graph capturing.
            # See https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/78549
            # FIXME: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/78656 describes
            # a better longer-term solution.

            # remove stats from empty inputs
            mask = count_all.squeeze(-1) >= 1
            count_all = count_all[mask]
            mean_all = mean_all[mask]
            invstd_all = invstd_all[mask]
```

156716.body: What is the use case for putting a `@contextlib.contextmanager` decorator on forward?  Won't this break `Module.__call__`?

Actually, the issue here seems to be a bug in your code `@contextlib.contextmanager` expects to be put around a generator, not a function.  So:
```py
return x
```
needs to be
```py
yield x
```

Though turning forward() into a context manager sounds like nonsense to me.

Is this issue coming from some sort of fuzz tester?

Thank you for the suggestion. You are absolutely right: changing ``return x`` to ``yield x`` resolves the issue.
Apologies for the noise - this test case was indeed generated by our fuzz tester. We'll adjust it to avoid creating invalid patterns like this.

156786.body:
This may be caused by the reinplace optimization. Under inductor, index_copy is lowered to index_put, and the reinplace.py further optimizes it into index_put_. However, the reinplace logic does not check whether the source and the destination tensors are the same, leading to an incorrect in-place transformation. As a result, this causes a read-write aliasing issue. If we add a check to skip such cases, the op remains as index_put, which internally performs a clone(), and the result remains consistent. Please feel free to correct me if I'm mistaken.
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/178515d0ff6833c8e9221482b2a650ab31e00019/torch/_inductor/fx_passes/reinplace.py#L342-L350
Just curious, only the CPP backend uses `reinplace optimization`? Because Triton backend looks good in this case?

156819.body: how do i fix can you give any idea or solution to solve  this issue

@zou3519 do we have an owner / timeline for this issue? 
I don't see a compile tag in the triage. Do we have clarity on dynamo vs. inductor component fix needs?

Here's the approach for how to solve this issue. There are two cases: HOPs can either have a subgraph as input (that is also a torch.fx.GraphModule), or not have a subgraph as input.

The fix is something like:
1. [pytorch/torch/_inductor/fx_utils.py](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/029e2b05c225588098d3eba445fd04189691f77d/torch/_inductor/fx_utils.py#L143-L152) should_process_node should return True for everything. This may lead to some fallout in existing tests, look at the fallout and see if anything needs to be fixed.
2. If the HOP does not take a subgraph as an input (as is the case with auto_functionalized), then I don't think we need to do anything special.
3. If the HOP takes a subgraph as an input: we need to add some sort of incremental update logic to the subgraph (today, there is not one). One design for the incremental update logic is
    - incremental_update should recursively incrementally update subgraphs
    - hash_node should be recursive (if the node has a subgraph as input, it should also include whatever the hash of all of the nodes in the subgraph is...)
    - The goal is that we should both (a) fake-tensor-update nodes inside a subgraph and (b) fake-tensor-update the HOP's node.
    - The HOP to test out here is probably invoke_subgraph.

Add some unittests for cases (2) and (3). These unittests should look like:
- construct a torch.fx.GraphModule somehow
- apply FakeTensorUpdater
- run a pass that replaces a node with another one
- apply FakeTensorUpdater
- check that the node actually got updated.

Part of the difficult of this task is coming up with the repro/unittests for this. I would recommend starting with those first. Also, I'll come back here and add a repro for one of the cases to bootstrap this.

> This may be an opportunity for me to correct a misunderstanding, but wouldn't doing this implicitly assume that a subgraph would only ever be called with the same size tensors within a given graph? 

Yes. This is true for the graph as well: we assume the graph will ever be called with the same size tensors, modulo symbolic sizes.

@choijon5 I just came back from holiday break; I believe I had one final change that needed to be made to the PR to make it mergeable. I'll have that pushed this afternoon.

156882.body:
My understanding is:

The reason why we need to set max_seq_len to be < 127 is because of this line: [`self.mask[:seqlen, :seqlen]`](https://github.com/pytorch/executorch/blob/f673a4b1692f98f73471cf708d27e078701f33a9/examples/models/llama/attention.py#L450). self.mask has shape [128, 128] and stride [128, 1]. 
When slicing, we check if the slice is contiguous. This can only be true if `seqlen == 128`. Since we specified `seqlen` to be a dynamic value between [2, 128], it could be either contiguous or not, so we specialize on the [not contiguous](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/7709ff55123dbdcaaa44004738fb767324f05dbd/torch/_prims_common/__init__.py#L286-L289) path. cc @laithsakka @pianpwk please correct me if I'm wrong

This doesn't happen in the `use_kv_cache=True` case because the 2nd dimension of the attn_mask is always kept at 128 and never sliced into via the dynamic seqlen.

157217.body: Is there any way to get Dynamo into the `except` regardless of the specific `Error`?

Currently, `torch.compile` doesn't handle ops that throw errors (except by falling back to eager for them).  Though it does not look like it is falling back properly here.

As a workaround you could move `torch.compile()` inside the try/except.

This issue helped me with `torch.compile()` dynamo problem with too old `torch` and newer `transformers` libs. 

The function `is_torchdynamo_compiling()` in `tranformers.utils.import_utils.py` relies on `torch.compiler.is_compiling()`, which is not introduced in torch==2.2.2, for example.  The workaround in the official code is using try+except. However, it can not work with `torch.compile()` according to this issue. 

My solution is defining `is_compiling()` for older versions of torch in my code, before using `my_model=torch.compile(my_model)`:
```
try: 
    torch.compiler.is_compiling()
except Exception:
    torch.compiler.is_compiling = torch._dynamo.is_compiling
```

157240.body: @davidberard98 did it use to work fine in 2.7? Or is this a new functionality that we want to have in 2.8?

@malfet TMA API has changed in Triton (the experimental API available in Triton 3.3 has been removed and a new API with the same functionality but different interface has been introduced in Triton 3.4).

This means that:
* The old/experimental API works fine in PyTorch 2.7 / Triton 3.3
* Triton users will need to migrate to the new API in Triton 3.4. If they migrate to the new API without this PR, it can lead to IMA. 

**regarding cherry-pick eligibility**, there's another regression fixed by https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/155896: **without this change, some PT2 matmul configs generated with max_autotune will now IMA in AOTI**, which is a regression compared to PyTorch 2.7. If you need a concrete failing test that exhibits this failure, LMK and I can find a command.
The PR; https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/155896 was cherry-picked, hence closing this issue

157248.body:
Thanks for the report.  The bug here was actually that the eager kernel in PyTorch handles stride==0 tensors incorrectly, and TorchInductor maps `unsqueeze` to stride=0 (while eager does not).

Fixed in #157326

157363.body:
I can reproduce this issue using a latest pytorch nightly. Instructions to reproduce:

1. Install https://github.com/pytorch/extension-cpp. This is `git checkout <repo>` and then `python setup.py develop`
2. Run the script above.

The allclose fails in that the compiled model output is all zeros.

Looking at the custom operator, it looks like it does support CUDAGraphs: https://github.com/pytorch/extension-cpp/blob/master/extension_cpp/csrc/cuda/muladd.cu
@zou3519 do you want to take this one?

Not sure if it helps, but i have tried to debug it and ran into some interesting behaviors that could help with pinpointing the problem: 
- The behavior I see is that for Linear + custom, the resulting tensor has first $N$ elements correct, then they are $0$. $N$ has small variation across runs and is larger for more performant GPU (NVIDIA 2080 has ~3000, NVIDIA 4090 has over 4000). 
- Adding `cudaDeviceSynchronize` before the custom kernel launch solves the problem for 1st run, but if you try to run the model again, it breaks with different error. 
- Chaining only custom layers seems to work fine.

It feels like some synchronization issue, like the dependency between inputs and outputs was incorrectly evaluated. I suspected that the custom kernels are scheduled on a different stream or something, but running it inside the same stream didn't help.
At last, the problem is in the tutorial code, not in torch. It does not set the stream for the kernel `at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream()`.
thanks for getting to the bottom of this @vlejd . I've put up a PR with the fix and will merge it in a bit

I think some change in https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/pull/37866 caused the sdpa mask to be changed to the vmap version by default, which causes the exportability regression. Will work with the transformers team offline to resolve this!

Looks like the workaround is to use `export_with_dynamic_cache`, which switches the sdpa version to use the non-vmap one under the hood:

This should work now:
```python
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, DynamicCache

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf", attn_implementation="sdpa")
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf")

inputs = tokenizer(text="My name is Llama", return_tensors="pt")

from transformers.integrations.executorch import export_with_dynamic_cache
ep = export_with_dynamic_cache(model, inputs['input_ids'])
```
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/154650 could unblock the sdpa vmap issue. Or if we directly returned post dispatch IR using `torch.export._trace._export(.., pre_dispatch: bool = False)`

I previously found that for ONNX export purposes the executorch wrapper registers the cache tensors as buffers and causes them to show up as initializers in the ONNX model, not graph inputs. More detail at https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/155862#issuecomment-2971974039

157417.body: This is not a regression from PyTorch 2.7. But still important because it is einops.
I'm confused as to what is going on, because it sounds like einops expects the TypeError? https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/f8c0a4bd28087b02958b92d7b4f41ebc607292b7/torch/__init__.py#L612-L617
@ezyang you added the TODO, do you have more context behind this?

All the context I have is that originally when I wanted to force specialization this broke einops because einops actually did have dynamic shapes generic code... you just have to raise a TypeError when you hash so that it bypasses the cache. Looks like the TypeError isn't being caught here; post the full backtrace?

Edit 2: 
The first version of fix it in draft PR. Works for 0.6.1 now, too

Edit 1:
Actually, the monkeypatch fix works only for [V0.6.2rc0](https://github.com/arogozhnikov/einops/releases/tag/v0.6.2rc0) and [V0.7.0rc1](https://github.com/arogozhnikov/einops/releases/tag/v0.7.0rc1). In 0.6.1 there's `_prepare_transformation_recipe` with `lru_cache` used in `einops.reduce`, so `SymInt.__hash__` is called anyway. I still work on the proper fix

@zou3519 The TypeError is thrown by `__hash__` in SymInt when `not self.node.is_nested_int()`. `einops._apply_recipe` uses `_reconstruct_from_shape`, which is `_reconstruct_from_shape_uncached` wrapped with `functools.lru_cache(1024)`. `lru_cache` uses `__hash__` to check hit/miss. When it calls `__hash__` on SymInt, the TypeError is thrown

 `_apply_recipe` >= 0.7.0rc2 catches TypeError and fallbacks to `_reconstruct_from_shape_uncached`. They assume that  TypeError probably comes from unhashable value (like our SymInt), that's why einops >= 0.7.0rc2 work fine with pytorch

So how can we fix it - modify `SymInt.__hash__` to always return a valid hash or monkeypatch the einops to use `_reconstruct_from_shape_uncached` in place of `_reconstruct_from_shape` when einops version is < 0.7.0rc2

Returning a hash for SymInts is tricky, because the hash value can't be stored on a heap - it's checked by asIntArrayRefSlow https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/9e792f583afae92ec8ddedac1660bd79991d1f4f/c10/core/SymIntArrayRef.h#L41 in `view_symint` (I don't know how to link to source, because it's in build/ directory - build/aten/src/ATen/RegisterCPU_3.cpp). So as @ezyang wrote in the comment in code, we can't just specialize with `return hash(builtins.int(self))`. It's a rabbit hole a bit, because to not have an int on the heap, we can forcefully clamp it to range [-5, 256] (small ints are cached; we need to also ensure it's always taken from cache, and it turns out that's not obvious, try it yourself, as long as you use literal `return

FYI pull request https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/155310 might fix this. It forces Dynamo to trace through the einops code instead of putting its ops in the graph. The blocker was a recompilation issue (https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/171488) caused by a global dict update, but PyTorch can work around it by initializing einops before torch.compile runs.

157569.body: the feature had originally landed in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/106211

I believe the bug is due to 

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/e3fe001d9e38153a7797ad25377fdfa34e461113/torch/_numpy/_ndarray.py#L471

This line of code assumes that numpy and torch advanced indexing are equivalent. But they are not, notably in the case where advanced indices are separated by a slice (see https://numpy.org/devdocs/user/basics.indexing.html#combining-advanced-and-basic-indexing).

> This line of code assumes that numpy and torch advanced indexing are equivalent. But they are not, notably in the case where advanced indices are separated by a slice

So an instance of gh-141563 (bug report by author of the tweet linked in the issue description)? The `torch.compile` difference seems like the wrong thing to focus on; I believe PyTorch and NumPy advanced indexing were always meant to match. Or error out in real corner cases, but not intentionally mismatch.

There are a few other open issues related to advanced indexing, e.g. gh-71673. They're going to show up in a similar way as descrepancies between `numpy` and `torch.compile` on numpy-using functions. I think it's a matter of prioritizing fixes to advanced indexing logic.

@ezyang can probably confirm the intent here of matching to NumPy, since he tried hard to do so a couple of years ago (see, e.g., https://dev-discuss.pytorch.org/t/how-does-advanced-indexing-work-when-i-combine-a-tensor-and-a-single-index/558).

To be honest, the numpy behavior is quite unintuitive. I much prefer the one of torch. If i remember correctly, there was a proposition to modify numpy's behavior.

See https://x.com/giffmana/status/1937580049689194807

Until reading through the linked issues here, I hadn't realized how porous our attempt at matching Numpy's advanced indexing semantics were. The bug I spent time with @nkaretnikov  on would have been merely making something that currently errored into something that didn't, surely non BC breaking (though if we had ever finished the patch, perhaps a test would have informed us that it was wrong.) The ellipses bug, OTOH, would be clearly BC breaking and it probably would have been very difficult to change the behavior of. So I am somewhat sympathetic to assertions that we need to faithfully implement the Numpy advanced indexing semantics for the translation layer.

In fact, it's not clear to me that OP's but is even the same as the other two linked ones. I will have to reason through it carefully...

Ok so OP's case is I think the outer vs vectorized indexing confusion described in https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0021-advanced-indexing.html which this NEP proposes to introduce new oindex/vindex indexing schemes to disambiguate between the two. I /think/ but have not verified that PyTorch defaults to something like oindex semantics on basic advanced indexing. If this is correct, we are definitely not changing the behavior, and we need to fix the translation of advanced indexing in the compile translator.
i've assigned the issue to myself. i'll fix the translation layer

I have read and listened to the podcast you shared, it mentioned that graph generated for `batch size 0` or `batch size 1` might not be valid for `batch size >= 2`. Does relaxing of the constraints means that `0/1 specialization` issue has been solved and moving forward the graphs are stable for all batch sizes?

Just to check, I have compared the output of PyTorch model and ONNX model across batch size of 1 to 10 using this:
```python
for batch_count in range(1,11):

    example_input = torch.randn(batch_count, 3, 224, 224)

    onnx_output = onnx_session.run(["output"], {"input": example_input.numpy()})[0]


    print(f"Output is close for batch size {batch_count}: {np.allclose(pytorch_output, onnx_output, rtol=1e-3, atol=1e-5)}")

Hi - for a simple model like ResNet50 this is expected to work. We are relaxing the constraint in this up coming release (2.8). It doesn't necessarily mean the 0/1 issue is resolved. It just means we are no longer specializing the dimensions that are declared to be dynamic. It PyTorch cannot guarantee that the model is sound over all possible dimension sizes it will raise an error instead (of specializing the dim).
I see, thanks for your help!

Hi @xuhancn , the `_is_msvc_cl` in `check_msvc_cl_language_id` can crash because of locale. A user reported a crash with stack trace: https://github.com/woct0rdho/triton-windows/issues/191

The interesting thing is that their `locale.getpreferredencoding()` returns `utf-8`, which fails to decode MSVC's output. I guess it's because they have set 'Beta: Use Unicode UTF-8 for worldwide language support' in Windows.

I've did some tests with Windows 11 build 26200.7840, Python 3.14.3, and [Python UTF-8 Mode](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#python-utf-8-mode) is disabled:
* When the beta UTF-8 support in Windows is disabled, `locale.getlocale(), getencoding(), getpreferredencoding()` return a codec like cp936 rather than `utf-8`, and the `oem` codec maps to cp936.
* When the beta UTF-8 support in Windows is enabled, `locale.getlocale(), getencoding(), getpreferredencoding()` return `utf-8` or `cp65001` (an alias of `utf-8`), and the `oem` codec maps to `cp1` (also an alias of `utf-8`).

I cannot reproduce the crash as of MSVC 19.50.35724 . It may happen with some older version of MSVC. So we can say it's not strictly an issue in PyTorch, but more like an issue that the output from the old MSVC does not fully work with the beta UTF-8 support.

If we want to work around this in PyTorch, we can add `errors="surrogateescape"` to `SUBPROCESS_DECODE_ARGS`. Then when an encoding mismatch happens, the output is not directly readable, but strings like 'Microsoft' can still match, and we can recover the original encoding from the user's output when we need debugging.

157792.body: I remember @bobrenjc93 @laithsakka running into a similar problem with dynamic shape and caching (SymInts were getting different names in the graph from run to run, causing unnecessary cache misses). IIRC, they also added a pass to normalize SymInt names for that case.

Repro:
- patch in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/157803/files#diff-03de9d44b47c56dedd2bca3c1b0b21a08c93df662579165feafd49c5226fd83dR1857
- run that test
- note that AOTAutograd cache misses 3 times (it should cache hit on the last two)
For dynamic shapes what we end up doing is that when we pick the symbol names we pick them such that same code locations always get the same name.

> Repro:
> 
> * patch in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/157803/files#diff-03de9d44b47c56dedd2bca3c1b0b21a08c93df662579165feafd49c5226fd83dR1857
> * run that test
> * note that AOTAutograd cache misses 3 times (it should cache hit on the last two)

here is a TL-parse for the repo [internal link]
https://manifold.edge.x2p.facebook.net/v0/read/tree/logs/lsakka/bb96d84f-144b-45a7-ad53-db3f62f0f0dd/custom/index.html?bucketName=tlparse_reports&apiKey=tlparse_reports-key&withPayload=1&timeoutMsec=10000

Although the issue bisects to #152661 it actually is more complicated than that: 
1. #152661 just fixes the cache - the cache key was incorrect before. If I back out #152661 and then remove the cache entirely then the problem still happens (so it's an existing problem that the broken cache was masking)
2. If I back out #152661 on main then the problem still happens because of something that changed in #153432. So backing it out is not easily feasable at this point anyway because of other changes after it.
3. Because #153432 isn't easily backed (lots of conflicts) it I couldn't tell if backing them both out was possible.

It may be worth checking out just before #152661, disabling the cache and trying to fix the error to see what the underlying issue is.

HI @aorenste @zou3519 and @malfet According to First point in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/157921#issuecomment-3055231416 this is pre-existing issue in 2.7 Should we de-milestone it from release 2.8 ?
@atalman Not sure what do you mean, I can clearly see that snippet above compiles fine in 2.7.1 and fails in 2.8.0

The problem here is that as far as I can tell this repro SHOULD fail:
It's using `mark_dynamic(x, 0)` which says that it wants `x.size(0)` to be dynamic. But then it specializes on `x.size(0)` (because it's a parameter to `torch.narrow`). The definition of `mark_dynamic()` is that if you specialize to a single value it will fail. If you're okay with it being specialized then you're supposed to use `maybe_mark_dynamic()`.

So 2.7 was definitely the incorrect behavior for this repro and we're now doing the documented correct behavior.

@aorenste can you point to a line of documentation that says it's an invalid behavior?
The public document I can find is https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/torch.compiler_dynamic_shapes.html, which says
>  If you know ahead of time something will be dynamic, you can skip the first recompile with `torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic(tensor, dim)`. If you know ahead of time the min and max value this dimension can take, you can specify `torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic(tensor, dim, min=min, max=max)`

I.e. user tells that x shape is dynamic, and I'm not sure why 2.8 compiler says that code above specializes it to be constant...

Hmm, marking dimension of emebed as dynamic makes code compilable on 2.7 and 2.8 and actually produces the constrains I've expected to have when only one dim were marked as dynamic
```python
import torch

embed = torch.randn(2, 8192)
x = torch.zeros(8192)

torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic(x, 0)
torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic(embed, 1)

@torch.compile(backend="aot_eager", fullgraph=True)
def f(embedding_indices, x):
    added_tokens_mask = torch.where(x > 10000, 1, 0)
    ei = torch.narrow(embedding_indices, 1, 0, x.size(0))
    return ei.clone()

f(embed, x)
```

@zou3519 should this just be changed in the vLLM code? But PR that introduces the check should be marked as BC-breaking (imo)

@malfet I guess I don't see it in the docs - but it's definitely expected behavior - for example: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/102814

The error message itself says what to do:
```
  - You marked L['x'].size()[0] as dynamic but your code specialized it to be a
    constant (8192). If you're using mark_dynamic, either remove it or use
    maybe_mark_dynamic. If you're using Dim.DYNAMIC, replace it with either
    Dim.STATIC or Dim.AUTO.
```

> user tells that x shape is dynamic, and I'm not sure why 2.8 compiler says that code above specializes it to be constant

I believe using `x.size(0)` as a parameter to `torch.narrow` is what is specializing it. Rather than something like `x > 10000` where it's a  range, using it as a parameter makes it an exact value (I'm guessing - @laithsakka would know for sure).

But the change in behavior between 2.7 and 2.8 here isn't in the dynamic behavior - that didn't change. The problem is that in 2.7 the cache for evaluating dynamic shapes doesn't include the "suppress guards" flag in the key even though it uses it when computing the value - and in some cases (in this particular case) the code calls it twice - once w/ "suppress guards=True" as a test and then again w/ "suppress guards=False" when it is actually building the fx graph. The problem is that because the first call had "suppress guards=True" it never built the guards and the second call is just returning the cached value and never building the guards that the resulting graph is actually relying on. And the cache is just a 128 entry cache - so the behavior can be nondeterministic depending on how much stuff you did between the two checks.

We should include in the bc-breaking section anything we expect is going to break legitimate usage.
As vLLM showed, this one is a legitimate usage. So we should definitely put it there.

@laithsakka @aorenste could one of you add a BC-breaking note to the release notes please? Then we can close out this issue.   Should coordinate with @jbschlosser who is leading the release notes process (I don't know what stage we're in)

157973.body: @jansel Created a PR to fix this issue

@jansel @Michaelgathara if I understand this correctly, this means that all (current) versions of PyTorch are incompatible with NumPy v2.x, correct? But given PR https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/158036, the next release of PyTorch should be NumPy v2.0 compatible?

> [@jansel](https://github.com/jansel) [@Michaelgathara](https://github.com/Michaelgathara) if I understand this correctly, this means that all (current) versions of PyTorch are incompatible with NumPy v2.x, correct? But given PR [#158036](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/158036), the next release of PyTorch should be NumPy v2.0 compatible?

Not quite, the PR [#158036](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/158036) is to ensure that `numpy.bool_` is utilized within PyTorch correctly. PyTorch was trying to treat the boolean as an integer rather than as a boolean. This is one of the things that NumPy v2.x introduced that wasn't quite supported on PyTorch, but everything else I have tried thus far works. If you find things that don't work using NumPy v2.x feel free to @ me on the issue and I'd be happy to see if it's something I can tackle

OK, so this class of problems is tricky.

What's supposed to happen when we raise a GuardOnDataDependentSymNode is that we're supposed to instead graph break at that point in time and then let Dynamo take care of stepping across the DDE. However, here, the DDE doesn't happen during Dynamo, but instead at a later point in the compile pipeline (in this case, AOTAutograd). At this point, there is no way to easily trigger a graph break, since Dynamo is all done by this point.

The *simplest* way to solve this problem is to trigger a restart when this happens. On the restart, we should rerun Dynamo with `capture_scalar_outputs = False`. We must be careful not to trigger this restart in export-like flows. A more clever strategy would be to directly "graph break" in the same way normal graph breaks work (they also trigger a restart to go up to the point where graph break is possible) but you'd have to reconstruct the log so we properly stopped at the right spot, which might not be so simple.
BTW, this is not exclusive to AOTAutograd; Inductor can also trigger this.

158120.body: Larger scope than this issue, but is there interest in cleaning up/enforcing dependency structure, using tools like import-linter?
Import linting would definitely be wise - speaking of QA, and definitely within the scope of this issue, test cases for torch export on different devices would also catch this.

I agree. If a maintainer gives the go ahead,  I will give import linting a go. This task would be pretty involved and require some design decisions, so I would like to get the community input before putting in the effort.
What import linter would we use? I am not opposed in principle but the details matter.

Pyright can flag circular imports by specifying the reportImportCycles field. For more targeted tool that also supports more specific import rules, there are things like import-linter, https://github.com/seddonym/import-linter.

Unfortunately, a purely circular import only checker will probably light up the codebase. I'm not sure if I would ask people to maintain no circular imports on a permanent basis... maybe it's good? Unclear.

import-linter looks more promising. @albanD maybe you have some rules in mind!
I think https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/158931 fixes this particular failure, but let's keep the issue open for purpose of discussing the import linter :)

The closest to this we have is https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/8e07c9870d07c5a318ab21bb16b3fa27576851e6/test/test_public_bindings.py#L271 that imports every submodule one after the other.
but it will fail to capture two cases that can happen here:
- when a direct import breaks only if "import torch" was not done before (the case for some submodules of dynamo/functorch today)
- imports happening inside functions (also happens quite a bit in PT2 codebase as they do in-function imports heavily to avoid circular import issues).

The problem is that spawning a new process to do the import (and thus catch 1) is way too slow (we have 2k+ submodules and launching 2k processes is quite slow!). Maybe with some good engineering and forkserver we can make it happen?

It won't help with the in-function imports though which is the case of this issue. For this one, I am not sure what kind of testing we can do beyond smoke testing some specific APIs from a fresh process (instead of in the middle of the test suite)?
we agreed to closed this on as fixed by https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/158931 during triage

158131.body:
The issue is what the warning is saying 
```
[W913 21:35:09.698622612 RNN.cpp:1479] Warning: RNN module weights are not part of single contiguous chunk of memory. This means they need to be compacted at every call, possibly greatly increasing memory usage. To compact weights again call flatten_parameters(). (function _cudnn_rnn)
eager success
[W913 21:35:09.106144962 RNN.cpp:1479] Warning: RNN module weights are not part of single contiguous chunk of memory. This means they need to be compacted at every call, possibly greatly increasing memory usage. To compact weights again call flatten_parameters(). (function _cudnn_rnn)
```

RNN doesn't use a contiguous chunk, so RNN kernel calls also have the nature.
So the solution is just to follow the advice, create a RNN module first, then call flatten_parameters() to make the memory layout consistent.
I see, closing this issue until we can find a better reproduction.

# Issue 158212: Wrong gradients with compiled flex attention

## Summary
Compiled `flex_attention` produces incorrect forward outputs and gradients vs eager
when mask function uses floor division with negative numerators.

## Environment
- PyTorch: 2.12.0a0+git178144e (built from source)
- Python: 3.12.11
- GPU: NVIDIA H100 (SM90, 132 SMs)
- Conda env: dev
- Triton: bundled with PyTorch

## Root Cause: Triton compiler bug in nested tl.where broadcast store

The inductor's `floordiv` codegen emits:
```python
tl.where((a < 0) != (b < 0),
    tl.where(a % b != 0, a // b - 1, a // b),
    a // b)
```

This correction is semantically correct (converts C-style truncation division to Python
floor division). It computes the right values when stored to a matching-shape output.
But when the result has shape `[1, N]` (N >= 16) and gets **broadcast-stored** to a
`[N, N]` output, the values are corrupted.

Specifically, for `(-1 - 0) // 2`:
- Stored to `[1, N]`: correct value `-1`
- Stored to `[N, N]` via broadcast: wrong value `-2`

The `[N, 1]` direction is unaffected — only `[1, N]` triggers the bug.

This is a Triton 3.6.0 compiler bug on H100 (SM90). The nested `tl.where` pattern
interacts badly with the broadcast store codegen at N >= 16, likely due to
vectorization or register allocation optimizations.

## Evidence Log

### Step 1: Reproduced minimal repro
- `repro_158212.py`: Confirms forward error of 3.5 and grad errors in millions
- Bug confirmed with offset=2, LEN=64, w=2

### Step 2: Verified floordiv codegen is correct in generated Triton kernel
- The generated kernel uses the `tl.where` correction pattern at lines 487/490
- `repro_158212_triton_check.py`: Triton `//` IS truncation (not floor) — confirmed
- `repro_158212_triton_modulo.py`: The correction pattern works for 1D tensors of size 16

### Step 3: Isolated the trigger condition
- `repro_158212_fwd_only.py`: Bug is forward-only (no backward needed)
- `repro_1582

158414.body: Is the custom op libtorch_tp_jit.jit_conv_forward.default a c++ custom op? Or is it registered via the torch.library.custom_op api?

We fakify script object for safety reasons (e.g. to avoid  side effects produced by the real script objects) in torch.compile and export. But the fakification logic works only in Python so it can only works with custom ops that are registered via the python custom op registration APIs.

There are two ways to fix it:
- Write the custom op with torch.library.custom and the register_fake api to register a python fake implementation for the custom op.
- Change the torchbind object to allow tracing with real mode following the example [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/test/cpp/jit/test_custom_class_registrations.cpp#L254-L256).

The extension is written in C++, and registers via the C++ registration process. below are the c++ bindings. 

https://github.com/PASSIONLab/OpenEquivariance/blob/f76b21e31d8b701131c565eb4f8f4a334dfa4c23/openequivariance/extension/libtorch_tp_jit.cpp#L340

Regarding the two potential fixes. 

> Write the custom op with torch.library.custom and the register_fake api to register a python fake implementation for the custom op.

We usually register a fake implementation via python implementation and it works ( except in this situation where it's being composed with autocast and jitscript). It composes with either autocast or jitscript, but the combination causes the errors. 

https://github.com/PASSIONLab/OpenEquivariance/blob/f76b21e31d8b701131c565eb4f8f4a334dfa4c23/openequivariance/implementations/convolution/LoopUnrollConv.py#L243

What would it mean to "write the custom op with torch.library.custom" would we have to import the C++ implementation as an opaque op and call it from python? Would that have negative effects on our ability to compose with other pytorch subsystems? Like AOTI? 

> Change the torchbind object to allow tracing with real mode following the example [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/test/cpp/jit/test_custom_class_registrations.cpp#L254-L256).

This is interesting, thanks for sharing. Immediately making this change returns new errors about not returning SymInts, but the idea would be that we would rewrite the class / function to return objects with symbolic sizes somehow. 

Do you have some advice as to which of the solutions is more common and will have better support? I'm guessing the first one?

yeah, the custom op + fakification approach has better support.

1. f1 uses `cross_entropy` at top-level, resulted in skipping.
```
skipping: cross_entropy (reason: in skipfiles, file: /home/ryanguo99/repos/pytorch/torch/nn/functional.py)
```

```
def forward(self, L_target_: "i64[8192][1]cuda:0", L_x_: "bf16[8192, 16384][16384, 1]cuda:0"):

    l_x_ = L_x_

```

158566.body:
Thanks for reporting this. We recommend using **torch._higher_order_ops.scan** . This is the user-facing API, which does safety checks, e.g. aliasing/mutation checks to avoid silent incorrectness, also lift closures as explicit argument. So re-writing the model with **torch._higher_order_ops.scan** :
```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

torch._dynamo.config.capture_scalar_outputs=True

class ScanModel(nn.Module):
    def forward(self, x):

        def loop_body(y_list, iv_list):
            y = y_list[0] # CHANGE: y_list and iv_list are pytrees
            iv = iv_list[0]
            i = iv.item() # i is data-dependent, by default its range is unspecified.
            #  CHANGE: so use torch._check to tell the pytorch that it's safe to select
            torch._check(i >= 0)
            torch._check(i < y.size(0))
            torch._check(i < x.size(0))
            y_clone = y.clone() #  CHANGE: aliasing are unsafe, we need to clone the inputs
            y_clone[i] = x[i]
            return [y_clone, iv.clone()]

        y = torch.zeros(x.size(0))
        res = torch._higher_order_ops.scan(
            loop_body,
            [y],
            [torch.arange(x.size(0))],
        ) #  CHANGE: no need for the additional_inputs, which is handled automatically.
        return res

x = torch.randn(5)

model = ScanModel()
print("Eager output:", model(x))
print("Compiled output:", torch.compile(model)(x))
```
However, this gives a Data-dependent error that's under fixing  see [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/158467/files/180ae101b915dc6156644a827b9ab03d0f66ee59..6094c4c586aa930186b78dcc01463940cee1a507).

158873.body:
This is the desired behavior.

```
M1 = MyMod(16, 32)
M2 = MyMod(8, 64)

M1 = torch.compile(M1)
M2 = torch.compile(M2)
```

Because you are calling torch.compile on the same model with two different shapes, the second time torch.compile will trigger a recompilation. But you set `recompile_limit` to 1 and `fail_on_recompile_limit_hit` to True, so you will hit an error in this case.

@desertfire Thank you for the quick triage. I am surprised to see that compilation of a separate instance counts as a recompilation - and wanted to ask if there is a workaround to prevent this? If not - could one be added or the design changed?

I've tested `torch._dynamo.config.inline_inbuilt_nn_modules=True` based on `https://docs.pytorch.org/tutorials/recipes/regional_compilation.html` but it does not resolve the issue.

For example, it means regional compilation raises excessive warnings. These warnings pollute important warnings -  warnings that signify truly bad / unneeded recompiles. Excessive recompiles are a particularly painful problem - and they pop up quite easily when folks new to compile are writing model code - so it seems fairly important to have a way to quickly communicate bad recompiles without complaining about good recompiles.

```
import torch
print(torch.__version__)
torch._logging.set_logs(recompiles=True)
torch._dynamo.reset()
torch._dynamo.config.recompile_limit = 1
torch._dynamo.config.fail_on_recompile_limit_hit = True

class MyMod1(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, a, b, c):
        super().__init__()
        self.layer1 = torch.compile(torch.nn.Linear(a,b))
        self.uncompiled = torch.nn.ReLU()
        self.layer2 = torch.compile(torch.nn.Linear(b,c))

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.layer2(self.uncompiled(self.layer1(x)))

M1 = MyMod1(16, 32, 64)

x1 = torch.randn(512, 16)

M1(x1)
```

```
V0723 13:47:04.645000 893 torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3489] [0/1] [__recompiles] Recompiling function inner in /usr/local/lib/python3.11/dist-packages/torch/_dynamo/external_utils.py:68
V0723 13:47:04.645000 893 torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3489] [0/1] [__recompiles]     triggered by the following guard failure(s):
V0723 13:47:04.645000 893 torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3489] [0/1] [__recompiles]     - 0/0: tensor 'args[0]' requires_grad mismatch. expected requires_grad=0
W0723 13:47:04.646000 893 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1002] [0/1]

https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/torch.compiler_dynamic_shapes.html explains how dynamic shapes work in torch.compile. I don't know if there is an easy to report "good" recompile vs. "bad" recompile, cc @anijain2305

I think the philosophical dis-alignment is around the "re" in recompile. My initial impression was that since they're separate instances / models - each of which is only being compiled once - it should not count as a "re"-compile. I understand that my definition was wrong - and what torch means is merely that the compiler was run again - (speaking a bit inexactly) it considers any run of the compiler a "re"-compile - even if the compilation work is for separate entities. 

Perhaps the wish here is for an instance specific concept of "re" compile - perhaps a "duplicate" compile, or "second compile"  - one where the tracking is specific to each instance, or perhaps to the class + initialization arguments (e.g. Linear(X, Y) would be different from Linear(A, B), but perhaps multiple Linear(X, Y) instance may share cached compile objects). 

The questions I want torch logs to answer are - are there any instances / models being compiled multiple times? How many times? Why? Are these for silly reasons (e.g. self.iter += 1) or necessary reasons.

I realize this goes beyond the scope of a bug report - but wanted to share as I understand many users struggle with unwanted recompilation and I think communicating them more clearly would be a huge boon to torch 2.0 usability.

I'm not sure what goes in the module case. But if you wrap the call to the module in different functions, and compile the functions instead of the modules, torch.compile will treat them as two distinct compiles:
```
import torch
print(torch.__version__)
torch._logging.set_logs(recompiles=True)
torch._dynamo.reset()
torch._dynamo.config.recompile_limit = 1
torch._dynamo.config.fail_on_recompile_limit_hit = True

class MyMod(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, a, b):
        super().__init__()
        self.linear = torch.nn.Linear(a, b)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.linear(x)


M1 = MyMod(16, 32)
M2 = MyMod(8, 64)

def f1(x):
    return M1(x)

def f2(x):
    return M2(x)

f1 = torch.compile(f1)
f2 = torch.compile(f2)

x1 = torch.randn(512, 16)
x2 = torch.randn(10, 8)

f1(x1)
f2(x2)
```

> what torch means is merely that the compiler was run again - (speaking a bit inexactly) it considers any run of the compiler a "re"-compile - even if the compilation work is for separate entities.

So under the hood `torch.compile` initiates the compilation at the code object level (either the function passed to `torch.compile`, or the `__call__` method of module if compile is applied to a module). That's why you observe these "recompilations" on different instantiations of the same module class. It's like there's a `map[code_object, list[compiled_artifact]]`, and recompilation means we add a new artifact for some code object.

This is also why wrapping the call into a function prevent the "recompilation", because there you have 2 distinct code object, it's separate compilations, rather than re-compiling for the same code object. But note that this makes compiler unable to detect and reuse compiled artifact when possible (say you have 2 instances of the same module class, with the same configs, so they are effectively equal).

We are also working on a programming model document that covers recompilation, and hopefully it'll bring some clarity to this.

159041.body: `torch.set_default_device("cuda:0")` didn't seem to control `torch.tensor([1000], dtype=torch.int64` inside of `fn`.

I ran into a similar case which runs smoothly in eager mode but fails with compile.

```python
@torch.compile(backend="eager")
def foo(x):
  y = torch.arange(0, 10)
  x = x * y
  return x

# torch.set_default_device("cuda")
with torch.device("cuda"):
  print(torch.overrides._get_current_function_mode_stack())
  y = foo(torch.randn(1, 10))

print(y)
```

I guess the problem has some relation with a previous issue: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/151202.

I looked into [convert_frame.py](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/e7cc42df58a86bee05944f6e80c535aa1d099443/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py#L298) and I found something interesting.

When performing transform, the `torch_function_mode_stack_state_mgr` cleared the whole torch function stack. As a result, `DeviceContext` , which is also a torch function mode, will not take effect during frame conversion.

```python
class TorchFunctionModeStackStateManager:
    def __init__(self):
        self.stack = []

    def __enter__(self):
        self.stack = torch.overrides._get_current_function_mode_stack()
        clear_torch_function_mode_stack() # !! **Here, DeviceContext outside compilation region get unrespected**

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, traceback):
        set_torch_function_mode_stack(self.stack)
        self.stack = []

    @contextlib.contextmanager
    def temp_restore_stack(self):
        prev = torch.overrides._get_current_function_mode_stack()
        set_torch_function_mode_stack(self.stack)
        try:
            yield
        finally:
            set_torch_function_mode_stack(prev)


torch_function_mode_stack_state_mgr = TorchFunctionModeStackStateManager() 

```

To further verify my guess, I tweak some code in `torch/_dynamo/utils.py`, restoring the torch function mode stack in `run_node()`:

```
def run_node(tracer, node, args, kwargs, nnmodule):
  op = node.op
  from .variables.torch_function import torch_function_mode_stack_state_mgr
  with set_current_node(node), torch_function_mode_stack_state_mgr.temp_restore_stack():
    ...
```

And then the example run successfully.

But I guess this is not the final solution, or it is not the expected way `torch_function_mode_stack_state_mgr` works. Can any one give some suggestion?

During compilation, torch function tracing should provide the correct device to the node burned into the graph. Perhaps we are tracing a node directly in the graph without taking into account its kwargs? 

Clearing the stack before running the graph is the semantically correct thing to do here, because we have already traced all of the torch function logic into the graph itself, so we should not execute the modes again when executing the graph.

> I tried to reproduce on the nightly, but got a different error: > > /data/users/shangdiy/pytorch/torch/nn/modules/activation.py:1380:0: error: failed to legalize operation 'torch.aten.scaled_dot_product_attention' that was explicitly marked illegal I tried to reproduce on PyTorch 2.8.0 **Without model.eval()**, I get the following error in fx.export_and_import() call: -> error: failed to legalize operation '**torch.aten.scaled_dot_product_attention**' that was explicitly marked illegal **With model.eval()**, I get the following error in fx.export_and_import() call: -> error: failed to legalize operation '**torch.constant.int**' I also noticed that 'scaled_dot_product_attention' op comes directly before permute/view ops. So, this seems to be a different issue when importing an exported program from PyTorch 2.8.0, while the reported issue (permute/view and non-contiguous memory format) may still exist.

Update:
By modifying the code to use the default decomposition table instead of a custom table, as follows:
The problem will be resolved on PyTorch 2.8.0 (**with and without 'model.eval()'**) and **OutputType.TOSA**.
This means the original reported issue of "non-contiguous memory and the eliminated clone() op" seems to be resolved with an exported program in PyTorch 2.8.0 once 'torch.aten.scaled_dot_product_attention' op can be decomposed (https://github.com/llvm/torch-mlir/issues/4279).

```
from torch_mlir import fx
from torch_mlir.compiler_utils import OutputType

def importExportedModelToMlir(input_file: str, output_file: str, experimental_support_mutation : bool = True):

    prog = torch.export.load(input_file)
    prog = prog.run_decompositions() #modified

    mlir_str = fx.export_and_import(prog, output_type=OutputType.TOSA, 
                                    experimental_support_mutation=experimental_support_mutation)
    with open(output_file, "wt") as f:
        print(mlir_str, file=f)

importExportedModelToMlir("exported_model.pt2", "exported_model.mlir")
```

Note:
OutputType.TOSA -> works 
OutputType.TORCH -> works 
OutputType.RAW -> works 
OutputType.LINALG_ON_TENSORS -> same errors.

159139.body:
Hi , I've been looking into this issue and I think I've found a fix. 
## The Cause
The bug occurs because the OptimizedModule wrapper in torch._dynamo.eval_frame doesn't call the __len__  method. This causes Python's bool() check to fall back to the default object truthiness (always True) instead of correctly evaluating containers with len() == 0 as False.

## The Fix
A very easy fix . I just added the len method to OptimizedModule in torch._dynamo.eval_frame class to delegate the call to the original module
```python
def __len__(self):
    """
    Proxy the len() call to the original module to fix truthiness checks.
    """
    return len(self._orig_mod)
```
This successfully fixes the issue . The script now works as expected.
## Reproduction Script
```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Create an empty nn.ModuleList
original = nn.ModuleList()

# Compile it using torch.compile
compiled = torch.compile(original)

# Compare their boolean evaluations
print(f"bool(original): {bool(original)}")
print(f"bool(compiled): {bool(compiled)}")

# Trigger failure if they differ
assert bool(original) == bool(compiled), "BUG: truthiness behavior mismatch after compilation"
```
Output

bool(original): False
bool(compiled): False


I'm happy to open a pull request to contribute this fix. Let me know if this is the expected approach.

@i3hz 

Thanks so much for looking into this! While I'm not an official maintainer, I'm quite confident that your proposed fix achieves exactly the behavior I was expecting. I really appreciate your effort and your willingness to contribute a PR!

Hi @tinywisdom could you try `model.compile()` instead? That should fix the issue, and it's also the [recommended way](https://docs.pytorch.org/tutorials/intermediate/torch_compile_tutorial.html#best-practices-and-recommendations) of compiling an nn Module now. I'm working on generating warnings for potential footgun usages, and also publicizing `model.compile()` more.

Hi @StrongerXi , thanks a lot for the suggestion!

I just tried `model.compile()` and everything works perfectly now — no issues at all.

I’m curious—does PyTorch now recommend `model.compile()` over `torch.compile()` in all scenarios? Is the priority higher mainly because torch.compile might have more side effects?

Also, if a bug occurs with `torch.compile` but not with `model.compile()`, do you think it’s still valuable to report it?

Thanks again for your time and insights!

> I just tried model.compile() and everything works perfectly now — no issues at all.

Glad to hear!

> I’m curious—does PyTorch now recommend model.compile() over torch.compile() in all scenarios? Is the priority higher mainly because torch.compile might have more side effects?

It's a bit hard to say conclusively, but in general `model.compile()` should become the default, i.e., first thing you try. If that breaks, you can try `torch.compile(model)`. The "advantage" the latter gives you is that it allows you to install hooks on the resulting module and the hooks won't be compiled (but hooks installed on the original module will be compiled).

> Also, if a bug occurs with torch.compile but not with model.compile(), do you think it’s still valuable to report it?

Absolutely, at the very least it'll help others who run into similar issues in the future since it'll show up in searches:).

159239.body: The appears to be isolated to the Inductor backend only, and it's only reproducible with `bfloat16`.

I have a way to workaround this, as most LLMs do rope via converting q/k to `float32` even the original type is `bfloat16`, and some of the occurrences do this conversion explicitly. For example, llama4 does this at [here](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/main/src/transformers/models/llama4/modeling_llama4.py#L198-L207).
However, the [Qwen model](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/4f8f51be4e230a98b53561b1a0e63c713caad3d4/src/transformers/models/qwen3/modeling_qwen3.py#L92-L116) among many others in HF doesn't do this explicitly and caused the discrepancy. Probably we can have a quick fix to use `.float()` at HF, but still need to look at why eager can handle this conversion implicitly but Inductor doesn't do it.

If you run the test on CUDA with `TORCHINDUCTOR_EMULATE_PRECISION_CASTS=1`, it will pass, which shows this is related to the type conversion @yanboliang  mentioned.

I also tried `TORCHINDUCTOR_EMULATE_PRECISION_CASTS=1` on CPU, but it failed with 
```
torch._inductor.exc.InductorError: TypeError: CppVecOverrides.to_dtype() got an unexpected keyword argument 'use_compute_types'
```. So I am marking this as an CPU inductor issue.

import torch
import unittest
from typing import List

torch._inductor.config.emulate_precision_casts = True

def rotate_half(x):
    """Rotates half the hidden dims of the input."""
    x1 = x[..., : x.shape[-1] // 2]
    x2 = x[..., x.shape[-1] // 2 :]
    return torch.cat((-x2, x1), dim=-1)

def apply_rotary_pos_emb(
    q: torch.Tensor,
    k: torch.Tensor,
    cos: torch.Tensor,
    sin: torch.Tensor,
    position_ids: torch.Tensor = None,
    unsqueeze_dim: int = 1,
) -> List[torch.Tensor]:
    """Applies Rotary Position Embedding to the query and key tensors.

    Args:
        q (`torch.Tensor`): The query tensor.
        k (`torch.Tensor`): The key tensor.
        cos (`torch.Tensor`): The cosine part of the rotary embedding.
        sin (`torch.Tensor`): The sine part of the rotary embedding.
        position_ids (`torch.Tensor`, *optional*):
            Deprecated and unused.
        unsqueeze_dim (`int`, *optional*, defaults to 1):
            The 'unsqueeze_dim' argument specifies the dimension along which to unsqueeze cos[position_ids] and
            sin[position_ids] so that they can be properly broadcasted to the dimensions of q and k. For example, note
            that cos[position_ids] and sin[position_ids] have the shape [batch_size, seq_len, head_dim]. Then, if q and
            k have the shape [batch_size, heads, seq_len, head_dim], then setting unsqueeze_dim=1 makes
            cos[position_ids] and sin[position_ids] broadcastable to the shapes of q and k. Similarly, if q and k have
            the shape [batch_size,s seq_len, heads, head_dim], then set unsqueeze_dim=2.
    Returns:
        `tuple(torch.Tensor)` comprising of the query and key tensors rotated using the Rotary Position Embedding.
    """
    cos = cos.unsqueeze(unsqueeze_dim)
    sin = sin.unsqueeze(unsqueeze_dim)
    q_embed = (q * cos) + (rotate_half(q) * sin)
    k_embed = (k * cos) + (rotate_half(k)

I think it's slow because for `batch size = 1`, `bmm` is a bad pick, it's better to just use `mm`. However, since the batch size is dynamic, we decided to use `bmm`. 

I verified that if `mm` is used instead of `bmm`, fp16 would be faster than fp32 (0.92 vs  3.48 ) when batch size = 1. (if you compile AOTI with example input torch.randn(1, 1000, embed_dim) without using dynamic shapes, you get `mm` instead of `bmm`. )

A summary of this issue:


Current implementation: In our current aten.linear decomposition and aten’s matmul implementation, we choose to [fold bmm to mm](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/16ce2c15fab06565821b0d908c13a717f02746c0/torch/_decomp/decompositions.py#L4506) if this doesn’t incur extra copy. More specifically, if the input tensors are not contiguous, we will not fold bmm to mm. One exception is if require_grad=Ture, in that case we do incur in an extra copy to avoid creating an unnecessary large tensor in the backward. 

Proposed implementation: We should choose aten.mm over aten.bmm when row dimension is low, even if that means we need to call .contiguous() to copy. If we have [B, M, K] x [K, N], we should fold when M is small.

Benchmark:

Let’s try to AOTI-compile and run the following small model. This is a minimal repro of this [MultiheadAttention example](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/159346). 

The inference time between using aten.mm and aten.bmm is 0.54ms vs 7.54ms (14x) !

device = "cuda"

weight = torch.randn(6144, 2048, device=device).to(torch.float16)

# we do a transpose here to make a non-contiguous. This can happen in nn.MultiheadAttention when batch_size_first=True

So in this case we have activations of shape [t, b, in_features] and weight of shape [in_features, out_features]. If we treat it as bmm, we expand weight to [t, in_features, out_features] and have t batches of [b, in_features] @ [in_features, out_features] matmuls. With small b these matmuls will never be efficient (small b doesn't allow you to read big tiles which is a precondition for efficient matmul). Avoiding inefficient matmuls should not be dependent on `requires_grad` status and yeah bmm won't help here, even if generated kernels was somewhat better than cublas. We need heuristics change, although we have to realize that this heuristics change will lead to higher memory usage due to copy.

Actually, in this case we could call `.transpose(0,1)` on activations inside linear to make them contiguous, then fold, and then transpose output in the same way, no extra memory, good perf, output layout is the same as input layout (batch-first)
