161372.body:
@rasbt you can fix this by setting this config:
```
torch._dynamo.config.allow_unspec_int_on_nn_module = True
```

---
More in-depth discussion

Root cause is this https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/155099/files#r2345122548. A few guesses/observations:
1. Maybe the recompilation was supposed to be there all along, and the "regression" in 2.8 is actually a "fix".
2. Like (1), but maybe the fix was overly aggressive, so it ended up affecting a harmless case like this issue.
3. Setting `torch._dynamo.config.inline_inbuilt_nn_modules = False` also fixes this issue, so even if we did (1) from the start, this would've been a regression from flipping `inline_inbuilt_nn_modules` on by default.

Anyways, here's a minimal repro. Maybe automatic dynamic has to do with this as well, so cc @anijain2305 @bobrenjc93
```python
import torch

class Mod(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.pos = 2

    def forward(self, x):
        old_pos = self.pos
        self.pos += 1
        return x + old_pos

x = torch.rand(10)
mod = Mod()
mod = torch.compile(mod)

mod(x)
mod(x) # expected recompile due to `self.pos` bump
mod(x) # unexpected recompile since automatic dynamic shape should've kicked in last time
```

Cc @pianpwk for his recent work on new API for marking intergers dynamic. 

Thanks @rasbt for opening the issue. Though unfortunate, the change was intended to remove inconsistency in handling dynamism for integers. @pianpwk is adding a new API so that we don't have to rely on hidden config flags in future
Thank you @anijain2305 @pianpwk -  do you have a (WIP) PR to tie to this issue?

From triage meeting: the solution is
```
torch._dynamo.config.allow_unspec_int_on_nn_module = True
```

cc @pytorch/pytorch-dev-infra @Lucaskabela we probably want to put this in the release notes since it's a breaking change, i.e., users would need to turn on this flag explicitly to restore the old behavior.
@rasbt @miladm https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/162194 is the WIP PR for wrapping ints

161425.body: Maybe the answer is: "we need to run FakeTensorUpdater for joint graph passes too", but would love to confirm.

This issue no longer reproduces on PyTorch 2.9+. On 2.8, the AOT forward graph shows `view_10: "f32[...]" = view(slice_tensor_5: bf16[...])` — a `view` incorrectly annotated as f32 when its input is bf16. On 2.9+ the graph topology changed so `view_10` operates on the f32 `bmm` output instead, making the annotation correct.

Verified on PyTorch 2.12.0a0+git89d1757 (current main).

(Analysis done by Claude.)
Closing as this was fixed in PyTorch 2.9.

161473.body: Could you try triton.testing.do_bench_cudagraph for benchmarking? The measurements might be cpu overhead

The only notable thing here is fp32 inputs which flex is 11x worse than mem-efficient where for fp16 we are 2x faster, 

This is because mem-efficient attention by default enables tf32 precison for fp32, to enable the same behavior 
```
import time
import statistics
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

from torch.nn.attention.flex_attention import flex_attention, create_block_mask


B = 2
H = 32
L = 4096
D_head = 128
device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"

WARMUP = 10
ITERS = 50

torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = False
torch.backends.cudnn.allow_tf32 = False

torch._dynamo.config.cache_size_limit = 512
torch._dynamo.config.accumulated_cache_size_limit = 4096

try:
    flex_attention = torch.compile(flex_attention)
except Exception as e:
    print("torch.compile(flex_attention) failed or not necessary:", e)

torch.set_float32_matmul_precision("high")

def causal_mask(b, h, q_idx, kv_idx):
    return q_idx >= kv_idx


torch.manual_seed(0)
q = torch.randn(B, H, L, D_head, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
k = torch.randn(B, H, L, D_head, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
v = torch.randn(B, H, L, D_head, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)

block_mask = create_block_mask(
    causal_mask, B=None, H=None, Q_LEN=L, KV_LEN=L, device=device, _compile=True
)


def call_flex(q, k, v, block_mask):
    return flex_attention(q, k, v, block_mask=block_mask)


def call_sdpa(q, k, v):
    return F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=True)


def benchmark(fn, name, warmup=WARMUP, iters=ITERS, use_block_mask=False):
    device_is_cuda = device.startswith("cuda")
    if device_is_cuda:
        torch.cuda.empty_cache()
        torch.cuda.reset_peak_memory_stats(device)

    for i in range(warmup):
        out = fn(q, k, v, block_mask) if use_block_mask else fn(q, k, v)
        if device_is_cuda:
            torch.cuda.synchronize()
    times = []
    for i in range(iters):
        if device_is_cuda:

I believe your repro is fixed now. New repro with `torch._dynamo.error_on_graph_break`:
```python
import torch


@torch._dynamo.error_on_graph_break(True)
@torch.compile(backend="eager")
def fn(x):
    y = x + 10

    with torch._dynamo.error_on_graph_break(False):

        class Foo:
            def __init__(self, x):
                self.x = x

    f = Foo(x)
    return f.x - y


x = torch.tensor([1.0])
y = fn(x)
print(y)
```

Note that the underlying meta kernel bug (#159192) is still open and would need to be fixed before re-landing the inlining change.

(Analysis done by Claude.)

161671.body: For `torch.export.save`, order to save the exported program to a schematized format with backwards compatibility guarantees and can be loaded in non-python environments, we must adhere to a certain [specification](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/main/export/ir_spec.html). Thats why you're seeing the error `call_module is not valid: got a class 'p_conv_weight_obs'`. To adhere to spec, you can re-export the modified model, but I'm not sure if this will work since it's not really following the [pt2e](https://docs.pytorch.org/ao/main/tutorials_source/pt2e_quant_ptq.html) workflow.

161705.body: @bobrenjc93 @laithsakka @pianpwk  Adding high pri because of the inconsistent behavior, but please re-assess the severity. I am not knowledgeable enough about dynamic shapes to answer here.

@xuantengh this is unfortunately expected behavior with the named Dims API for export (`Dim(name="len", ...)`). Because slicing has very flexible semantics, the output size of `x[:, :5]` isn't consistent across input values; it's 5 for `x.size(1) >= 5`, and `x.size(1)` otherwise.

The slice decomposition has logic on which branch to take (export can only specialize on 1), and it's guided by the tracing sample input, after which a guard is installed according to the branch. Additionally, the named Dims API also has an invariant that the guards have to be consistent across all values in the min/max range.

This is why you see inconsistency - the sample input sizes of 3 & 8 lead you to different branches, install different guards, and the named Dims API raises in either case (behavior diverges across the range) - just raising on a different set of guards in each case.

As a solution, I'd recommend choosing a slice bound & min/max range that has consistent output behavior, e.g.
```python
class SliceModel(nn.Module):
     def forward(self, x):
        return x[:, :5]

dynamic_shapes = {"x": {1: torch.export.Dim(name="len", min=5, max=13)}}  # output shape is always 5
```

or
```python
class SliceModel(nn.Module):
     def forward(self, x):
        return x[:, :5]

dynamic_shapes = {"x": {1: torch.export.Dim(name="len", min=0, max=5)}}  # output shape is always x.size(1)
```

but if you do need to support divergent behavior in your program, we can explore usage of unbacked semantics.
@anijain2305 I don't think it's too high-pri; a known export issue. I'll remove the label? Feel free to add it back.

@pianpwk thansk for your explanation!

> but if you do need to support divergent behavior in your program, we can explore usage of unbacked semantics.

Yeah, the background is: we want to support the variant sequence length after exporting the model (which is supported in the eager mode). Although we can achieve this by padding the sequence input into the maximal length (13 in this case), I think it's better to have `slice` support the flexible behavior which is also consistent with native PyTorch execution.

Besides, I think the guards added by `slice` are somehow too restrict? Given a sample input `x` with shape `(4, 3)`, why should  `x[:, :5]` imply that the later inputs should always have its second dimension less than 5? From the perspective of semantics, I think it's reasonable for slice `[:, :5]` to accept `x` with shape `(4, 8)`.

> Besides, I think the guards added by slice are somehow too restrict? Given a sample input x with shape (4, 3), why should x[:, :5] imply that the later inputs should always have its second dimension less than 5? From the perspective of semantics, I think it's reasonable for slice [:, :5] to accept x with shape (4, 8).

@xuantengh the compiler needs to compute a symbolic value for the output size of `x[:,:5]`.
- if `x.size(1) < 5` -> it's `x.size(0)`
- if `x.size(1) >= 5` -> it's 5
In a ideal world it's `min(5, x.size(0))`, but this type of conditional expression is hard for the compiler to reason with, and it's a WIP (e.g. see #157944)

In the extremely general case, consider a slice `x[:u0]`, where `u0` is between `[-inf, inf]`.
Because of slice's flexible semantics, the output size is represented by `min(x.size(0), max(0, u0 if u0 >= 0 else x.size(0) + u0))`.

You can imagine the compiler can't do much with such an expression, and it's simpler to specialize on one particular case of slicing.

Also, the model could be exported by setting:

```python
dynamic_shapes = {"x": {1: torch.export.Dim.AUTO }}
```

But the range constraints still exist after exporting the model:

```python
print(ep.range_constraints)
# {s0: VR[5, int_oo]}
```

> You can imagine the compiler can't do much with such an expression, and it's simpler to specialize on one particular case of slicing.

So at this stage, we can only take this as an expected behavior and try some sidesteps to workaround this? Or is there any approach for us to treat `x.size(1)` as an unbacked symint?
I only skimmed, but making x.size(1) unbacked to force alternate semantics in the 1 case seems right to me

> I only skimmed, but making x.size(1) unbacked to force alternate semantics in the 1 case seems right to me

yeh we need to convince export to support unbacked i dont think export have a way to mark unbacked now.  @avikchaudhuri 
side note @pianpwk  is working on making slicing works with preserving semantics with unbacked very soon!
@laithsakka I think export support mark unbacked is potentially something the dynamic shapes pod should be willing to roll up sleeves and do if needed, it seems to block a lot of use cases.

161763.body: This happens because the output of `torch.neg(c) + x` is `float32` and therefore `c, x` are both first cast to `float32` and then the result is computed. This results in `torch.neg(c)` being actually negative instead of the two's complement.
We can fix the `aten.neg` by using a `2**bits - x` implementation for unsigned dtypes, but the overall issue remains.

161783.body: AOTAutograd is known to be a lot, if not most, of the overhead.

Hi, I was wondering if this is the same issue that I was mentioning on the forum ([here](https://discuss.pytorch.org/t/reducing-compiled-call-overhead/222740)). I was wondering also because I posted there just a few hours before this issue was opened.
In case we are talking of the same problem, I just wanted to ask the same question I was asking there: is there anything that I can do to reduce the overhead between the python function call of a torch-compiled function and the actual launch of the cuda graph? From my understanding a lot of time is spent in the python code that wraps the compiled/graphed code, is there any way to reduce that or to go around it? Especially because after the few iterations it would probably be possible to just load the data and launch the cuda graph, skipping most of the checks. Thanks!

@c-rizz Yes this seems to be related. I have been profiled runtime overheads, mostly for AOTAutograd. Unfortunately theres no "easy" area to cut, since the overhead seems to be fairly distributed from my profiling.

For cudagraphs, copying nonstatic addresses can also appear as overhead, but this isn't my area of expertise. @BoyuanFeng if you have more advice specific to cuda graphs, can you follow up on the forum?

Quick summary of current state after doing some preliminary investigations:

1. I found that a large portion (potentially up to half) was due to inefficiency in `record_function`, which is present even if profiling isn't present. I'm working on (a) removing the `record_function` if not profiling #163747, and (b) making `record_function` use `RecordFunctionFast` which has less overhead #163566. Still some uncertainties on (b), but (a) is safe to merge.
2. I found that between PT 2.4 and 2.5, `eval_frame.py (compile_wrapper)` regressed in overhead time by a significant margin, and now is reported by cProfile to be the single most time consuming function. This handles saving and changing the eval frame before entering the compiled function. I'm still looking into what can be done here.
3. Between `_aot_autograd/runtime_wrappers.py (forward)`, `_aot_autograd/runtime_wrappers.py (runtime_wrapper)`, and `_aot_autograd/utils.py (call_func_at_runtime_with_args)`, eliminating the "prologue" and "epilogue" sections of each (ie code surrounding the actual compiled function call) reduces overhead by around 30%. I'm looking into what scenarios we can skip some parts of this code if any, but if we are able to make leads here it should help improve overhead in AOTAutograd.

Updates:

1. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/163747#event-19935185252 is landed and should reduce runtime overhead by nearly 50% from local testing. 
2. To speed up profiling, I started a draft PR to modify `record_function` https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/163566, but this is seeming harder to land so I will likely shelf this for now and push for https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/163976 to be merged instead.
3. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/163866 adds a PR time benchmark for runtime overhead to track this in the future
4. I'm still narrowing down where the increased eval frame overhead is coming from.
5. AOTAutograd overhead still needs to be investigated to see which of the previously mentioned components can be reduced.

Thanks for the work, eager to see the coming overhead improvements.
I tried implementing the first fix in my local code, but the root issue seems to not be there in 2.8, it's something that was introduced afterwards, right?

@c-rizz Correct, the change I mentioend before is available in the nightly, but won't be available in a release until 2.10. Are you saying you are not encountering your overhead issue in 2.8? If so, what version were you using previously?

I do have the overhead issue, I was just asking because I was hoping to cut it down by removing that record_function call, but I found it already was not there. I guess the overhead I am seeing comes from the other issues that you mentioned then.
Mostly I was hoping for some quick (even if dirty) way to reduce the overhead I have, as it probably accounts for more than half my training time. My usecase if a Soft Actor-Critic implementation, so nets are small.

Ah you're right, I didn't realize that profiling regression was recent. Unfortunately that means you may see higher overhead in 2.9. I'll be updating here when more progress is made with other sources of overhead.

Simple repro:
```
import torch
import triton
import triton.language as tl

@triton.constexpr_function
def log2(n):
    return len(bin(n)) - 3

@triton.jit
def kernel(x_ptr, output_ptr, n_elements, BLOCK_SIZE: tl.constexpr):
    pid = tl.program_id(axis=0)  # We use a 1D launch grid so axis is 0.
    block_start = pid * BLOCK_SIZE
    offsets = block_start + tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE)
    mask = offsets < n_elements
    x = tl.load(x_ptr + offsets, mask=mask)

    FIRST_DIM: tl.constexpr = x.shape[0]
    output = x + log2(FIRST_DIM)
    tl.store(output_ptr + offsets, output, mask=mask)

@torch.compile(fullgraph=True)
def f(x):
    out = torch.zeros_like(x)
    n_elements = x.numel()
    grid = lambda meta: (triton.cdiv(n_elements, meta['BLOCK_SIZE']), )
    kernel[grid](x, out, n_elements, BLOCK_SIZE=16)
    return out

x = torch.randn(16, device='cuda')

print(f(x))
```
I can see that the output code does not have the log2 function. Let me see how easy it would be to fix this.

161889.body:
SAC(compile(fn is not supported today. If you'd like to compose SAC with compile, you should use the wrapping order compile(SAC(fn. 

This is happening because SAC is a TorchDispatchMode, if it is enabled when compile is tracing, the frame will be skipped.
@soulitzer is correct
Why would it be infeasible to use the wrapping order `compile(SAC`?

> Why would it be infeasible to use the wrapping order `compile(SAC`?
```python
import functools

import torch
from torch.utils.checkpoint import checkpoint, CheckpointPolicy, create_selective_checkpoint_contexts


ops_to_save = [
   torch.ops.aten.mm.default,
]

def policy_fn(ctx, op, *args, **kwargs):
    if op in ops_to_save:
       return CheckpointPolicy.MUST_SAVE
    else:
       return CheckpointPolicy.PREFER_RECOMPUTE

context_fn = functools.partial(create_selective_checkpoint_contexts, policy_fn)

@torch.compile(fullgraph=True, dynamic=True)
def checkpoint_fn_with_context(fn, *args, **kwargs):
    return checkpoint(fn, *args, **kwargs, use_reentrant=False, context_fn=context_fn)

@torch.compile(fullgraph=True, dynamic=True)
def checkpoint_fn_without_context(fn, *args, **kwargs):
    return checkpoint(fn, *args, **kwargs, use_reentrant=False)

def f(x, y):
    x = 3 * x + 1
    y = 2 * y + 1
    return x @ y

x = torch.ones(10, 12, device="cuda", requires_grad=True)
y = torch.ones(12, 10, device="cuda", requires_grad=True)

# f_compiled = torch.compile(f, fullgraph=True, dynamic=True)

torch.cuda.cudart().cudaProfilerStart()
for i in range(50):
    torch.cuda.nvtx.range_push(f"f_compiled_without_context {i}")
    z = checkpoint_fn_without_context(f, x, y)
    z.sum().backward()
    torch.cuda.nvtx.range_pop()

for i in range(50):
    torch.cuda.nvtx.range_push(f"f_compiled_with_context {i}")
    z = checkpoint_fn_with_context(f, x, y)
    z.sum().backward()
    torch.cuda.nvtx.range_pop()
torch.cuda.cudart().cudaProfilerStop()
"""
nsys profile -w true -t cuda,nvtx,cudnn,cublas -s cpu --capture-range=cudaProfilerApi --capture-range-end=stop --kill=none --force-overwrite true -x true -o torch_compile_selective_activation_acheckpoint \
python3 torch_compile_selective_activation_acheckpoint.py
"""
```

> SAC(compile(fn is not supported today. If you'd like to compose SAC with compile, you should use the wrapping order compile(SAC(fn.
> 
> This is happening because SAC is a TorchDispatchMode, if it is enabled when compile is tracing, the frame will be skipped.

Using torch compile together with SAC (Selective Activation Checkpointing) is quite important for training performance improvements, as it can greatly save GPU memory and training speed. Are there any plans to support this functionality in the future? It's infeasible to use the wrapping order compile(SAC(fn in training.

from torch.distributed._composable.fsdp import MixedPrecisionPolicy

import torch.distributed as dist

def B_to_GiB(bytes: int | float) -> float:
    return bytes / 2**30


def get_tensor_bytes(tensor: torch.Tensor) -> int:
    """
    Returns the bytes of storage a given tensor takes up. If `tensor` is a view of a larger tensor,
    this function only returns the bytes associated with the view.
    """
    tensor_bytes = tensor.numel() * tensor.element_size()
    return tensor_bytes


class AllocatedMemContext:
    """
    Context manager which captures the allocated GPU memory at context exit and the change between
    enter and exit
    Only includes `allocated_bytes.all.`-prefixed keys in `memory_stats` with all readings converted
    to GiB.
    """

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        # Ensure CUDA libraries are loaded:
        torch.cuda.current_blas_handle()

        self.before: dict[str, int] = {}
        self.after: dict[str, int] = {}
        self.delta: dict[str, int] = {}

        self._mem_key_prefix = "allocated_bytes.all."

    def _get_mem_dict(self) -> dict[str, int]:
        return {
            k.replace(self._mem_key_prefix, ""): v
            for k, v in torch.cuda.memory_stats().items()
            if self._mem_key_prefix in k
        }

Hi @goldhuang, sorry for the inactivity here. We've been working on a design w/ @zou3519 and @bdhirsh  to better support the `SAC(compile(fn` wrapping order. The current proposal is roughly as follows:

The high level is that we'll need update what graphs partitioner generates:

1. Forward graph: 
  a. Take the original forward-only graph and also return the exact intermediates that SAC policy specifies.
2. Recompute graph and backward graph:
  a. Take the joint graph and DCE pieces of forward given that SAC policy has saved things
  b. Min-cut this joint graph while banning recompute of non-fusible ops in the closure/ancestors of the forward output 

Reasoning of 2b.
- We want to min-cut because we want to delay remat as much as possible! 
- We want to ban non-fusible ops because we don't want to recompute an extra time
- We can scope the banning to only ops that must be in the recompute function (which are the ones that in the closure of the forward output)

Thank you! In the meantime I found out that when the graph looks like this:
```
input_1 = ...
output_1 = ...
input_2 = ...
output_2 = ...
```
The pattern matcher will try to insert the replacement before `output_1`, and if `input_2` is used in the replacement, it will be used before its definition, producing the error.

I was able to work around it by manually moving all input nodes before the first output, I'll share the fix tomorrow.

@ProExpertProg Yes your understanding is correct! I also put up a possible fix to this issue in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/162029 which tells the pattern matcher to insert the replacement graph after all the input nodes

A workaround for `torch<2.9`:
```python
def process_match(match):
    """
    Process a multi-output match and move input nodes before output nodes.
    Returns True to allow direct use as the extra_check function in
    PatternMatcherPass.register() and enable automatic replacement.

    Example:
    input_1 = empty()
    output_1 = relu(input_1)
    input_2 = empty()
    output_2 = output_1 + input_2

    Pattern matcher inserts the replacement before the first output node,
    resulting in a use of input_2 before its definition.
    """

    # The pattern matcher inserts the replacement before the first output node.
    # If one of the inputs to the pattern occurs after the first output node,
    # the resulting graph will be incorrect.
    # Here, we manually move the pattern inputs to before the first output node.
    print(match.graph.python_code(root_module="self").src)
    nodes = list(match.graph.nodes)
    output_nodes = match.output_nodes()
    input_nodes = match.args + list(match.kwargs.values())
    assert len(set(input_nodes) & set(output_nodes)) == 0, \
        f"Overlapping inputs and outputs: {set(input_nodes) & set(output_nodes)}"

    node_indices = {node: nodes.index(node) for node in (output_nodes + input_nodes)}
    def node_index(node):
        # lazy compute other nodes as we don't need it for all nodes,
        # but there might be inputs to inputs
        if node not in node_indices:
            node_indices[node] = nodes.index(node)
        return node_indices[node]


    print(node_indices)

    first_output_node = min(output_nodes, key=lambda node: node_index(node))
    first_output_node_idx = node_index(first_output_node)
    print(f"first output node is {first_output_node} at index {first_output_node_idx}")
    insertion_point = nodes[first_output_node_idx - 1]
    print(f"Inserting nodes at {insertion_point}")

    nodes_to_process = list(input_nodes) # copy
    print(f"{nodes_to_process=}")
    while nodes_to_process:
        # brea

@ProExpertProg after some digging, I realized we have a builtin [`stable_topological_sort`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/b67c41039835bd9b20b83cd6233e86baaa5f5dde/torch/_inductor/pattern_matcher.py#L2180) which can be called after the pattern matching passes -- 

```python
from torch._inductor.pattern_matcher import stable_topological_sort

def custom_pass(graph: torch.fx.Graph) -> torch.fx.Graph:
    count = my_patterns.apply(graph)
    stable_topological_sort(graph)
    graph.eliminate_dead_code()
    return graph
```

This pass is run after all the custom post-grad passes [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/b67c41039835bd9b20b83cd6233e86baaa5f5dde/torch/_inductor/fx_passes/post_grad.py#L180-L185) -- so you could wait for it to be run then, or at the end of all the passes you could call this topological sort pass?
This seems reasonable, we'll do that. Might be worth improving the documentation here so it's clear the pattern matcher can reorder nodes

162151.body: reproducing it, will raise a PR

So this line in the user program:
```
x[0] = x[0].t()
```

will issue a call to something like `aten.copy_(x, x_alias)`, which reads and writes to the same memory concurrently. This is undefined behavior, and compile and eager happen to handle this behavior differently, leading to the different numerics.

I'm not sure that we can fix this - although I don't know if we have docs describing "concurrent read/write behavior in eager being undefined".

From triage: we should either:

(1) make compile error in this case (functionalization changes)

(2) make eager error in this case (beef up the eager overlap check without making it too slow)

(3) do both

cc @anijain2305 for guard issue. We are generating the seemingly correct TENSOR_MATCH guard: `TENSOR_MATCH: check_tensor(G['conv']._parameters['weight'], Parameter, DispatchKeySet(CPU, BackendSelect, ADInplaceOrView, AutogradCPU), torch.float32, device=None, requires_grad=False, size=[16, 3, 3, 3], stride=[27, 9, 3, 1])  # return self._conv_forward(input, self.weight, self.bias)`, but we're not recompiling, so something is probably wrong with guard eval.

@williamwen42 can you check if setting `torch._dynamo.config.wrap_top_frame = True` fixes the `conv.compile()` case? If so it might be related to #158455.
@anijain2305 any reason against turning `wrap_top_frame` on by default?

Can you try `torch._dynamo.config.skip_tensor_guards_with_matching_dict_tags = False`? 

This is an unfortunate case, where in order to make guards fast, we are treating parameters as essentially constants. But for cases like the one shared in this repro, users do inplace mutate the weight requires_grad, and Dynamo fails to trigger a recompile.

If we flip the value of this flag by default, this will lead to large increase in guard overhead. 


At the least, this definitely deserves as a mention in torch.compile docs. I will take an action on that. Thanks @UmaisZahid for opening the issue.
This is not a 2.9.0 cherry-pick issue - this issue existed in 2.8 as well.
I can confirm that `torch._dynamo.config.skip_tensor_guards_with_matching_dict_tags = False` guards/recompiles correctly for the reproducer. No problem - good to know it wasn't my end!

```

redirect_to_mode(<hop>, _CachingTorchDispatchMode)

162282.body:
Repeating a discussion with @aakhundov:

The issue appears to be a bad interaction between PT2's cudagraphs and a custom kernel (i.e. pytorch custom kernel, not a user-defined triton kernel) in sam_fast. The custom kernel uses a hand-written autotune implementation that we suspect is related to the issue.

https://github.com/meta-pytorch/segment-anything-fast/blob/6327c0df8ad4098b1fb83ee55c5a8b4f12fa0729/segment_anything_fast/flash_4.py#L308-L309

@aakhundov is continuing to investigate.

We should fix this before the PyTorch release. But I don't think this should block the PyTorch release; it's probably a relatively uncommon scenario and unclear whether PyTorch/PT2 even wants to support this scenario. A likely resolution for this will be a change to the benchmark itself.
@aakhundov fixed this in https://github.com/triton-lang/triton/pull/8115, we’re just waiting for the cherry pick to close the issue

162374.body: As mentioned in #162375, it's currently expected that the `pack_padded_sequence()` / `pad_packed_sequence()` APIs are not supported with torch.compile. For this specific issue, the error is less nice since a graph break happens but doesn't completely sidestep the issue. I'll let the oncall weigh in here but a simple workaround would avoid using these APIs.

162375.body: I believe this was done intentionally to avoid adding torch.compile support for `PackedSequence`. I'll leave it to the compiler folks to decide whether it's worth supporting this, but an obvious workaround is to avoid using the `pack_padded_sequence()` / `pad_packed_sequence()` APIs with torch.compile.
Can PackedSequence layout match internal layout of NJT? If so - maybe PackedSequence can be made a subclass of NestedTensor?

162430.body:
cc @BoyuanFeng for cudagraph trees
cc @jamesjwu as you worked on StaticCudaLauncher

For those who want to fix the issue by patching the image file here are the steps to do so. It is quite simple.
1. Download a hex editor like HxD
2. Find 'torch_python.dll', located in '...\Lib\site-packages\torch\lib'
3. Open a command prompt and type in 'tasklist /m torch_python.dll', if any processes come up they are currently using the DLL and they need to be shutdown.
4. Create a copy of the DLL just in case anything goes wrong.
5. Open the DLL with your hex editor
6. Search for the string 'KiiiiisOl'
7. You need to replace the last character 'l' byte '6C' with 'K' byte '4B'
8. Save the DLL.

Done. Next time you launch torch it will load the modified DLL and the issue above mentioned will be solved.

Update: actually `aot_eager` backend can pass the compilation, but the output is wrong(`scatter_` was running under non deterministic mode). 'eager' backend works well and produces right output, because there are `_set_deterministic_algorithms` call in the graph.

I think what is happening here is the same thing as the torch.cuda.synchronize() situation: Dynamo plops it into the graph and then AOTAutograd traces it and then ignores it.

Initial fix can be that Dynamo graph breaks on it. Marking hi-pri because this is technically silent incorrectness
Then the question is, how much do you want Dynamo to be able to fullgraph=True this code, @yanboliang ?
Also, on the Dynamo side we should try to proactively walk through some of these APIs to see what else needs a graph break on them...

> Then the question is, how much do you want Dynamo to be able to fullgraph=True this code, @yanboliang ?

I think it's important to have a set deterministic algorithms context manager and can trace it through as full graph. The use case is LLM training with deterministic is important, but some of the ops are pretty slow with deterministic mode. So we want to disable it if we are ensure the output is always deterministic, e.g. `torch.scatter_` if the indices arguments are unique. Then it introduce the use case of deterministic and non-deterministic mix in the same graph.

BTW, there is another bug here:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/36338fc7f22a65d3c4b53b3933e22ce13ae4ce4f/torch/_dynamo/variables/torch.py#L644

Actually `torch.use_deterministic_algorithms` is not a context manager, but we wrap this as context manager. This is a separate bug, even if I fix that, the above issue is still there.

        super().__init__()
        self.hidden_dim = hidden_dim
        self.tau = nn.Parameter(torch.tensor(tau))
        self.W = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(hidden_dim, hidden_dim) * 0.1)
        self.register_buffer('h', None)

    def forward(self, t, x=None):
        if x is None:
            x = torch.zeros_like(self.h)
        sigma_h = torch.sigmoid(self.h)
        Wh = torch.matmul(self.W, sigma_h.t()).t()
        dhdt = (-self.h + Wh + x) / self.tau
        return dhdt

    def reset(self, batch_size=1):
        self.h = torch.zeros(batch_size, self.hidden_dim, device=self.W.device)

class LiquidNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, output_dim, hidden_dim=32, solver='rk4', steps=10):
        super().__init__()
        self.input_dim = input_dim
        self.hidden_dim = hidden_dim
        self.solver = solver
        self.steps = steps
        self.input_layer = nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.output_layer = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, output_dim)
        self.liquid = LiquidNeuron(hidden_dim)
        self.register_buffer('t', torch.linspace(0, 1, steps))
    def forward(self, x):
        batch_size = x.size(0)
        self.liquid.reset(batch_size)
        h0 = self.input_layer(x)
        self.liquid.h.data = h0.clone()
        states = odeint(
            lambda t, h: self.liquid(t, x=None),
            self.liquid.h,
            self.t,
            method=self.solver
        )
        final_state = states[-1]
        return self.output_layer(final_state)

    def update_connectivity(self, plasticity_rate=0.01):
        sigma_h = torch.sigmoid(self.liquid.h.detach())
        delta_W = torch.matmul(sigma_h.t(), sigma_h) / sigma_h.size(0) * plasticity_rate
        self.liquid.W.data += delta_W -

    def forward(self, x):

            attended = (window * attn_weights).sum(dim=1)

            outputs.append(output)

162859.body: I am reluctant to literally have a SymmetricMemoryTensor type in JIT type system because it is non compositional and also just seems like a pain to implement

> The API to specify which input needs symmetric memory only applies to torch.compile. So a user would end up writing code that looks like:
> ```
> requires_symmetric_memory(collective, input=0)
> 
> def user_code(x):
>     if torch.compiler.is_compiling():
>         with symmetric_memory():
>             y = custom_op(x)
>     else:
>         y = custom_op(x)
>     return collective(z)
> ```

Could we just allow the eager path for torch compile so that only one is required? And Inductor just "ignores" the symmetric memory region during tracing? Or maybe there's an official "special" eager-only symmetric context that automatically becomes a no-op if compiling

- for option 1 (built-in operator), if the collective is not in-place, we also need to guarantee that the output is also allocated under the symmetric_memory ctx manager.
- for option 2  (custom operator), since all the allocations in the custom_op are going to use symmetric_memory, then we need to ensure that all participating ranks are going to maintain the symmetry. this can become a challenge and require further changes to the kernels--e.g. different ranks might works on different sizes of data.

more generally, option 1 can be quite useful for pure communication ops, but if we want arbitrary symm memory (for instance, in gemm+ar kernel the output and several barriers need to be allocated in symm memory), we still need to wrap the whole pattern and kernel call in a custom op.
@Amir-19 do you have more details behind the gemm+ar example?

> [@Amir-19](https://github.com/Amir-19) do you have more details behind the gemm+ar example?

I'm trying to integrate [gemm+ar kernel from cute_dsl](https://github.com/flashinfer-ai/flashinfer/blob/main/flashinfer/cute_dsl/gemm_allreduce_two_shot.py) (currently in flashinfer) into vllm, the [output tensor and itermidiate barriers](https://github.com/flashinfer-ai/flashinfer/blob/main/tests/test_cute_dsl_gemm_allreduce_two_shot.py#L346-L353) need to be in symm memory as we perform `multimem` operations on them. we can wrap the entire kernel prepration, memory allocation, etc. in a custom op but it's not ideal. I was thinking about extending this example beyond [torch symm memory](create_mc_tensor) which in its current form only supports single-node nvlink to either nvshmem or nccl backend to also support multi-node.
@amir-19 To clarify, torch symmetric memory supports multinode via nvshmem backend.

> [@Amir-19](https://github.com/Amir-19) To clarify, torch symmetric memory supports multinode via nvshmem backend.

I thought that `multicast_ptr` wasn't implemented yet. just to summarize my point:

1. (built-in operator) this should support collective operations--like nccl symm all reduce.
2. (custom operator) it can have sharp edges, since we have `with symmetric_memory():`, the user is required to maintain the symmetry of the memory allocations across the process group.

kernels that require symmetric memory (e.g. gemm+ar) would benefit from option 2.

1. I think this covers the compile case:
```
torch.library.define("my_collective(SymmTensor x) -> SymmTensor")
```
This is a contract of the op per se, between the author and the caller, in whatever context.

2. Re "it is non compositional and also just seems like a pain to implement"

I think it being a plain tensor data structure wise makes it compositional, i.e. developer does not need to write dispatch rules for existing ops. 

About implementation, this "type" of tensor does not exist at all, and thus does not require any implementation.

> ```
> torch.library.define("my_collective(SymmTensor x) -> SymmTensor")
> ```

I like this a lot. If users want to allocate intermediates inside a custom op, that can be handled by the op itself.

My only question is if we have this in eager mode:

```
def func(a: torch.Tensor):
    b = a.relu()
    c = torch.ops.my_collective(b)
    d = c.relu()
    return d
```

`b` should ideally be in symmetric memory and `c` will be in sym memory automatically, and during compilation we can enforce that, but in eager mode we'll have to annotate:
```
def func(a: torch.Tensor):
    with torch.symm_mem():
        b = a.relu()
    c = torch.ops.my_collective(b)
    d = c.relu()
    return d
```

So basically the annotation becomes a noop in compilation
torch.compile cannot trace the MemPool context today. So the annotation is a fall-safe here. Said in a different way, it is an alternative solution to tracing the MemPool context.

>I am reluctant to literally have a SymmetricMemoryTensor type in JIT type system because it is non compositional and also just seems like a pain to implement

I think we would just make SymmetricMemoryTensor a type alias of Tensor and everything would work. See, existing type aliases here: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/b66aa1ade12fe3910d5fc4a3bba0cd6f27c587fd/torch/csrc/jit/frontend/script_type_parser.cpp#L211-L221

This will be easier than `Tensor(s!)` because it won't touch any of the aliasing logic.

symm_mem_hint is another possibility..

Custom operators today just assume the input is being mutated, not that the input is being completely written to (in the out= fashion), also, there is no way to associate a functional custom operator with its out= custom operator variant.

@zou3519 Thanks for following up on that RFC!

Do you mind updating this RFC with the latest proposal for both eager and compiled scenarios and for both built-in and custom ops?

Ideally I want a solution for existing eager code like below to work with `torch.compile()`:
```
   with torch.cuda.use_mem_pool(symmetric_pool):
       y = compute_op(x)
   ....
   symmetric_op(y)
```
It is ok if code changes are required. But I would like to still be able to use a context manager for symmetric memory allocations in eager-mode, so that I don't need to chase all the pytorch allocations in the app/lib code base but I can do a broad catch at a higher level in the call stack (even if i end up with a few extra tensors in the symmetric pool that don't need symmetric memory). 
For `torch.compile` mode, I technically would not need to do the "broad catch" of allocations and just annotate the communication op with memory pool requirements for input/output. Then, `torch.compile` can trace the source of the allocations and allocates them properly inside the pool (what is done Today with NCCL/NVSHMEM memory inside XLA's buffer assignment pass)

FYI [here](https://github.com/openxla/xla/blob/9a77a882bb2bc75cb8c29620ff8cd0fd089bdc86/xla/service/gpu/gpu_memory_space_assignment.cc#L95-L99) is the XLA code where buffers are colored with a specific memory space.

@nvcastet we are on the same page re eager design and UI.

Some clarification re compile discussion above:

1. We want to have the same user code no matter in eager or torch.compile mode.
That is, the user code looks what @nvcastet points out:
```
   with torch.cuda.use_mem_pool(symmetric_pool):
       y = foo(x)
   ....
   symmetric_op(y)
```

2. Given 1, it seems to me and @zou3519 that Dynamo would already hit the MemPool context, and thus should be able to leverage it as hint to Inductor codegen. For example, Dynamo can tag each output variable in the context region with a "symm" label. This may render the hints in the op's `m.def` somewhat redundant, other than cross checking. (Well, no entirely redundant in some cases, see 3).

3. The hints in `m.def` may not be entirely redundant because a user can "over-apply" a MemPool, for example:
```
   with torch.cuda.use_mem_pool(symmetric_pool):
       y = foo(x)
       z = bar(y)
   ....
   regular_op(y)
   symmetric_op(z)
```
With hints, it is possible for torch.compile to figure out that `y` does not actually need to be symmetric, thus shrinking the size of the symmetric MemPool. This can potentially have a positive effect that the default pool is kept big, thus not so easy to OOM.

4. While 3 is an add-on effect torch.compile can make, it is not essential to the enablement of torch.compile + symm MemPool. Thus we can leave it out for step 1 of this RFC, and defer the discussion of the hint format -- "SymmTensor" or "Tensor(&)" for example.

@kwen2501 Thanks for summarizing!

I would also add a case 4. "torch.compile + hints only":

In cases where I just know that I am always running this code section with `torch.compile`, I should not have to use context managers and just let the compiler look at the hints and trace the allocations correctly.

If for some reason this code is executed in eager-mode, it should still be functional (with some perf lost). For NCCL comm ops, NCCL will either copy to symmetric intermediate buffers or fall back to non-symmetric kernels. For other symmetric custom ops, a copy to symmetric buffers may be needed before execution.

```
@torch.compile
def opt_foo():
    y = foo(x)
    z = bar(y)
    ....
    regular_op(y)
    symmetric_op(z)
```

As case 3. it is not a must-have in phase 1 but would be a good UX for the user (aka not worrying about mem_pool context managers) with torch.compile.

+1 on less-performant but still correct eager mode without annotations, in vLLM we still want eager correctness but it's fine if it causes extra copies, and it would be nicer UX if context managers are not required.

(Corrected) Plan of action after some more discussion with @eellison and @ezyang :

- annotated input on symmetric memory op and/or c10d ops (or annotate the entire `torch.ops.symm_mem` and `torch.ops.c10d` namespace, if annotating each input is cumbersome / too late for c10d ops) 

- inductor and aot eager will do memory planning/context so that input will be from comm mempool

- user can use mem pool context to avoid clone, although we will ignore in compile bc the compiler will memory plan that anyway

cc @syed-ahmed @eee4017 please let us know if you think the above plan would not cover the example code in #167026.

Hi @kwen2501,

I’ve been looking into @mlazos recent work on user-stream support in Dynamo, where `FxTracebackAnnotateVariable` is used to annotate context regions of the graph ([https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/166472](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/166472) and its follow-ups).

Would a similar annotation mechanism be a viable path for supporting `torch.cuda.use_mem_pool` in `torch.compile`?

When Dynamo traces user code, it can recognize the `torch.cuda.use_mem_pool` context manager and record the active mempool ID (or handle) in Dynamo’s symbolic state. Any tensor allocated inside this region would then receive a corresponding annotation on the FX node.

Concretely, for code such as:

```python
def compute(pool1, pool2):
    num_elements = 1024 * 1024 // 4
    data0 = torch.randn(num_elements, device='cuda')
    with torch.cuda.use_mem_pool(pool1):
        data1 = torch.randn(num_elements, device='cuda')
    with torch.cuda.use_mem_pool(pool2):
        data2 = torch.randn(num_elements, device='cuda')
    data0 = data0 + data1 + data2
    return data0, data1, data2
```

Dynamo could emit an graph roughly like:

```python
class GraphModule(torch.nn.Module):
    def forward(self):
        data0 = torch.randn(...)

        # Annotation: {'mempool': 0}
        data1 = torch.randn(...)

        # Annotation: {'mempool': 1}
        data2 = torch.randn(...)

        out = data0 + data1 + data2
        return out, data1, data2
```

After AOTAutograd, this annotation would flow into `inductor_post_grad_graph`, e.g.:

```python
# Annotation: {'mempool': 0}
inductor_random_default_1 = torch.ops.prims.inductor_random.default(...)

# Annotation: {'mempool': 1}
inductor_random_default = torch.ops.prims.inductor_random.default(...)
```


I’m still exploring the best way for this to integrate cleanly with Inductor, so any guidance on where this logic should live would be very helpful. My current thinking is as follows:

In [`torch/_inductor/codegen/wrapper.py`](

Btw, we've just landed MemPool caching support for symm_mem's CUDA backend (NVSHMEM backend was previously done).

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/169740
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/170008

Should have made their ways to 2.10 release.

@eee4017 it might help to take a look at this old pr : https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/138519

163048.body:
Hi, so this is an issue where AOTAutograd can not know ahead of time what gradients the user will invoke, so the ahead of time generated backward will compute gradients without stopping at the intermediate. And becase the AOT backward is installed as a single autograd node, the autograd engine's minimal set of nodes will still compute all gradients here.

To workaround the issue, we need to graph break on the intermediate (or rewrite the compiled function's forward to lift the intermediate as an input). Unfortunately, it's unfeasible to generate all permutations of potential backwards ahead of time, so we can't fix this without opting into compiled autograd.

```python

         # File: /home/xmfan/core/pytorch/allowunused.py:11 in forward, code: s = (y * y).sum()           # scalar, explicitly depends on y
        expand: "f32[2, 4][0, 0]cpu" = torch.ops.aten.expand.default(tangents_1, [2, 4]);  tangents_1 = None
        mul_1: "f32[2, 4][4, 1]cpu" = torch.ops.aten.mul.Tensor(expand, mm);  expand = mm = None
        
         # File: /home/xmfan/core/pytorch/allowunused.py:11 in forward, code: s = (y * y).sum()           # scalar, explicitly depends on y
        add: "f32[2, 4][4, 1]cpu" = torch.ops.aten.add.Tensor(tangents_2, mul_1);  tangents_2 = None
        add_1: "f32[2, 4][4, 1]cpu" = torch.ops.aten.add.Tensor(add, mul_1);  add = mul_1 = None
        
         # File: /home/xmfan/core/pytorch/allowunused.py:10 in forward, code: y = self.fc(x)              # (B, H)
        permute_1: "f32[4, 2][1, 4]cpu" = torch.ops.aten.permute.default(add_1, [1, 0])
        mm_1: "f32[4, 8][8, 1]cpu" = torch.ops.aten.mm.default(permute_1, primals

Yeah, this is a pretty famous problem, there are also ways you can tickle this such that it is silent incorrectness unfortunately.

While it's impossible to generate all permutations of graphs for potential autograd relationships, (1) I think it should be possible (although somewhat involved) to have autograd give a better error message when this happens, via some special extra metadata the autograd engine can consult when a grad wrt intermediate is requested, and (2) I think it should be possible for a user to explicitly say what sub-gradients they want, so that we can split up the generated autograd function accordingly. Both of these are nontrivial projects.

Another way to get around this besides compiled autograd is to do a non-strict trace on the entire code including the grad code. You won't be able to get guards this way but the right thing will happen.

cc @bdhirsh

> [@desertfire](https://github.com/desertfire) does AOTInductor release the GIL around kernel calls? That might be a better path for thread parallelism since it doesn't use Python wrapper code.

We do release GIL in the cpp-wrapper mode for torch.compile, https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/0696a4b0b81cf244afe4a0911593bf7cae5ea53b/torch/_inductor/codegen/cpp_wrapper_cpu.py#L588 . Can you try with `torch._inductor.config.cpp_wrapper = True`, @syuoni ?

Hi @desertfire and @jansel , thanks for your reply. I tried `torch._inductor.config.cpp_wrapper = True`, and it works.

I found the cpp wrapper should be turned on before the first execution of the torch.compile function, otherwise it's not effective. I'm wondering:
* Can I turn on this cpp wrapper by default? Does this have some functional or perf-related side effect? 
* If no side effect, will PyTorch turn on this by default in some future version?

You can also do:
```py
torch.compile(..., options={"cpp_wraper": True})
```

It should improve performance slightly, but it doesn't support everything so will fail in some corner cases.  That is the main blocker for on-by-default.

Thanks @jansel , this works perfectly. I came across https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/109074, which mentioned that `torch.compile` holds the GIL during compilation. Since the issue is still open, I suppose it's still the case. I'm wondering if there is another path like `cpp_wrapper` that releases GIL for compilation? I encountered cases during compilation where some operators hold the GIL and make CUDA API calls. For example, [this D2H copy](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/a213848703af037ef57e3ae2b1db2b1780beda40/torch/_inductor/fx_passes/joint_graph.py#L289) is holding the GIL. This is strange, because outside compilation, the same operator calls D2H copy with GIL released. Is there any global state during compilation that affects the GIL behavior?

> I came across #109074, which mentioned that torch.compile holds the GIL during compilation. Since the issue is still open, I suppose it's still the case. I'm wondering if there is another path like cpp_wrapper that releases GIL for compilation?

Our compiler is mostly implemented in Python, so it is expected for it to hold the GIL since it is Python code.  Though newer versions of Python have a no-GIL mode you can enable at compile-time.


> I encountered cases during compilation where some operators hold the GIL and make CUDA API calls. For example, [this D2H copy](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/a213848703af037ef57e3ae2b1db2b1780beda40/torch/_inductor/fx_passes/joint_graph.py#L289) is holding the GIL. This is strange, because outside compilation, the same operator calls D2H copy with GIL released. Is there any global state during compilation that affects the GIL behavior?

That code should just be calling the same eager mode kernel, so not sure what is going on there.
Thanks for you reply. Closing the issue.

163064.body: @anijain2305 could this be a missing guard?

Case A is a known gap -- `torch.compile` currently doesn't always error gracefully if the program also errors in eager, especially if it's due to some shape/device constraint error from pytorch ops.

Case B is definitely a bug.

Indeed, this does look related to https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/162174.

Using `torch._dynamo.config.skip_tensor_guards_with_matching_dict_tags = False` will cause case B to also give the TorchRuntimeError on inductor backend (instead of segfault). (eager and aot_eager do not segfault but there is no recompile, which is not correct behavior).

163066.body:
We are deleting functorch (and ODR mentioned there) in  https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/163340

Dynamo one is trickier... So it should have `oncall: pt2`

@pganssle-google it is actually much much worse than that. We also need some of their c code to work with their struct but they don't expose it. See https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/torch/csrc/dynamo/cpython_defs.c

The root cause of this is that we use this API https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/init.html#c._PyFrameEvalFunction but as you can see it takes a bunch of private struct and APIs in newer versions. So we have to grab a lot of these in any way we can to be able to continue using this API.
Unclear how to work around this easily, cc @williamwen42, @anijain2305

We could technically remove the internal headers by copy-pasting the headers and renaming symbols, but it seems like this would be difficult/annoying to maintain - we would need to do even more version-specific branching.

From what I can tell, this is not especially difficult to work around from our end, but it seems like fragile code that could grow to be a bigger problem in the future. From what I can tell, `dynamo` is built around this PEP 523 Frame inspection API, and the parts that y'all were using were broken, I think as part of the faster CPython efforts, in 3.12 or 3.13 or so.

I think the ideal solution would be to determine the minimum set of requirements for the PEP 523 API that dynamo requires to actually work, and propose to make that public in CPython. I feel like in the past few years there have been several parallel efforts that could cause further problems with this kind of metaprogramming that is highly implementation specific (e.g. nogil, JIT, faster cpython, ongoing efforts to clean up the C API), it's probably worth trying to surface y'all's particular use case so that it is well-represented when these kinds of changes come up, rather than reacting to changes as they happen.

Unfortunately, I don't know enough about `dynamo` to propose any specific minimum viable API, but I'm happy to help however I can.

I've actually been thinking of proposing a new "frame bytecode hook" PEP that aims to remove Dynamo's requirement of allocating/initializing/cleaning/deallocating/etc. the frame - this should significantly decrease reliance on CPython internals.

I also did have a task in mind to add some PEP 523 tests to CPython.

Unfortunately, I haven't had time to get around to doing either of these.

I was able to export with the latest nightly pytorch version and a small change in the inputs ordering. The exporter gets confused if the inputs and the dynamic shapes do not appear in the same order in both dictionaries (python dictionaries are ordered). We'll make it more robust to the order.

```python
sample_input = {
    "hidden_states": torch.randn(
        batch_size,
        latent_channels,
        latent_frames,
        latent_height,
        latent_width,
        dtype=dtype,
        device=device,
    ),
    "timestep": torch.tensor([1.0] * batch_size, dtype=dtype, device=device),
    "encoder_hidden_states": torch.randn(
        batch_size,
        text_maxlen,
        model.config["text_embed_dim"],
        dtype=dtype,
        device=device,
    ),
    "fps": torch.tensor([16] * batch_size, dtype=dtype, device=device),
    "condition_mask": torch.randn(
        batch_size,
        1,
        latent_frames,
        latent_height,
        latent_width,
        dtype=dtype,
        device=device,
    ),
    "padding_mask": torch.ones(
        1, 1, image_height, image_width, dtype=dtype, device=device
    ),
}

input_names = [
    "hidden_states",
    "timestep",

163189.body:
From what I can gather I see in the _export function that:
[def _export(](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/264e7f68a095b5b2f10e7ca66a3bbb16f6e56e28/torch/export/_trace.py#L2192)


The export system uses FakeTensorMode to intercept tensor creation and mutation for tracing.
When you mutate a buffer from None to a tensor inside forward, the tracing system sees this as a buffer assignment and will keep a FakeTensor as the value after tracing.
Since I don't believe you can restore the buffer to the original version, something like initialization with zeros could work.

`self.register_buffer("tensor_cache", torch.zeros(1))`

Maybe torch.export could produce a warning when buffers initialized as None are mutated.

import torch

def repro():
    n = 8

    dtype = torch.complex64

    A = torch.randn(4, n, n, dtype=dtype, requires_grad=True)
    A = A.clone(memory_format=torch.contiguous_format)

    I0 = torch.eye(n, dtype=A.dtype, device=A.device)  # (n, n)

TorchInductor has poor support for complex numbers.  Most complex ops end up being "fallbacks" where it just runs eager mode kernels.  So I doubt it is generating any kernels for this example.

You are also dealing with a very tiny graph, so disabling cudagraphs (mode="max-autotune-no-cudagraphs") should get you closer to eager performance.

If I tweak the compile mode, for example, `mode="reduce-overhead"` or `mode="max-autotune"`, the compile path breaks again.  You end up with the same underlying error from a different place:

```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/torch/_inductor/cudagraph_trees.py", line 657, in run
    check_memory_pool(self.device_index, self.cuda_graphs_pool, refs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/torch/_inductor/cudagraph_trees.py", line 1797, in check_memory_pool
    if torch._C._cuda_checkPoolLiveAllocations(device, pool_id, unique_storages):
       ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
RuntimeError: pluggable does not yet support checkPoolLiveAllocations. If you need it, please file an issue describing your use case.

Set the environment variable HYDRA_FULL_ERROR=1 for a complete stack trace.
```

In order to do this you would need a caching allocator that extends from CUDACachingAllocator. There are a series of PRs [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/160958) which make this possible.

Thanks @eellison - so, are you saying that external pool allocators that don't extend CUDACachingAllocator will not be possible to support?  Or perhaps, the trick will be to wrap external allocators in the CUDACachingAllocator interface?

The use case here is sharing pools of memory across different frameworks (RAPIDS, PyTorch, maybe others) and it'd be nice to have a unified pool -it's better for memory usage and better for performance, too, in my testing.

@coreyjadams potentially either directly extending the allocator, or an interface are both possible. The interactions are kind of subtle.

See more details [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/ebddbe787a1d193a56b3ed60986726b7a4b61ba3/c10/cuda/CUDACachingAllocator.cpp#L105-L136) and [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/ebddbe787a1d193a56b3ed60986726b7a4b61ba3/c10/cuda/CUDACachingAllocator.cpp#L2250-L2295).

     )

I'm more in favor of fixing move_to_device_pass so that the behavior matches .to and does not create a new parameter group. Probably we need to update [this code](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/4264fd34ecb2026a7dbbd2eca5c9544d7640b3f5/torch/export/passes/__init__.py#L41-L48) to look something like [this](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/4264fd34ecb2026a7dbbd2eca5c9544d7640b3f5/torch/nn/modules/module.py#L954-L1020)?

It would be ideal if move_to_device_pass and to(device) had consistent behavior 👍 . Alternatively, if using the to method is not recommended for graphs exported with torch.export, there should be clearer documentation or explicit warnings when an exported graph calls to(device).

@angelayi  normally the torch export does not need to be aware of device information right? We noticed that there is a `insert_aten_to_metadata_assert_pass` [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/d746b987d8239595438b9dbd0f96ec6a4fed4653/torch/_export/utils.py#L704) when we export this function. What if we do not assert device in that line, or we call context manager`_disable_aten_to_metadata_assertions` https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c261c71f3e8de35e1b0f6feaf9d8249c44a303e9/torch/_export/utils.py#L652 
During the export?

Thank you @angelayi for the detailed explanation and clarification.

I think the use cases for device casting are becoming clearer — especially the differences between applying it on an nn.Module versus an exported fx.Graph.

For nn.Module, the default .to() method moves registered buffers and tensors, but it isn’t meant to handle IR.
For fx.Graph, the recommended approach is to use move_to_device_pass.

In this case, perhaps we can work on improving move_to_device_pass so that its behavior is closer to .to(), providing a smoother user experience. For example, as you mentioned, ensuring this method doesn’t create a new parameter group for the exported module would be a good step.

It looks like the line you linked is followed by:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/33e6c5a93d4e249d3a05cbe434877b8a77922a90/torch/_inductor/triton_bundler.py#L395-L396

You are right, it was wrapped into try/catch [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/commit/19b76bd873f51401e061b33ad1f12b8dbe8e9ca6), before it was a crash. Even though [this function](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/33e6c5a93d4e249d3a05cbe434877b8a77922a90/torch/_inductor/runtime/cache_dir_utils.py#L31) is inconsistent IMO: if you set TRITON_CACHE_DIR, you have 1 folder for everything. If not, you add `str(device)` to the dir name, i.e. creating a folder per device. I wonder if try/catch is just hiding the problem when different ranks overwrite each other's directories.
The rename should be atomic/thread safe.  So overwrite will happen, but readers will either get one or the other (and both will be the same).

Here's another 0-related example that's slightly more reasonable:

```
import torch
import sys
torch._dynamo.config.capture_scalar_outputs = True
torch._dynamo.config.capture_dynamic_output_shape_ops = True
torch._inductor.config.emulate_precision_casts = True

def foo(arg0):
    t0 = arg0 # size=(1, 399, 3, 464, 195), stride=(108304560, 271440, 90480, 195, 1), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    t1 = torch.cat([t0, t0, t0], dim=0) # size=(3, 399, 3, 464, 195), stride=(108304560, 271440, 90480, 195, 1), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    t2 = t1.var(dim=0) # size=(399, 3, 464, 195), stride=(271440, 90480, 195, 1), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    t3 = t2.std(dim=1) # size=(399, 464, 195), stride=(90480, 195, 1), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    output = t3  # output tensor
    return output

arg0 = torch.rand([1, 399, 3, 464, 195], dtype=torch.float32, device='cuda', requires_grad=True) # size=(1, 399, 3, 464, 195), stride=(108304560, 271440, 90480, 195, 1), dtype=float32, device=cuda
if __name__ == '__main__':
    out_eager = foo(arg0)
    out_eager.sum().backward()
    print('Eager Success! ✅')
    compiled_foo = torch.compile(foo, fullgraph=True, dynamic=True)
    out_compiled = compiled_foo(arg0)
    out_compiled.sum().backward()
    print('Compile Success! ✅')
    # Compare outputs (forward)
    out_eager_sum = out_eager.sum()
    out_compiled_sum = out_compiled.sum()
    diff = (out_eager_sum - out_compiled_sum).abs().item()
    rel_diff = diff / (out_eager_sum.abs().item() + 1e-12) * 100
    print(f'Relative diff (sum): {rel_diff:.6f}%')
    if rel_diff > 5:
        print(f'❌ Forward output sums differ significantly (relative)!')
        print('out_eager_sum:', out_eager_sum.item())
        print('out_compiled_sum:', out_compiled_sum.item())
        print('Absolute diff:', diff)
        print('Relative diff (%):', rel_diff)
        sys.exit(1)
```

@jansel When Triton compilation fails, it now directly throws an InductorError and terminates the program. Can we modify the exception type to fall back to Eager mode instead, avoiding this issue?

That would be possible, but would have worse performance than eager mode because we have already decomposed the eager ops into many smaller operations that don't always line up with Triton kernel boundaries.  It would also complicate the output code.

IMO we should fix the Triton bug.

    t6 = t5.min(dim=0).values # size=(184,), stride=(1,), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    t7 = arg3 # size=(184, 2), stride=(1, 184), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    t8 = t7.transpose(0, 1) # size=(2, 184), stride=(184, 1), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    t9 = t8.norm(dim=0) # size=(184,), stride=(1,), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    t10 = arg4 # size=(32,), stride=(1,), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    t11 = torch.nn.functional.relu(t10) # size=(32,), stride=(1,), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    t12 = arg5 # size=(5, 152), stride=(152, 1), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    t13 = t12.var(dim=0) # size=(152,), stride=(1,), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    t14 = torch.cat([t11, t13], dim=0) # size=(184,), stride=(1,), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    t15 = torch.pow(torch.pow(torch.pow(torch.pow(t3, t6), t6), t9), t14) # size=(184,), stride=(1,), dtype=float32, device=cuda
    output = t15  # output tensor

163588.body: So the eager baseline is going be pretty different in implementation compared to what SDPA w/ masking will run especially in bfloat16, can you run w/ compiled

I can repro with the script and the divergence disappears with torch.compile

```
Mask 580/1000 (58%): Error 0.0% (OK)
Mask 590/1000 (59%): Error 0.0% (OK)
```

But Driss, doesn't this just mean we have to try harder to have a more accurate eager implementation?

I think there are a few things here;
Our eager implementation is very very different to flex-attention's compiled implementation. We are a vmapped, unfused impl. This means that we don't actually do iterative softmax which can have a significant impact on numerics, in fact this in theory is a benefit for the eager impl see internal note Ed: https://fburl.com/workplace/heavwoh5

Besides, and what I suspect is showcasing in this impl is that we don't actually skip work in the eager impl but instead we emulate it by masking all attention scores to 0 where they would be skipped by the kernel for a non None block-mask. 

So making a more accurate impl in eager seems like quite a large lift IMO. That being said there may be a `real` issue for eager but I that would require some more review of whats going on here.  

>Does NOT occur with float32: Same code with float32 has <0.001% error

leads me to believe that is not the case and this is quark of FP

TinyEnc + bool pad_mask → torch._dynamo.exc.Unsupported “torch.* op returned non‑Tensor” at _nested_tensor_from_mask_left_aligned.

It is expecting --> either trace this path or short‑circuit with a supported Tensor result; at least emit a clearer error.

Workarounds: set enable_nested_tensor=False on TransformerEncoder, or avoid bool pad_mask in this path; eager works.

The version of nested tensors utilized in the MHA fast path ("nested strided tensors" AKA "NSTs") are not supported in torch.compile and won't be.

The suggested workaround is to disable the use of the fast path when using torch.compile:
```python
torch.backends.mha.set_fastpath_enabled(False)
```

That said, I think we should have a clearer error message describing this scenario along with the workaround.

163641.body:
The code makes torch.compile fail because it branches on sizes while the inputs are marked “unbacked”, and fullgraph=True forbids graph breaks.

Problem is  mark_unbacked + size-based if-condition → compiler can’t decide the branch, so it errors.
With fullgraph=True and mark_unbacked, branching on a.size(0)//b.size(0) triggers a compile/graph-break error; expect guard or clear error.

Also remove mark_unbacked, drop fullgraph=True, or avoid control flow on unbacked sizes (e.g., use tensor ops or cond)

I have a fix https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/164676, it keeps `torch._check` in the FX graph - when a predicate is not a Python constant. If it is, _check is executed eagerly, if true then nothing happens, no node is created, if false then exception is raised (from torch._check). So this is a compile time check without adding an FX graph node. If predicate is not a Python constant, the fix creates a proxy and runs checks at a runtime - it becomes a runtime check.

The general issue here is that inductor cudagraphs does not support persisting tensors from iteration to iteration, based on invariants we need related to reclaiming memory. So not all models will succeed out of the box with cudagraphs. 

With graph partitioning, models that might have been skipped previously will now be compiled, where they might error. This is what occurred with vision maskrcnn. There are longer term efforts to avoid this error but it's not there yet.

Short term options:
- user can revert to not using cudagraph partitioning. any graph that has any number of partitions would have been skipped so we can detect this in error msg.
- we can add an api to mark a tensor for exclusion from cudagraph partitioning. we could potentially detect this in dynamo, when a tensor is assigned as an attribute.

163674.body: cc @eellison I feel like I've seen this before.

This is actually common with the usage of 
`@torch.compile` inside a training loop with something like `x.item()` usage  : 
Something like this ...
	
```
loss = nn.L1Loss()

@torch.compile(mode='max-autotune')
def get_loss_by_inference(self, data, partial_latent_decode_mask):
	with torch.autocast(device_type=self.device):
		encoded, h, w = self.encoder_model(data)
                # Masking away some parts of the encoded latent using a tensor of 1's in some channels and 0's everywhere else
		partial_latent_decode = encoded * partial_latent_decode_mask
		partial_decoded = self.decoder_model(partial_latent_decode, h, w)
               # Calculate the ratio of masked channels using mean and scale the image data accordingly
		ratio = (torch.mean(partial_latent_decode_mask)).item()
                scf = ratio ** 0.5
		sc_data = interpolate(data, scale_factor=scf, mode='bilinear', align_corners=False, antialias=True)
		sc_partial_result = interpolate(partial_result, scale_factor=scf, mode='bilinear', align_corners=False, antialias=True)
	        partial_loss =loss(sc_partial_result, sc_data))
```

In the code above the line `ratio = (torch.mean(partial_latent_decode_mask)).item()` will cause a graph break.
Now if one sets `TORCHDYNAMO_CAPTURE_SCALAR_OUTPUTS=1` then this error occurs.

Issue is fixed by this PR: (https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/166890). The problem is `torch.compile` calls .item() on a float tensor arg, the generated kernel references an unbacked float symbol (for example, zuf0). However, this symbol is not included in the kernel's parameter list, which leads to a compilation error.

163791.body:
> File "/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/transformers/modeling_rope_utils.py", line 50, in longrope_frequency_update
if seq_len > original_max_position_embeddings:

Seems like the Phi model uses dynamic RoPE, so it tries to do `if seq_len > original_max_position_embeddings` to determine whether to use the long or short frequency — depending on if the sequence is longer than the original pretraining length. But `seq_len` is a dynamic value only known at runtime. Since `torch.export` can't know the actual sequence length ahead of time, this comparison fails during export. `torch.export` can't decide which branch to specialize.

I think the workaround is just to avoid this kind of data-dependent control flow. e.g. manually comment out the `@dynamic_rope_update` decorator in Phi's RoPE module forward function:

> class Phi3RotaryEmbedding(nn.Module):
    inv_freq: torch.Tensor  # fix linting for `register_buffer`
	...
>  @torch.no_grad()
    @dynamic_rope_update  # power user: used with advanced RoPE types (e.g. dynamic rope)
    def forward(self, x, position_ids):
		...

However, it will, obviously, hurts the model's output quality when the seq length is larger than Phi's pretrained length (i.e. 128K).

- The tracer/AOT-autograd tries to capture the forward graph nodes and also the saved tensors used in backward. wait @pythongiant are you telling me dynamo is tracing into the triton kernel?
and failing to understand what how i am handling the output tensor?
and custom_op is a workaround (prevents dynamo from looking into the triton kernel) thats why it works?

> wait [@pythongiant](https://github.com/pythongiant) are you telling me dynamo is tracing into the triton kernel? and failing to understand what how i am handling the output tensor? and custom_op is a workaround (prevents dynamo from looking into the triton kernel) thats why it works?

Exactly.
Dynamo does trace the Python wrapper logic around it (the `torch.autograd.Function.forward` where you allocate `output = torch.empty_like(x)` and then call your Triton kernel to fill it). To Dynamo, the tl.store inside the Triton kernel is invisible. All it sees is: you allocated an empty tensor, you handed it into some *opaque* call.

*and then* you return that same tensor.

AOTAutograd wants saved tensors to be “valid graph values” (things it can replay or rematerialize).

But here output is from a pre-allocated buffer, mutated in-place by a kernel Dynamo doesn’t know about. 

So the FX graph treats output as an “invalid node” (basically “I don’t know how this tensor was produced”). When that “invalid node” gets marked as both “output of the forward graph” and “something backward needs,” the partitioner asserts → your error.

When you wrap the Triton kernel in a `torch.library.custom_op`:

- Dynamo sees a single opaque “op node” with inputs and outputs.

- It never has to reason about how the outputs were produced, they’re just results of a legal op.

- The saved tensor is now considered a valid graph output → partitioner is happy. This is why the swiglu code you linked works fine: its forward path ultimately returns tensors from known aten ops (or is wrapped as a proper op), so Dynamo never sees “invalid” outputs.

Wrapping in a custom_op (or hiding the save) is a workaround that prevents Dynamo from needing to reason about your kernel’s internals.

hmm interesting.

By putting the kernel behind torch.library.custom_op we are essentially treating this whole thing as a single, valid graph node.

That bypasses Dynamo’s attempt to trace inside, so no InvalidNode ever appears.
That’s why it works for both RMSNorm and Softmax.

Correct behavior would be: Dynamo should either treat the Softmax kernel as a black-box node (like a custom_op), or at least gracefully graph-break but it half-traces, produces InvalidNode, then crashes at partitioner time.

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch._inductor import config

# Inductor configuration
config.fallback_random = True

class Model(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):

        # Changed input channels to match input tensor shape
        self.conv = nn.Conv1d(16, 4, kernel_size=3, padding=1)

    def forward(self, x):
        # Remove permute since input shape is already correct
        x = self.conv(x)
        return x

# Update input tensor shape to match expected dimensions
# [batch_size, channels, sequence_length]
x = torch.randn(1, 16, 1)  # Shape: [1, 16, 1]

model = Model()
inputs = [x]

# Force device to MPS if available, otherwise CPU
device = "cuda" if torch.backends.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"

# Force backend to inductor even on MPS
backend = "inductor"

def run_test(model, inputs, device, backend):
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    model.to(device)
    inputs = [inp.to(device) for inp in inputs]

    try:
        model = torch.compile(model, backend=backend)
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Inductor compilation failed: {e}. Falling back to eager.")
        backend = "eager"

    try:
        output = model(*inputs)
        print(f"Succeeded on {backend} ({device})")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Runtime error on {backend} ({device}): {e}")

run_test(model, inputs, device, backend)
```

Well, I report this because I think it is a bug in Inductor. Because eager can correctly process `permute-conv1d` and outputs tensors. However, Inductor can't do this in such a case.

As a DL compiler, inductor should behave the same as eager (at least not throw this error).

163876.body:
Here is simpler reproduction code for this:
```
import torch
from torch.utils._sympy.functions import Mod  # <-- Torch's custom Mod
from sympy import symbols

s31, s64 = symbols('s31 s64')

expr = 4*s31 + 4*s64 - 4*Mod(s31 + s64, 8) + 32

while True:
    expr.is_constant(s31, s64)
```

The issue seems to be that sympy Expr.is_constant employs random numbers to determine whether the expression is constant, but the torch custom `Mod` function cannot handle that. This is why it randomly fails, not always.

any known workaround would be greatly appreciated, until this can be fixed in a torch release. Currently it causes *random* compile failures.

Auto-grad experienced folks, three questions if anyone know the answer in top of his head

How does output strides get computed when forward is compiled? Do we actually evaluate and run eager with the example inputs? It's getting computed accurately even when it's data-dependent. fwd_output_strides, compiled_fw_func = _aot_stage2b_fw_compile, refering to fwd_output_strides.

When backward is compiled, we do check and compare real strides computed in (1) with ones that are output of compiled (fake tensor ones), and if they deviate, we call as_strided on the fake tensor input of the backward.

164264.body: Hi @galv, fsdp2 maintainer here. thanks for documenting the gaps for fsdp2 + cuda graphs! It your workload cpu bound because of local batch size 1? Is this for finetuning?

Hi @weifengpy, this is a customer workload that I cannot disclose in detail, but it's a very large transformer model.

In general, workloads are becoming more and more host bounded. For example, many workloads on GB200 (aka, Grace CPU with B200 GPU) are host bounded because of the Grace CPU's poor single-threaded performance (compared to x86_64 CPU's at a similar price point). Part of this is due to the fact that Blackwell is faster than Hopper, but the CPU did not improve between the two generations (both Grace Hopper and Grace Blackwell chips use the same Grace CPU).

I will have to see if I can find a simple example that demonstrates CPU overhead on a GB200 setup, but I promise nothing right now.

To clarify, (1) and (3) are easy fixes, and (1) is not something you would even have to review. (3) is also an easy fix (I already made a fix in a local branch, though it may be pessimizing.) (2) is a pain for various reasons, so I will have to see if that is something that can be integrated in a non-hacky way.

> many workloads on GB200 (aka, Grace CPU with B200 GPU) are host bounded because of the Grace CPU's poor single-threaded performance
