164484.body:
Form AI
I see the issue. Look at this section of the generated code:

auto tmp17 = tmp14 + tmp16;
auto tmp18 = at::vec::convert<at::BFloat16>(tmp17);  // <-- DEAD CODE, never used!
auto tmp20 = tmp17 + tmp19;  // <-- Uses float32 tmp17, not bf16 tmp18

The bug is that tmp18 (the bf16 conversion) is computed but never used. The subsequent tmp20 uses tmp17 (float32) directly instead of converting through bf16.

In the original program:

var_node_4 = torch.nn.functional.layer_norm(...) # outputs bfloat16
var_node_3 = torch.add(var_node_4, var_node_13)  # bf16 + float32 -> promotes bf16 to f32

Eager behavior: layer_norm output → bf16 → promote to float32 → add Compiled behavior: layer_norm computed in float32 → add directly (skips bf16 roundtrip)

The compiled version skips the precision loss from the bf16 intermediate, making it numerically different. This is a fusion bug where the dtype conversion is being incorrectly eliminated as dead code.

Would you like me to help track down where in the inductor fusion/lowering logic this bf16 conversion is being dropped?
not a bug expected as discussed in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/173461

164546.body:
Can I understand it this way: when the tensor is continuous, `torch.randn_like decomposition` adopts an additional optimization (such as permute the result), while `torch.rand_like` does not?
such optimization causes this inconsistency?

The registered decomposition for torch.randn_like creates a tensor with values generated from a normal distribution, then permutes the generated tensor if needed. When compiling with a non-decomposing backend, the tensor is permuted first, and then values are generated in the permuted tensor via a normal distribution. The difference in outputs occurs because the normal distribution kernel has different logic for contiguous and non-contiguous tensors. The decomposed randn_like is handled differently than the non-decomposed randn_like because it is contiguous, as the permutation occurs after the normal kernel generation. I was using rand_like as an example to demonstrate that it works consistently regardless of the backend, since it generates values using the uniform distribution kernel, which handles contiguous and non-contiguous tensors the same way.

I have a PR out right now (#177994) that should fix this. I ran the above code on that branch it printed out

This seems to be a `__torch_function__` x Dynamo related issue: when we attempt to trace https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/b63bbe16615cc7680836dbb151bd848bce4893d6/torch/nn/attention/flex_attention.py#L246 (via https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/b63bbe16615cc7680836dbb151bd848bce4893d6/torch/nn/attention/flex_attention.py#L1072), we end up doing `__torch_function__` tracing, which causes us to attempt to trace `torch.Tensor.eq(tensor, inspect._empty)`, which results in an error.

164814.body:
I minimized this further:
```
import torch
def repro(sentinel):
    x = torch.unique(torch.ones(1))
    x = torch.reshape(x, [1])
    x = torch.squeeze(x)  # 0-d tensor
    return x * sentinel

sentinel = torch.tensor(1.0, requires_grad=True)
# Eager works
print('Eager:', repro(sentinel))
# Compiled fails with stride dimension mismatch
compiled = torch.compile(repro, fullgraph=True)
print('Compiled:', compiled(sentinel))
```
Running with torch.logs, the error seems to be a subtle interaction here:
* unique creates a dynamic/symbolic shaped tensor with stride = (1,)
* reshape preserves the stride metadata
* squeeze changes size here to `()` but note stride is preserved as (1,)
* aot compilation tries to restore, but has mismatch in arg.size() `()` vs tracked_stride `(1,)

So, we can fix this propogation in squeeze's meta/fake implementation or in the stride restoration in aot; I'll go ahead and guard this in the upstream (aot_autograd playback)

164823.body:
Thanks for filing this bug! I have simplified this repro even more - we don't even need the `.to_dense()` here - any operation that produces output on a sparse tensor will fail in inductor.  The reason why is inductor has strong assumptions on the format of tensors which fail when calling `reinterpret_tensor`
```
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class TestModel(nn.Module):
    def forward(self, x):
        x_sparse = x.to_sparse()
        return x_sparse * 2

x = torch.randn(10, 10)


model = TestModel()
# aot_eager_comp_res = torch.compile(model, backend="aot_eager")(x)
compiled_res = torch.compile(model)(x)
eager_res = model(x)

assert eager_res.allclose(compiled_res), f"eager_res should be close to compiled_res"
```

Looking in the codebase, it seems in many places we already try to graphbreak when seeing operations on sparse tensors; this code actually graph breaks on the `to_dense` call.  Unfortunately, that is after the `*2`, which inductor attempts to codegen and fails on

I have a working PR that attempts to fix by graph breaking sooner (any trace outputting sparse tensors). We will see if that is too large of a blast raidus or not :)

164891.body: cc @bdhirsh @anijain2305 @SherlockNoMad @IvanKobzarev seems to be an interesting intersection of distributed and tracing - marking high pri given our investments in distributed training
The most obvious explanation is that the individual ranks are compiling differently

The problem in the repro script appears to go away when I swap out shape of the start token in the tiny model from (1, 1, dim) to (dim) like so:

```
class TinyStartTokenModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.in_proj = nn.Linear(DIM, DIM)
        self.mid = nn.Linear(DIM, DIM)
        self.out_proj = nn.Linear(DIM, DIM)
        self.start_token = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(DIM))

    def forward(self, x):
        x = F.gelu(self.in_proj(x))
        x = F.gelu(self.mid(x))
        x = x + self.start_token.view(1, 1, -1)
        x = F.gelu(x)
        return self.out_proj(x)
```

I tried the same fix on my real model and it didn't work, so there may be other shapes/ops that cause this as well. What's special about 1, 1, DIM vs DIM that would cause this?

~~Additionally, I got the compiled graph from the ranks and they appeared to be identical apart from different devices.~~ nvm, see below

I think I figured it out (I think @ezyang was right): Two ranks call torch.compile() on the same graph, but one rank sees fewer all_gather ops because a parameter with shape (1,1,whatever) is sharded along dim0. The shard length on rank1 is zero, so inductor drops the all_gather for that rank. The bucketing pass then merges a different number of collectives per rank, so NCCL waits forever. Replicating any tensors whose shard dimension is smaller than or not divisible by the mesh slice keeps both ranks with identical collectives and the hang disappears. Bucketing probably needs to guard against zero-length shards rather than silently removing the all_gather (or maybe shard along a better dim?).
cc @zaptrem, the bucketing passes are a little bit in flux, and assume spmd graphs. eventually we will have a better user api for this workflow but it doesnt exist yet. for now use at your own risk.

> cc [@zaptrem](https://github.com/zaptrem), the bucketing passes are a little bit in flux, and assume spmd graphs. eventually we will have a better user api for this workflow but it doesnt exist yet. for now use at your own risk.

Thanks for getting back, I'm just experimenting to see if there's significant performance left on the table with this new stuff compared to our current manual FSDP2 wrapping+compile setup. Even with the above workaround and the overlap preserving bucketer performance seems to significantly trail FSDP2+compile. Can you explain what an SPMD graph is in this context? Docs seem to indicate it's got something to do with XLA/is for TPUs.

> Even with the above workaround and the overlap preserving bucketer performance seems to significantly trail FSDP2+compile.

When Elias says "in flux", this is part of it; we haven't gotten parity on llama 8b yet 🤣 . We're pretty close though.
@zaptrem I owe you a longer explanation but I need to take time to write it up

> [@zaptrem](https://github.com/zaptrem) I owe you a longer explanation but I need to take time to write it up

Thanks! Looking forward to it.

> > Even with the above workaround and the overlap preserving bucketer performance seems to significantly trail FSDP2+compile.
> 
> When Elias says "in flux", this is part of it; we haven't gotten parity on llama 8b yet 🤣 . We're pretty close though.

Ah, I assumed the results would be similar to the SimpleFSDP paper which claimed to be ahead of FSDP2. I was excited to try running an FSDP llama 8b with full cudagraphs (I got this to work via a tweak to SimpleFSDP that would replicate all the params whose dims weren't divisble by the world size), but not with the overlap bucketer so it was still slightly slower than FSDP2). I assume not all the tricks from their Inductor version have been upstreamed yet? I'm also curious how Inductor would be able to handle FSDP2's multi-stream communication stuff.

OK, so the current recommended configs are:

```
    torch._inductor.config.test_configs.aten_fx_overlap_preserving_bucketing = True
    torch._inductor.config.test_configs.aten_fx_overlap_insert_overlap_deps = False
    torch._inductor.config.allow_buffer_reuse = False
```

this might change but this is the most updated overlap pass. It's not super battle tested but we have done some testing on it.

164922.body: cc @anijain2305  @williamwen42  @ryanguo seems this has come up a few times now (see https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/125171#issuecomment-2083994787 as well).
For context, a solution to support this particular use case could be adding a variable tracker like randInt to support tracing datetime since this is used often for timing scenarios

Even if we were to support `datetime` similar to `randInt`, we would probably reorder all the `datetime` calls to one particular place in the dynamo generated bytecode (i.e. we won't do them around the compiled call), so the timing measurements within a compiled region wouldn't be meaningful.

Either you should compile around `datetime` (either move `datetime` calls outside the compiled function or consider guarding `datetime` calls with `torch.compiler.is_compiling()`) or consider using the pytorch profiler instead: https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/torch.compiler_profiling_torch_compile.html

As far as I understand, we would treat `datetime.now()` in two different ways, depending whether it's used for computation or not. If it is, do what @williamwen42 suggests, if it doesn't, wrap with @ezyang's PoisonVariableTracker (as @Lucaskabela mentioned https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/125171#issuecomment-2083994787)?

I'd like to start working on it, once we more or less agree on how we want to build it

Btw.  What do you mean with using profiler in this context @williamwen42 - use it to measure which of the two approaches you proposed we should choose?

"it's used for computation or not." is impossible to tell in general.

I mean "using profiler" as in use the profiler instead of using `datetime` to profile.

Ok thx, I start working on it right away. In high level, I think I'll try to do what @williamwen42 wrote - to run `datetime.now()` in one place, so in the compiled code it won't be executed. 

I'm still researching how to implement it, but for now it might be something along the lines: recognize when `datetime.datetime.now()` is called - perhaps in a bytecode or earlier? If in bytecode, then perhaps using InstructionTranslatorBase `call` or `call_function` or similar in `symbolic_convert.py`?

Fwiw `now()` in bytecode is `CALL`:
```
V1013 14:29:49.735000 1781491 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1282] [0/0_1] [__bytecode]               8 LOAD_GLOBAL              0 (datetime)
V1013 14:29:49.735000 1781491 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1282] [0/0_1] [__bytecode]              18 LOAD_ATTR                2 (now)
...
V1013 14:29:49.735000 1781491 torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py:1282] [0/0_1] [__bytecode]   7          58 CALL                     0
```

Knowing where `now()` needs to be invoked, we can save it to be evaluated later, like we do for random variables:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/4874cce52fc4cbb0f823ab94954452a56ce6dc31/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py#L698C1-L701C43
and 
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/4874cce52fc4cbb0f823ab94954452a56ce6dc31/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py#L1516C9-L1534C68

Somewhere in `convert_frame` (?), compute the value of `datetime.datetime.now()` once per frame, so the value will be the same within the frame, but will be different between multiple runs of the code (conceptually it makes sense to me, but I'm unsure how to implement it yet, I'll start with mimicking how `random` is implemented)

And last step would be to make sure that guards are not generated anymore for `now()`

I also wonder if we could/should utilize `output_graph.pregraph_bytecode` to evaluate `now()` outside of the graph? That's a different approach and I don't see it much used in the current code, but maybe it's worth exploring?

There are many unknow
"we would probably reorder all the datetime calls" - see `torch._dynamo.config.reorderable_logging_functions` for an example of function reordering.

If I understand correctly, the novel thing about reordering for datetime is that we want to store and load the variable. For other currently reorderable functions we don't do that (all are loggers, warnings and print). My understanding now is that we want to put result of datetime.now() and variables that store it to `debug_locals` and store/load them before the rest of code is generated, so something like this, but here we put loggers at the end, so for datetime we'd like to do the opposite? @williamwen42 

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/torch/_dynamo/output_graph.py#L1923C1-L1934C53
Ok, makes sense - I think we can take an approach similar to how we deal with `random`, but we don't need to deal with restoring random state.

164972.body: cc @XuehaiPan  I remember you did some work earlier with `_cxx_pytree`. I am wondering if you are interested in this work. The desire is to have `optree` in the user code, but Dynamo tracing the `pytree` or implementing a VT to simulate the optree.

> cc [@XuehaiPan](https://github.com/XuehaiPan) I remember you did some work earlier with `_cxx_pytree`. I am wondering if you are interested in this work. The desire is to have `optree` in the user code, but Dynamo tracing the `pytree` or implementing a VT to simulate the optree.

Hi @anijain2305, there are already Dynamo polyfills for `torch.utils._cxx_pytree`: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/torch/_dynamo/polyfills/pytree.py

Both `torch.utils._cxx_pytree` and `torch.utils._pytree` can be traced smoothly.

It would be very easy to support basic raw `optree` functions (e.g., `tree_{flatten,unflatten,map,iter}`) based on current existing polyfills. That requires some extensions:

1. Handle `namespace` registry argument (in `_cxx_pytree` and its polyfills, it is hardcoded to `"torch"`).
2. Handle dict key ordering for non-`"torch"` namepaces (`namespace="torch"` will use insertion order for `dict` and `collections.defaultdict`).
3. Handle `none_is_leaf=False` case (`_cxx_pytree` hardcoded `none_is_leaf=True`).

------

> TorchDynamo traces through python bytecode but optree is implemented in C, and therefore Dynamo graph breaks here.
> 
> A workaround is to use pytree when compiler is tracing. This is an example PR for an OSS model - [lucidrains/lumiere-pytorch#7](https://github.com/lucidrains/lumiere-pytorch/pull/7)
> 
> However, there is a desire to you optree in the original eager code, and Dynamo to sneakily replace it will pytree while tracing.

I think the problem is that both `_pytree` and `_cxx_pytree` are not official public APIs. Maybe we should consider prioritizing:

- #65761
- #137400

to let the community use an official public `torch.utils.pytree` module more easily (like `jax.tree.*` and `jax.tree_util.*`).

cc @zou3519 @jansel @ezyang @albanD

@XuehaiPan So yes, I agree that using `torch.utuils.pytree` might be an easier solution. But folks that are comfortable with jax pytree in general will benefit from directly supporting this. 

If you are interested in working on this, it will be really helpful. Your three comments make me believe that you already are way ahead of anyone else :)

> If you are interested in working on this, it will be really helpful.

@anijain2305 I'll work on this maybe this weekend.

165032.body: @alaa-ali is there any issue with using `Dim.DYNAMIC`? You should be able to specify `Dim.DYNAMIC(min=1, max=1000)` as well.

@alaa-ali both `Dim(“name”, min=1, max=1000)` and `Dim.DYNAMIC(min=1, max=1000)` are fine syntactically, but the gotcha is they're different APIs; the former is just stricter about guards & constraint violations.

165034.body:
you probably want to utilize guard_or_true/guard_or_false to define proper explicit unbacked semantics
for is_tensor_shardable
or force users otherwise to give hints in the form of torch._check(..)
if this is not decidable anonymously. 
cc @bdhirsh 
```

def is_tensor_shardable(shape: Sequence[int], spec: DTensorSpec) -> bool:
    """Check if the shape is shardable according to the spec."""
    # number of shards in each tensor dimension
    shards_map = [1] * len(shape)
    for i, placement in enumerate(spec.placements):
        if placement.is_shard():
            shard_dim = cast(Shard, placement).dim
            if shard_dim >= len(shape):
                return False
            shards_map[shard_dim] *= spec.mesh.size(i)

    for i, dim_size in enumerate(shape):
        # TODO: maybe we should determine is_shardable based on
        #       whether it's evenly sharded or not
        if shards_map[i] > 1 and dim_size < shards_map[i]:
            return False

    return True

```

@laithsakka Do you happen to know if `guard_or_true/false` is significantly slower than a regular comparison? I believe there's active work on reducing the sharding propagation overhead, so I think that may be sensitive if so.

It shouldn't be significantly slower - there's a couple extra small checks that guard_or_* does (see `_guard_or()` in symbolic_shapes.py)- but then it's the same path as `SymBool.bool_()` (which is what is called if you just do a direct "if") calling sym_node.shape_env.evaluate_sym_node().

one question I have (that maybe @XilunWu or @tianyu-l can answer) is that: is there an obvious "fallback" path that the matmul sharding rule can take (or any other sharding rules that branch on shape can take) if we are doing sharding prop on a tensor and we don't know its shape?

Having fallbacks like this in general seems like a useful property, since today the branching means that compile needs to specialize DTensor's sharding decisions on the input tensor sizes.

> is there an obvious "fallback" path that the matmul sharding rule can take (or any other sharding rules that branch on shape can take) if we are doing sharding prop on a tensor and we don't know its shape?

Would appreciate info here as well. For `is_tensor_shardable` (used by matmul) specifically, it seems like the default would be to say the tensor isn't shardable, so using `guard_or_true` in this case.



> branching means that compile needs to specialize DTensor's sharding decisions on the input tensor sizes

I think specializing can be beneficial for fixed shapes, since I'm assuming the specific branches in sharding prop are optimized for the shapes/mesh. Is that true, and if so is there a way to provide a default while allowing specialization for scenarios where it's beneficial?

So the context here is that when we do sharding propagation we need to do two things:

1. We need to figure out what the output placements would be if we ran this function is exactly as is, if possible. This is not hard to do without shapes.
2. Assuming that failed, we want to find a redistribute which will take our tensor to a new sharding that WILL work. Now this is where we have to be careful. If a tensor is too small on a dimension, it cannot be sharded on that dimension because you will run out of entries to distribute to other ranks (e.g., tensor is size 4 but the mesh dimension is 8). Our generation of potential input-output shardings from (1) does NOT consult shapes, so the sharding rules then filter out the ones that propose illegal shards. It's important to do this!

It is not feasible to remain in a state of superposition after shard prop; we need to know exactly what redistribute we're issuing. But on the other hand, it is relatively reasonable to assert that an unbacked dimension is big enough to be sharded on; this is, for example, likely to be true in balanced routing on MoEs. But in any case, the medicine here is to reason about the use case, and not just try to very locally solve problems with guard or false.

> We need to figure out what the output placements would be if we ran this function is exactly as is, if possible

If we assume is_tensor_shardable=False, we will get a subset of sharding strategies we can use [code link](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/eaeaa08e3a8071be46f833f7b46aa642ec14e0f7/torch/distributed/tensor/_ops/_matrix_ops.py#L88-L96).

However, I am curious if `is_tensor_shardable` is necessary in compiler mode.  Can we just always assume `is_tensor_shardable=True`? From the example above, even though we shard tensor size 4 on mesh dimension 8, we can have zero padding and that shouldn't cause any numerical correctness issue. The consequence is that some nodes are doing meaningless computation. From the strategy generation perspective, we may want to eliminate those inefficient strategies though.

I agree we could in principle do this, although we'll have to check DTensor can handle zero size dims correctly (I can't think of any reason it wouldn't). I do worry that we might accidentally start picking these strategies!

I am suspecting it is linked to this other failing: `V1009 11:25:45.231000 3024 site-packages/torch/_dynamo/guards.py:3006] [0/1] [__recompiles]     - 0/0: expected type of 'args[1]' to be a tensor type, ' but found <class 'torch.nn.parameter.Parameter'>` mentioned at https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/135011.

            if isinstance(X, torch.nn.Parameter):
                X = X.data
```

In my case, I compose the usage of `torch.ops` and `torch.compile`. I think something is wrong in this case.

165096.body:
Turn your model to eval(). The following code works for me (nightly torch)

```python
from torchaudio.models import wav2vec2_xlsr_300m
import torch

torch_model = wav2vec2_xlsr_300m().eval()
example_inputs = (torch.rand(1, 64600),)
onnx_program = torch.onnx.export(torch_model, example_inputs, dynamo=True)
```
The model has a None output. You may want to wrap it with an nn.Module to drop the output before exporting. (or use pytorch 2.9)

165177.body:
FSDP2 currently does not support torch.compile unfortunately.

Edit: to clarify, you can still apply FSDP2 on a compiled nn.Module, e.g.
```
cmod = torch.compile(mod)
fmod = fully_shard(cmod)
```

What is not supported is:
```
fmod = fully_shard(mod)
cmod = torch.compile(fmod)
```

You can perhaps try SimpleFSDP if you want to compile it:
https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.00284
cc @ruisizhang123

@kwen2501 Thanks for your feedback. Unfortunately, applying `torch.compile()` before distributing also doesn't work.

```diff
+ gen_model = torch.compile(gen_model)  # type: ignore

for submodule in gen_model.model.layers:
    if isinstance(submodule, LlamaDecoderLayer):
        fully_shard(submodule)
fully_shard(gen_model)

- gen_model = torch.compile(gen_model)  # type: ignore
```

AssertionError: s52 (could be from ["L['position_ids']._base.size()[0]"]) not in {s53: ["L['attention_mask'].size()[1]", "L['attention_mask'].stride()[0]"], s58: ["L['cache_position'].size()[0]", "L['position_ids']._base.size()[0]"], s55: ["L['input_embeds'].size()[1]"], s9: ["L['position_ids'].size()[1]", "L['position_ids'].stride()[0]"]}.  If this assert is failing, it could be due to the issue described in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/90665
```

PS: I got the original information about applying `torch.compile` after from torchtriton (https://github.com/pytorch/torchtitan/issues/472#issuecomment-2242066578) and couldn't find better information in PyTorch's documentation. Apologies if I missed something in the documentation.

165280.body:
The kernel is because of zeros, not because of view. If you replace with `empty` there will be no kernel.

There are generic optimizations we could do for zeros that we haven't yet:

- if the zeros is internal to the graph, and used functionally in an extern kernel which doesn't have a striding constraint, we could allocate an internal 1-numel zero tensor at compile time and view it appropriately to pass to the kernel. 

- If there is a striding requirement, we could potentially have a larger zeros tensor, and view it appropriately for each use. This has a memory penalty because of persistent allocation.

But if it's escapes graph, or is mutated, there's no way to avoid the zero kernel.

165341.body:
Strawman:
- have an inductor config that specifies which custom ops should be partitioned against
- the config takes in the operators names as string names to avoid the serialization issue
- the config ignores all custom operators marked with cudagraph unsafe. It takes priority (unless it is set to None).

thoughts? @BoyuanFeng @eellison

> Inductor doesn't guarantee a way to get FakeTensors for an IR node, but we rely on needing that.

How would the proposed design fix this issue?

> the config ignores all custom operators marked with cudagraph unsafe

the original idea was to store the name str of ops to partition. For some reason, we thought the custom op tag is better in #148927. Should we suggest the name str to all future users to replace the tag? If so, should we just deprecate the tag since not many people use it yet?

> Inductor doesn't guarantee a way to get FakeTensors for an IR node, but we rely on needing that. How would the proposed design fix this issue?

The current design is that the user gives us a `custom_graph_partition_rule` function. This function accepts the FakeTensor inputs to the custom operator and returns True/False on if we should partition the function or not.

The problem is that we are unable to robustly figure out how to get FakeTensors for an IR Node. We could try really hard with that, or we can do the next thing.

The "have an inductor config that specifies which custom ops should be partitioned against" proposal fixes this via just partitioning on all of the operators listed in the config. It will not try to grab any FakeTensors to be sent into a `custom_graph_partition_rule` function because there is no function anymore.

> the original idea was to store the name str of ops to partition. For some reason, we thought the custom op tag is better in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/148927. Should we suggest the name str to all future users to replace the tag? If so, should we just deprecate the tag since not many people use it yet?

I think there's two ways to use Inductor graph partition. The first way is if you care about using Inductor's "safe cuda graphs", then you need a way to specify if your operator is CUDAGraph safe. There needs to be a way to specify this. This can either be via the cudagraph safe tag, or the `custom_graph_partition_rule`. I think we should just have one of them to avoid confusion. Specifying this at the custom operator library itself means that users of the custom operator library don't need to think too hard.

The second way to use Inductor graph partition is that a power user (vllm in this case) wants to completely override the partitioning decisions to be whatever graph partition vLLM decides. So, the pitch is we have a second mechanism that is just "an inductor config that specifies which custom ops should be parti

> The problem is that we are unable to robustly figure out how to get FakeTensors for an IR Node. We could try really hard with that, or we can do the next thing.

I don't think this is a hard to do, we should just fix this. I think I linked you code that does this in your original pr.

165395.body: I believe the issue is because the pytree unflatten implementation of dynamic cache is not correct ([code](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/9aab965b1e61d92d402809bd467c317ec464e560/src/transformers/integrations/executorch.py#L1110)). When calling run_decomp, we unflatten the cache which [triggers this code path](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/9aab965b1e61d92d402809bd467c317ec464e560/src/transformers/cache_utils.py#L94-L95), causing new tensors to be created. cc @tugsbayasgalan
https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/pull/41754 seems to fix the issue, but I'm not sure if my changes are entirely correct.

165398.body: Leaving a note here that this seems to be an issue known on the vllm side: https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/29992 The inclusion of a string argument in the vllm custom op is resulting in unnecessary unique compilation for each layer name (example shown in images). The `ComboKernel` message is coming from each compile. A naive adjustment of the large pointwise threshold does allow for the combination to occur and avoids a print but doesn't improve tokens/sec. I think the logging is helping identify an opportunity (limit unnecessary recompilations).
@trichmo how is that issue related to ComboKernel ?

@zou3519  This may be an incorrect read on the situation, but I viewed the excessive ComboKernel prints as a symptom of the graph being recompiled. The recompilations are only due to the layer_name in the [vllm.moe_forward](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/main/vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/layer.py#L2110C1-L2118C59) definition. 

Following a similar reasoning mentioned in the vllm issue - avoiding the recompilations will reduce the ComboKernel spam to a single notification to the user. Obviously this note doesn't address potential optimization gains. If I understood the vllm issue correctly, then a change there would resolve recompilations due to layer_name across all moe models.

165407.body: marking high pri to investigate the memory leak. I ran the repro and confirmed that "number of live tensors" does increase each training step, and goes away after setting `torch._inductor.config.triton.cudagraph_skip_dynamic_graphs`
cc @CoffeeVampir3 number of tensors increase with new cudagraph re-capturing for dynamic shape, but they dont hold onto memory. You can fallback for dynamic shapes as with @bdhirsh command.

As @bdhirsh said, using torch._inductor.config.triton.cudagraph_skip_dynamic_graphs is actually correct. You will see slight performance (1-5% deg which is better than static approach if you absolutely cannot stop creating a new graph for-all dynamic shape outputs) impact and VRAM allocations very noisy depending on which dim/s is dynamic.... but it works non the less when doing max-compile with full-graphs.

Additionally, if you know your shapes and know which shapes/dims are dynamic you can set cudagraph_capture_sizes which would limit new graph creation causing "ghosts". This is quite a bit more advanced.

165409.body:
This is quite hard to fix - the code here creates a tensor with overlapping data and mutates it, which does not have defined behavior (the order in which we mutate the overlapped elements in eager is undefined), so we are unable to easily replicate the behavior under compile.

You can always work around this issue by removing the inplace op, or cloning your tensor first.

@ezyang 
Thanks for the quick reply!
I understand that the behavior involves an overlapped view and an in-place mutation, which can be undefined in general.
However, the reason I reported it is that this pattern can still arise naturally in user code — for example, when using unfold for sliding-window operations followed by light in-place transforms for efficiency.

It would be great if the compiler could detect such cases more gracefully (e.g., by raising a clearer error or automatically cloning when necessary), rather than crashing during functionalization/AOTAutograd.

The reproducer is manually minimized from a real case I encountered, not automatically generated.
Hope this helps clarify the motivation!
The workaround I would propose you do is `abs_` on the original base tensor (prior to unfold) and then (cheaply) unfold it again.
@ezyang Thanks a lot — that’s really helpful!

165444.body:
This is also a problem for in-place mutation of tensors, but bizarrely it seems to depend on the kind of in-place mutation, the shape of the tensor, and the value of `always_keep_tensor_constants`.  For example:

```python
import torch

def test_with_shape(shape):
    class DummyModel(torch.nn.Module):
      def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.register_buffer("counter", torch.ones(shape))
      
      def forward(self):
        self.counter.add_(1.0)
        return self.counter
      
    ep = torch.export.export(DummyModel(), tuple())
    so_path = torch._inductor.aoti_compile_and_package(ep, inductor_configs={"always_keep_tensor_constants": True})
    loaded_module = torch._inductor.aoti_load_package(so_path)

    print(loaded_module())
    print(loaded_module())

test_with_shape((1,))
test_with_shape((2,))
```

produces:
```
tensor([2.])
tensor([2.])
tensor([2., 2.])
tensor([3., 3.])
```

So the in-place update works for shape (2,), but not for shape (1,).  It fails to update in both cases when `always_keep_tensor_constants=False`.  Some more variations below.


| mutating op | counter.shape | always_keep_tensor_constants | updates |
| ------------ | -------------- | -------------------------------- | -------- |
| self.counter.add_(1.0) | (1,) | TRUE | No |
| ,, | (2,) | TRUE | Yes |
| ,, | (1,) | FALSE | No |
| ,, | (2,) | FALSE | No |
| self.counter[:] = self.counter[:] + 1.0 | (1,) | TRUE | No |
| ,, | (2,) | TRUE | Yes |
| ,, | (1,) | FALSE | No |
| ,, | (2,) | FALSE | No |
| self.counter[0] = self.counter[0] + 1.0 | (1,) | TRUE | Yes |
| ,, | (2,) | TRUE | Yes |
| ,, | (1,) | FALSE | No |
| ,, | (2,) | FALSE | No |

To summarise, updating never works when `always_keep_tensor_constants=False`.  When it is true, it works for all the above in-place ops when `counter.shape=(2,)` but for shape `(1,)` it only works when updating a selected index.

According to the comments on [this issue](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/106217), I
The 1 vs 2 divergence is likely due to how we decide whether or not something can be done as a constant or not

Further pinpointing the issue, we need to do one of the following to run AOT precompile on the same model multiple times.

1. Create a **UNIQUE** inductor cache.
```python
inductor_cache_dir = tempfile.mkdtemp()
os.environ["TORCHINDUCTOR_CACHE_DIR"] = inductor_cache_dir
```

2. Manually delete the cache under `/tmp/torchinductor_{username}`
